```mermaid
    S2A[Machine Calibration]:::sub
    S2B[Wear and Tear]:::sub
    S2C[Maintenance Schedule]:::sub
    S2D[Machine Capability Cm]:::sub
    S3A[Supplier Consistency]:::sub
    S3B[Material Grade]:::sub
    S3C[Storage Conditions]:::sub
    S3D[Incoming AQL]:::sub
    S4A[Work Instruction]:::sub
    S4B[Process Sequence]:::sub
    S4C[Cycle Time]:::sub
    S4D[Process FMEA]:::sub
    S5A[Gage R and R]:::sub
    S5B[Instrument Precision]:::sub
    S5C[Calibration Interval]:::sub
    S5D[MSA Study]:::sub
    S6A[Temperature]:::sub
    S6B[Humidity]:::sub
    S6C[Dust and Cleanliness]:::sub
    S6D[Lighting and Noise]:::sub

    Q --- M1
    Q --- M2
    Q --- M3
    Q --- M4
    Q --- M5
    Q --- M6
    M1 --- S1A
    M1 --- S1B
    M1 --- S1C
    M1 --- S1D
    M2 --- S2A
    M2 --- S2B
    M2 --- S2C
    M2 --- S2D
    M3 --- S3A
    M3 --- S3B
    M3 --- S3C
    M3 --- S3D
    M4 --- S4A
    M4 --- S4B
    M4 --- S4C
    M4 --- S4D
    M5 --- S5A
    M5 --- S5B
    M5 --- S5C
    M5 --- S5D
    M6 --- S6A
    M6 --- S6B
    M6 --- S6C
    M6 --- S6D

    classDef main fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef cat fill:#c9d4e6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#555,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How the 6M Factors Flow into Process Output

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP[Raw Inputs]:::inp
    M3F[Material Factor]:::factor
    M2F[Machine Factor]:::factor
    M4F[Method Factor]:::factor
    M5F[Measurement Factor]:::factor
    M1F[Man Factor]:::factor
    M6F[Milieu Factor]:::factor
    PROC[Production Process X]:::process
    SENS[Sensor and Gauge Array]:::sensor
    FB[Feedback Loop SPC Chart]:::feedback
    OUT[Output Quality Y]:::output
    DEC[Decision Accept or Reject]:::dec

    INP --> M3F
    M3F --> PROC
    M2F --> PROC
    M4F --> PROC
    M1F --> PROC
    M6F --> PROC
    PROC --> OUT
    OUT --> SENS
    SENS --> M5F
    SENS --> FB
    FB --> DEC
    DEC -.Corrective Action.-> M1F
    DEC -.Corrective Action.-> M2F
    DEC -.Corrective Action.-> M4F

    classDef inp fill:#fde2c5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef factor fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef feedback fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#b5e7a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#e0c3fc,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each factor (M1–M6) acts as an *input stream* into the production process $X$. The Measurement factor (M5) operates both as an input *and* as a feedback sensor via SPC. Any drift detected by the feedback loop triggers a corrective action that re-tunes one or more upstream factors — this is the **closed-loop quality control** principle.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
List any **six factors** that affect product quality in a manufacturing industry. Briefly explain any **two** of them in 2–3 lines each.

**Model Answer (Keywords for 3 marks):**

The six classical factors are **Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, and Milieu (6M)**.

- **Man:** Quality depends on the skill, training, motivation, and fatigue level of the operator. An untrained worker introduces human errors that cause dimensional or assembly defects.
- **Machine:** Worn-out tools, miscalibrated spindles, or un-maintained equipment cause variation beyond tolerance. Capability indices ($C_m$, $C_{pk}$) quantify this factor.
- **Material:** Variation in hardness, purity, or dimensional stock from suppliers leads to inconsistent finished goods. Incoming inspection (AQL) controls this.
- **Method:** Poorly documented or non-standardized work instructions cause process drift and inconsistency between shifts.
- **Measurement:** An inaccurate gauge masks true defects; precision instruments are a prerequisite for reliable quality decisions.
- **Milieu:** Temperature, humidity, dust, and lighting directly affect precision manufacturing (e.g., electronics, pharma).

*(Expected length: 8–10 lines. Award ½ mark per factor listed, 1 mark each for the two explanations.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
What is **Deming's 85/15 Rule** in the context of quality? State its significance.

**Model Answer:**

Deming's 85/15 Rule states that approximately **85% of quality problems** in an organization are caused by the **system, process, and management** (i.e., factors largely outside the worker's direct control), while only **15%** are attributable to the individual worker.

**Significance:**
1. It shifts the blame culture away from workers toward systemic improvement.
2. It emphasizes management's responsibility to design robust processes, provide proper tools, training, and materials.
3. It forms the philosophical foundation of **Total Quality Management (TQM)** and Continuous Improvement (Kaizen).

*(Award 2 marks for statement, 1 mark for significance.)*

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[Internal Choice as per KTU ESE Pattern — Answer ANY ONE]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**  [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Ishikawa Fishbone Diagram** for analyzing the factors affecting quality. List all the categories (M-factors) and provide **two examples of sub-causes** under each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A grinding process has specification $LSL = 9.95$ mm and $USL = 10.05$ mm. A sample of 100 components yields $\bar{X} = 10.01$ mm and $s = 0.012$ mm. The machine-only standard deviation (from a short-run capability study) is $\sigma_m = 0.006$ mm.  
Compute (i) Machine Capability $C_m$, (ii) Process Capability $C_p$, and (iii) $C_{pk}$. Comment on the process state. *(7 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Diagram:** (Refer to the Mermaid Fishbone in Section 4.1.)

The Ishikawa Fishbone Diagram is a structured brainstorming tool developed by **Kaoru Ishikawa** in 1968 to identify the potential causes of a quality problem (the "effect"). It visually groups causes into major categories — the **6M framework**:

- **Man (Workforce):** Skill level, training, fatigue, motivation, supervision style.
- **Machine (Equipment):** Calibration, maintenance, wear, capability, breakdown frequency.
- **Material (Inputs):** Supplier variability, grade, storage, moisture content.
- **Method (Process):** SOP adherence, sequence, cycle time, FMEA risk.
- **Measurement (Inspection):** Gage R&R, calibration interval, instrument resolution.
- **Milieu (Environment):** Temperature, humidity, lighting, cleanliness, vibration.

Each sub-cause is depicted as a smaller "bone" branching off the main category bone, all converging toward the central spine that points to the quality effect (e.g., "High Defect Rate").

**Marking Key (7 marks):**
- Neat labeled diagram → 3 marks
- Listing all 6 M-factors → 2 marks
- Two examples under each → 2 marks

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
$LSL = 9.95$ mm, $USL = 10.05$ mm, $\bar{X} = 10.01$ mm, $s = 0.012$ mm, $\sigma_m = 0.006$ mm.

**Step 1 — Machine Capability $C_m$:**

$$
C_m = \frac{USL - LSL}{6 \sigma_m} = \frac{10.05 - 9.95}{6 \times 0.006} = \frac{0.10}{0.036}
$$

$$
C_m \approx 2.778
$$

**Interpretation:** Machine is highly capable ($C_m > 2.0$ → excellent).

**Step 2 — Process Capability $C_p$:**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6 s} = \frac{0.10}{6 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.10}{0.072}
$$

$$
C_p \approx 1.389
$$

**Interpretation:** Process marginally meets the $C_p \geq 1.33$ threshold.

**Step 3 — Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$:**

$$
C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3s} = \frac{10.05 - 10.01}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.04}{0.036} = 1.111
$$

$$
C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3s} = \frac{10.01 - 9.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.06}{0.036} = 1.667
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.111, \; 1.667) = 1.111
$$

**Final Comment:**  
The process is **capable but off-center**. The mean ($10.01$ mm) is closer to the USL, making the upper specification the limiting constraint. **Recommended action:** Re-center the process by adjusting the grinding feed to shift the mean toward the midpoint $(10.00$ mm$)$.

**Marking Key (7 marks):**
- Correct formula application ($C_m$) → 2 marks
- Correct formula application ($C_p$) → 2 marks
- Correct $C_{pk}$ computation with both sides + min selection → 2 marks
- Correct interpretation / recommendation → 1 mark

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**  [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **variation theory** of Walter Shewhart and **Deming's 85/15 Rule**. How do these concepts relate to the factors affecting quality? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between **Common Cause Variation** and **Special Cause Variation** with suitable examples. Describe how each type of variation is detected and treated using a **Control Chart**. *(7 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Walter Shewhart's Variation Theory (1924):**  
Every production process, no matter how well-designed, exhibits inherent variability. Shewhart distinguished between two sources of variation:

1. **Chance / Common Cause Variation** — natural, expected fluctuations from the many small, independent factors (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Milieu) operating in their normal state.
2. **Assignable / Special Cause Variation** — non-random, identifiable disturbances arising from abnormal factor behavior (e.g., broken tool, untrained operator).

Shewhart's contribution was the concept that a process is **predictable** within common-cause limits, and **out-of-control signals** indicate assignable causes.

**Deming's 85/15 Rule:**  
Roughly 85% of quality failures are due to the **system** (management-controlled: process design, training infrastructure, equipment selection, material sourcing), while only 15% are due to **workers** within the system. Therefore, blaming workers without addressing systemic factors is futile.

**Linkage to Factors Affecting Quality:**  
The 6M factors collectively generate the variation. Common-cause variation is the *aggregate* output of these factors at their normal levels; special-cause variation arises when one factor deviates abnormally. Deming's rule highlights that management has the primary responsibility to control the **system-level** factors (Machine, Method, Measurement, Material), while the worker primarily influences only a small share.

**Marking Key (7 marks):**
- Shewhart variation theory → 3 marks
- Deming's 85/15 rule → 2 marks
- Linkage to 6M factors → 2 marks

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

| Aspect | Common Cause Variation | Special Cause Variation |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Inherent to the system (6M factors normal) | External assignable disturbance |
| **Frequency** | Continuous, persistent | Sporadic, intermittent |
| **Predictability** | Predictable within statistical limits | Unpredictable |
| **Examples** | Minor tool wear, ambient humidity | Broken cutting tool, power surge, untrained substitute operator |
| **Detection** | Process capability studies ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) | Control charts (points outside control limits, runs, trends) |
| **Treatment** | Management action (process redesign, training) | Operator action (remove the assignable cause, then resume) |
| **% Contribution** (Deming) | ~85% | ~15% |

**Control Chart Mechanism:**

A **Shewhart Control Chart** plots the quality characteristic over time with three reference lines: Center Line (CL = process mean), Upper Control Limit (UCL = $\mu + 3\sigma$), and Lower Control Limit (LCL = $\mu - 3\sigma$).

- **Common Cause Variation:** Points scatter randomly within UCL and LCL — process is "in statistical control." Action: management-led systemic improvement.
- **Special Cause Variation:** Points fall outside UCL/LCL, or exhibit non-random patterns (7 consecutive points on one side of CL, trend of 6 ascending points, etc.) — process is "out of control." Action: identify and remove the assignable cause immediately.

**Marking Key (7 marks):**
- Comparison table with 5+ rows → 3 marks
- Control chart explanation with UCL/LCL → 2 marks
- Treatment distinction (operator vs. management) → 2 marks

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing $C_m$ with $C_p$:** $C_m$ uses *machine-only* standard deviation; $C_p$ uses *process* standard deviation. Mixing these yields wrong answers.
> 2. **Forgetting the centering effect:** Reporting only $C_p$ without $C_{pk}$ loses at least 1 mark, since $C_{pk}$ reveals off-center processes.
> 3. **Mislabelled Ishikawa branches:** Students often write "labour" instead of "Man" or use only 4M instead of 6M — the KTU 2024 syllabus specifically mentions all six.
> 4. **Confusing Deming's 85/15 with Pareto's 80/20:** They are *different* principles. Deming = system vs. worker blame; Pareto = vital few vs. trivial many causes.
> 5. **Skipping the $\min()$ step in $C_{pk}$:** Always compute both sides and explicitly take the minimum.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Quality is the **totality of characteristics** satisfying stated and implied needs (ISO definition).
- ✅ The **6M factors** are: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Milieu.
- ✅ The **8M extension** adds Management and Information/Data.
- ✅ **Ishikawa Fishbone Diagram** is the standard tool to map 6M sub-causes to a quality effect.
- ✅ **Shewhart Variation Theory** divides variation into **Common Cause** (systemic) and **Special Cause** (assignable).
- ✅ **Deming's 85/15 Rule**: 85% of quality problems are management/system-controlled.
- ✅ **Machine Capability** $C_m = (USL - LSL)/(6\sigma_m)$ — uses machine-only $\sigma$.
- ✅ **Process Capability** $C_p = (USL - LSL)/(6\sigma)$ — ignores centering.
- ✅ **Process Capability Index** $C_{pk} = \min$ of upper and lower — penalizes off-center processes.
- ✅ **%R&R** from MSA quantifies measurement factor; benchmark is $< 10\%$.
- ✅ **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure costs.
- ✅ Industry benchmark: $C_p, C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for existing processes; $\geq 1.67$ for new processes (Six Sigma target: $C_{pk} = 2.0$).
- ✅ For every KTU question, **always show the formula, plug in values, compute, then interpret** — interpretation carries marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET611 - INDUSTRIAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Quality?

**Quality** is formally defined as the **totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs** (ISO 8402 / ISO 9000 definition). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, quality is not merely the absence of defects, but the **degree of excellence** and **conformance to requirements** that a product or service delivers to the customer.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Quality is the fitness-for-use of a product or service, measured by the customer's perception of value received relative to price paid. It is a multi-dimensional construct spanning performance, features, reliability, conformance, durability, serviceability, aesthetics, and perceived quality (Garvin's 8 dimensions).

### 1.2 Key Quality Concepts

| Term | Definition | Scope |
|------|------------|-------|
| **Quality Control (QC)** | The operational techniques and activities used to fulfill quality requirements. | Operational / Shop-floor level |
| **Quality Assurance (QA)** | All planned and systematic activities implemented within the quality system that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements. | System-wide / Management level |
| **Quality Management (QM)** | The coordinated activities to direct and control an organization with regard to quality. | Strategic level |
| **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | A management philosophy that seeks to integrate all organizational functions to focus on meeting customer needs and organizational objectives. | Enterprise-wide |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen 🍽️**
>
> Imagine a restaurant. The **chef** follows a recipe (a standard). Every dish that comes out is tasted (inspection = QC) before being served. The **manager** ensures the kitchen is clean, ingredients are fresh, and staff are trained (system-level activities = QA). The **owner** decides the menu based on what customers love and trains every employee to care about the customer's experience (TQM).
>
> **Quality Principles** are the universal rules — like "always taste before serving," "keep the kitchen clean," "listen to customer feedback" — that make this whole system work. Without them, even the best chef will eventually serve a bad meal.

### 1.4 The Evolution of Quality Thinking

Quality management has evolved through **four distinct eras** (often called the "Quality Eras"):

1. **Inspection Era (Pre-1900s – 1930s):** Focus on detecting defects after production.
2. **Quality Control Era (1930s – 1960s):** Introduction of statistical methods (Shewhart, Deming, Juran).
3. **Quality Assurance Era (1960s – 1980s):** System-wide documentation and prevention-based thinking.
4. **Strategic Quality Management Era (1980s – Present):** TQM, Six Sigma, Lean, and customer-centric strategies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Quality Gurus and Their Principles

The foundation of modern Quality Principles rests on the contributions of several pioneers. The KTU 2024 syllabus places strong emphasis on these contributors.

#### 2.1.1 W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993)

Deming is regarded as the father of modern quality management. His philosophy revolves around **system thinking** and **statistical process control**.

**Deming's 14 Points for Management (Key Highlights):**
1. Create constancy of purpose toward improvement.
2. Adopt the new philosophy.
3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.
4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone.
5. Improve constantly every process.
6. Institute training on the job.
7. Institute leadership.
8. Drive out fear.
9. Break down barriers between departments.
10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
11. Eliminate numerical quotas.
12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
13. Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.
14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.

**Deming's PDSA Cycle (Deming Wheel / Shewhart Cycle):**
$$\text{P} \rightarrow \text{D} \rightarrow \text{S} \rightarrow \text{A}$$
where P = Plan, D = Do, S = Study, A = Act.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Deming's 14 Points and the PDSA cycle appear regularly in 14-mark questions. Memorize both the points and the cycle direction (Plan → Do → Study → Act, NOT Plan → Do → Check → Act; that is the older variant).

#### 2.1.2 Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008)

Juran defined quality as **"fitness for use"** and structured quality management into three universal processes — the **Juran Trilogy**:

$$\text{Juran Trilogy} = \text{Quality Planning} + \text{Quality Control} + \text{Quality Improvement}$$

| Component | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| **Quality Planning** | Identify customers and their needs; develop product/process to meet those needs. |
| **Quality Control** | Evaluate actual performance vs. goals; act on differences. |
| **Quality Improvement** | Establish infrastructure to continuously improve. |

#### 2.1.3 Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001)

Crosby is famous for the slogan **"Quality is Free"** and his concept of **Zero Defects**. His **Four Absolutes of Quality** are:

1. **Quality = Conformance to requirements** (not elegance).
2. **Prevention, not inspection.**
3. **Zero defects performance standard.**
4. **Measure quality by the price of non-conformance (PONC).**

**Crosby's Quality Management Grid (5 Stages):**
- Stage 1: Uncertainty
- Stage 2: Awakening
- Stage 3: Enlightenment
- Stage 4: Wisdom
- Stage 5: Certainty

#### 2.1.4 Kaoru Ishikawa (1915–1989)

Ishikawa pioneered the **Quality Circle** concept and the **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** for root cause analysis. He emphasized **company-wide quality control (CWQC)** and stressed that quality begins and ends with **customer satisfaction**.

#### 2.1.5 Genichi Taguchi (1924–2012)

Taguchi introduced the **Loss Function**, redefining quality in monetary terms. Quality loss occurs whenever a product deviates from its target value, even if within specifications.

$$L(x) = k(x - T)^2$$

where $L(x)$ = quality loss, $k$ = quality loss coefficient, $x$ = actual value, $T$ = target value.

#### 2.1.6 Armand V. Feigenbaum (1920–2014)

Coined the term **Total Quality Control (TQC)** — the precursor to TQM. Stressed that quality is everyone's responsibility, not just the QC department.

### 2.2 Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Quality

David Garvin (1984) identified **eight critical dimensions** of product quality:

1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics.
2. **Features** — Secondary characteristics that add value.
3. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation.
4. **Conformance** — Degree to which design/operating characteristics meet standards.
5. **Durability** — Useful life including repairs.
6. **Serviceability** — Speed, courtesy, and competence of repair.
7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory appeal (look, feel, sound).
8. **Perceived Quality** — Image and reputation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Taguchi Loss Function | $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ | Currency (₹/$) | Monetary quality loss per unit |
| 2 | Loss Coefficient | $k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{\,2}}$ | Currency / unit² | Calculate $k$ from tolerance and replacement cost |
| 3 | Process Capability Index ($C_p$) | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | Measures inherent process capability |
| 4 | Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) | $C_{pk} = \min\left\{\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right\}$ | Dimensionless | Measures centered process capability |
| 5 | Defect Rate (ppm) | $\text{ppm} = \dfrac{\text{Number of defects}}{\text{Total units produced}} \times 10^6$ | parts per million | Six Sigma performance metric |
| 6 | Six Sigma Level | $3.4 \text{ defects per million opportunities (DPMO)}$ | DPMO | World-class benchmark |
| 7 | Cost of Quality (CoQ) | $\text{CoQ} = P + A + I + E$ | Currency | Prevention + Appraisal + Internal + External failure costs |
| 8 | First Pass Yield (FPY) | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passing first inspection}}{\text{Total units started}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Measures process efficiency |
| 9 | Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | Percentage | Multi-stage yield |
| 10 | Pareto 80/20 Rule | $80\% \text{ problems from } 20\% \text{ causes}$ | — | Prioritization tool |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on units and notation:** $\sigma$ = standard deviation, $\mu$ = process mean, USL/LSL = upper/lower specification limits, $A_0$ = average loss at functional failure, $\Delta_0$ = functional tolerance.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Quality principles form the backbone of **ISO 9001**, **AS9100**, **IATF 16949**, and **Six Sigma** certifications across industries. In manufacturing, the Taguchi loss function quantifies how a steel rod measuring 10.02 mm instead of 10.00 mm incurs a small but cumulative economic loss. In service industries (healthcare, banking, IT), cost-of-quality analysis helps managers decide whether to invest more in prevention rather than paying for failures. These principles are universally applied from automotive plants to software development.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Taguchi's Quality Loss Function

**Objective:** Show how the quadratic loss function $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ is derived from reasonable assumptions.

**Step 1 — Define the quality characteristic and target.**
Let $T$ = the target (nominal) value of a quality characteristic, and let $x$ = the actual measured value of that characteristic.

**Step 2 — Define the quality loss.**
Quality loss $L(x)$ is the economic loss to society (customer + manufacturer) caused by deviation from the target. By convention, $L(T) = 0$ when $x = T$ (the part is perfect).

**Step 3 — State the two fundamental assumptions.**

> **Assumption A (Symmetry):** Loss is symmetric around the target; i.e., a deviation of $+d$ incurs the same loss as a deviation of $-d$.
> Mathematically: $L(T + d) = L(T - d)$.

> **Assumption B (Monotonic Increase):** Loss is a non-decreasing function of $\vert x - T \vert$.

**Step 4 — Assume a Taylor series expansion around $x = T$.**

$$L(x) = L(T) + L'(T)(x - T) + \frac{L''(T)}{2!}(x - T)^2 + \frac{L'''(T)}{3!}(x - T)^3 + \cdots$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary conditions.**
Since $L(T) = 0$ (no loss at target), the first term vanishes. Since $L(x)$ has a minimum at $x = T$ (by Assumption B), we have $L'(T) = 0$. The third-order and higher terms are neglected (a common engineering approximation valid for small deviations).

**Step 6 — Simplify the expansion.**

$$L(x) \approx \frac{L''(T)}{2!}(x - T)^2$$

Letting $k = \dfrac{L''(T)}{2}$, we obtain:

$$\boxed{L(x) = k(x - T)^2}$$

**Step 7 — Determine the loss coefficient $k$.**
At the functional tolerance limit $\Delta_0$, the loss equals the replacement/repair cost $A_0$:

$$A_0 = k(\Delta_0)^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k = \frac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2}$$

**Step 8 — Final usable formula.**

$$\boxed{L(x) = \frac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2}\,(x - T)^2}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Taguchi Loss Function

> **Problem:** A shaft is specified at $T = 25.00$ mm. The functional tolerance is $\Delta_0 = 0.50$ mm, and the average cost to replace a shaft at this tolerance is $A_0 = \text{₹}400$. A batch produces shafts at $x = 25.15$ mm. Calculate the quality loss per shaft.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the loss coefficient $k$.**

$$k = \frac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2} = \frac{400}{(0.50)^2} = \frac{400}{0.25} = \text{₹}1600 \text{ per mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the deviation.**

$$x - T = 25.15 - 25.00 = 0.15 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the quality loss.**

$$L(25.15) = 1600 \times (0.15)^2 = 1600 \times 0.0225 = \text{₹}36 \text{ per shaft}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Even though the shaft may be within the usual specification limits ($\pm 0.50$ mm), Taguchi's principle shows that **every deviation from target costs money**. This is the philosophical heart of TQM — quality is not a binary "in/out of spec" decision.

### 3.3 Derivation of Process Capability Indices

**Step 1 — Define the process spread.**
For a stable process under statistical control, almost all measurements (≈ 99.73%) fall within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$. Hence the **natural process spread** is:

$$\text{Natural Process Spread} = 6\sigma$$

**Step 2 — Define the specification spread.**

$$\text{Specification Spread} = \text{USL} - \text{LSL}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{Specification Spread}}{\text{Natural Process Spread}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

**Step 4 — Centered vs. off-center processes.**
$C_p$ assumes the process mean equals the midpoint of the spec limits. If the process is shifted, we use $C_{pk}$:

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left\{\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right\}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (highly tested in KTU exams).**

| $C_p$ / $C_{pk}$ Value | Interpretation |
|-------------------------|----------------|
| $\geq 1.67$ | Excellent / Six Sigma capability |
| $1.33 - 1.67$ | Adequate (typical minimum) |
| $1.00 - 1.33$ | Marginal — process needs improvement |
| $< 1.00$ | Process is **incapable** — high defect rate |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Quality Gurus

| Aspect | Deming | Juran | Crosby | Ishikawa | Taguchi |
|--------|--------|-------|--------|----------|---------|
| **Key Phrase** | System thinking | Fitness for use | Quality is free | Customer focus | Loss function |
| **Focus** | Variation reduction | Planning + control + improvement | Conformance, prevention | Tools & people | Robust design |
| **Statistical Emphasis** | Very high | High | Low | Medium | High (DOE) |
| **Customer Emphasis** | Medium | High | Low | Very high | Medium |
| **Famous Tool** | PDSA / 14 Points | Juran Trilogy | Zero Defects | Fishbone / QC Circles | Loss function |
| **Era** | 1950s | 1950s | 1970s | 1960s | 1980s |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Map of Quality Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Quality Management] --> rootB[Quality Control QC]
    rootA --> rootC[Quality Assurance QA]
    rootA --> rootD[Total Quality Management TQM]

    rootB --> b1[Inspection]
    rootB --> b2[Statistical Process Control]
    rootB --> b3[Testing and Sampling]

    rootC --> c1[ISO 9001 Documentation]
    rootC --> c2[Process Audits]
    rootC --> c3[Preventive Action]

    rootD --> d1[Customer Focus]
    rootD --> d2[Continuous Improvement PDSA]
    rootD --> d3[Employee Involvement]
    rootD --> d4[Supplier Partnership]

    d2 --> d2a[Kaizen]
    d2 --> d2b[Six Sigma]
    d2 --> d2c[Lean Manufacturing]

    style rootA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style rootB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style rootC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style rootD fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Juran Trilogy — Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    jp1[Quality Planning] --> jp2[Identify Customers]
    jp2 --> jp3[Determine Needs]
    jp3 --> jp4[Develop Product Process]
    jp4 --> jp5[Establish Controls]

    jp5 --> jc1[Quality Control]
    jc1 --> jc2[Measure Performance]
    jc2 --> jc3{Compare to Goals}
    jc3 -->|Difference| jc4[Take Corrective Action]
    jc3 -->|No Difference| jc5[Continue Monitoring]

    jc4 --> ji1[Quality Improvement]
    jc5 --> ji1
    ji1 --> ji2[Establish Infrastructure]
    ji2 --> ji3[Identify Projects]
    ji3 --> ji4[Provide Training]
    ji4 --> ji5[Implement Improvements]

    ji5 -.Feedback Loop.-> jp1

    style jp1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style jc1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ji1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style jc3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Deming's PDSA Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pd1[Plan: Identify problem and plan change]
    pd2[Do: Carry out the test on small scale]
    pd3[Study: Analyze results and learn]
    pd4[Act: Standardize or revise plan]

    pd1 --> pd2
    pd2 --> pd3
    pd3 --> pd4
    pd4 -.If goal not achieved.-> pd1
    pd4 -.If goal achieved.-> pd5[Standardize and implement widely]

    style pd1 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pd2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pd3 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pd4 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pd5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Cost of Quality (CoQ) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    coq[Cost of Quality CoQ]

    coq --> cat1[Prevention Costs P]
    coq --> cat2[Appraisal Costs A]
    coq --> cat3[Internal Failure Costs I]
    coq --> cat4[External Failure Costs E]

    cat1 --> p1[Quality Planning]
    cat1 --> p2[Training]
    cat1 --> p3[Process Design]

    cat2 --> a1[Inspection]
    cat2 --> a2[Testing]
    cat2 --> a3[Audits]

    cat3 --> i1[Rework]
    cat3 --> i2[Scrap]
    cat3 --> i3[Re-inspection]

    cat4 --> e1[Warranty Claims]
    cat4 --> e2[Returns]
    cat4 --> e3[Loss of Goodwill]

    style coq fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style cat1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style cat2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style cat3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style cat4 fill:#F08080,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Prevention and Appraisal are "cost of conformance" (investments to get quality right), while Internal and External Failure are "cost of non-conformance" (penalties for getting quality wrong). As quality maturity increases, organizations spend **more on P & A** and **less on I & E**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define quality according to Joseph Juran and explain the term "fitness for use."**

**Model Answer:**
Joseph Juran defined quality as **"fitness for use."** This means a product or service must meet the customer's intended purpose across all stages — from design and production to delivery and after-sales support. Fitness for use comprises **five sub-elements**:
- **Quality of Design** — how well the product is conceived.
- **Quality of Conformance** — how well production follows the design.
- **Availability** — reliability and maintainability.
- **Field Performance** — behavior in actual use.
- **Psychological Quality** — the customer's perception of excellence.

A product with high fitness for use satisfies customer needs reliably, safely, and economically. **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **List any three of Deming's 14 Points for Management.**

**Model Answer:**
Three of Deming's 14 Points:
1. **Create constancy of purpose toward improvement of product and service.**
2. **Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality** — eliminate the need for mass inspection by building quality into the product in the first place.
3. **Institute leadership** — the aim of supervision should be to help people and machines do a better job; supervision of management is in need of overhaul.

(Any three correctly stated points from the 14 are acceptable.) **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail **Deming's 14 Points for Management** with their relevance to modern industry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Juran's Quality Trilogy** with a suitable diagram and explain each component in detail. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Deming's 14 Points for Management:**

Deming's 14 Points provide a philosophy of management for transforming business effectiveness. They are:

1. **Create constancy of purpose** toward improvement of products and services, allocating resources to meet long-term competitive needs.
2. **Adopt the new philosophy** — managers must awaken to the challenge of the new economic age and accept responsibility for change.
3. **Cease dependence on inspection** — quality must be built in, not inspected in. Inspection is too late and unreliable.
4. **End the practice of awarding business on price tag alone** — minimize total cost by working with a single supplier.
5. **Improve constantly** every process for quality and productivity.
6. **Institute training on the job** — too often workers are trained for old methods.
7. **Institute leadership** — supervisors should help people and machines perform better.
8. **Drive out fear** so that everyone can work effectively for the company.
9. **Break down barriers between departments** — team work replaces competition.
10. **Eliminate slogans and exhortations** — most failures are due to the system, not the worker.
11. **Eliminate numerical quotas** and management by objective; substitute leadership.
12. **Remove barriers** that rob people of pride of workmanship.
13. **Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.**
14. **Put everybody to work** to accomplish the transformation.

**Relevance:** Modern industries (Toyota, Tata, Bosch) use these points to drive continuous improvement, build customer trust, reduce waste, and create engaged workforces. **[7 Marks]**

**(b) Juran's Quality Trilogy:**

Juran's Trilogy is a three-part framework for managing quality:

$$\text{Quality Trilogy} = \text{Quality Planning} + \text{Quality Control} + \text{Quality Improvement}$$

**1. Quality Planning:**
- Identify customers (internal and external).
- Determine customer needs.
- Develop product features that respond to those needs.
- Develop process capabilities to produce the product features.
- Transfer the resulting plans to operating forces.

**2. Quality Control:**
- Evaluate actual quality performance.
- Compare actual performance to quality goals.
- Act on the difference (take corrective action).

**3. Quality Improvement:**
- Establish the infrastructure needed for continuous improvement.
- Identify the specific projects to be carried out.
- Establish project teams with clear responsibility for bringing projects to a successful conclusion.
- Provide the resources, motivation, and training needed for the teams.

The trilogy forms a continuous spiral; once one round is complete, the next planning cycle begins at a higher level of quality. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss **Crosby's contributions to quality management**. Explain his **Four Absolutes of Quality** and the **Quality Management Maturity Grid**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A shaft has a target diameter of $T = 30.00$ mm with functional tolerance $\Delta_0 = 0.40$ mm. The replacement cost at this tolerance is $A_0 = \text{₹}320$. Using Taguchi's loss function, calculate the quality loss per shaft for a measured diameter of $x = 30.18$ mm. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Crosby's Contributions:**

Philip Crosby was an American quality management expert who authored **"Quality is Free" (1979)** and **"Quality Without Tears" (1984)**.

**Four Absolutes of Quality:**

1. **Quality = Conformance to requirements.** Quality is not goodness or elegance; it is simply meeting the agreed-upon requirements.
2. **Prevention, not inspection.** It is cheaper to prevent defects than to find and fix them. Inspection only reveals problems after they occur.
3. **Zero defects performance standard.** This is not a motivational slogan but a working standard — every defect is preventable, and the only acceptable performance is zero.
4. **Measure quality by the price of non-conformance (PONC).** Quality is measured in monetary terms — what does it cost when things go wrong? PONC acts as the key motivator for improvement.

**Quality Management Maturity Grid (5 Stages):**

| Stage | Name | Characteristics |
|-------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Uncertainty** | No understanding of quality as a management tool; problems are fought as they occur. |
| 2 | **Awakening** | Management recognizes quality as a problem worth solving but no formal program. |
| 3 | **Enlightenment** | A formal quality program is defined and adopted; quality department takes charge. |
| 4 | **Wisdom** | Quality is treated as a management function; prevention is practiced. |
| 5 | **Certainty** | Quality is woven into the company's fabric; quality management is a normal part of operations. |

Crosby's contributions provided actionable, common-sense approaches that organizations could implement without deep statistical expertise, complementing Deming's more theory-heavy framework. **[7 Marks]**

**(b) Taguchi Loss Function — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Target $T = 30.00$ mm
- Functional tolerance $\Delta_0 = 0.40$ mm
- Replacement cost $A_0 = \text{₹}320$
- Measured diameter $x = 30.18$ mm

**Step 1 — Compute the loss coefficient $k$.**

$$k = \frac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2} = \frac{320}{(0.40)^2} = \frac{320}{0.16} = \text{₹}2000 \text{ per mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the deviation from target.**

$$x - T = 30.18 - 30.00 = 0.18 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the loss function.**

$$L(30.18) = k(x - T)^2 = 2000 \times (0.18)^2 = 2000 \times 0.0324 = \text{₹}64.80$$

**Result:** The quality loss per shaft is **₹64.80**, even though the shaft may be well within typical engineering tolerances. If 10,000 such shafts are produced per month, the monthly quality loss becomes **₹6,48,000** — a powerful argument for tighter process control. **[7 Marks]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PDSA (Deming) with PDCA (older model).** KTU expects "PDSA" — Plan, Do, **Study**, Act. Writing "Check" instead of "Study" costs a mark.
> 2. **Memorizing gurus without explaining relevance.** When asked about Juran or Crosby, do not just list points — explain the *philosophy* and give one industrial example.
> 3. **Taguchi Loss Function — unit errors.** Always quote $k$ in **₹ per mm²** (or appropriate squared units) and $L(x)$ in **currency**. Skipping units loses a mark in 14-mark answers.
> 4. **Cost of Quality — mixing prevention and failure categories.** "Rework" is *Internal Failure*, not Prevention. "Warranty" is *External Failure*. Categorical mistakes cost easy marks.
> 5. **Juran Trilogy diagram.** Always draw the cycle (spiral) showing Planning → Control → Improvement feeding back to Planning. A list without a diagram is considered incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Quality Principles — Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Quality** = conformance to requirements + fitness for use (Juran) + customer satisfaction (Ishikawa).
> - **Five Quality Gurus:** Deming (system & PDSA), Juran (trilogy), Crosby (zero defects, 4 absolutes), Ishikawa (fishbone, QC circles), Taguchi (loss function).
> - **Deming's PDSA:** Plan → Do → **Study** → Act (continuous loop).
> - **Juran Trilogy:** Quality Planning + Quality Control + Quality Improvement.
> - **Crosby:** "Quality is Free"; measure via Price of Non-Conformance (PONC); 5-stage Maturity Grid.
> - **Taguchi Loss Function:** $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ where $k = A_0 / (\Delta_0)^2$.
> - **Garvin's 8 dimensions:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.
> - **Cost of Quality (CoQ):** $P + A + I + E$ (Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure). Mature organizations invest more in P&A and less in I&E.
> - **Process Capability:** $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$; $C_{pk} = \min\left\{\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right\}$.
> - **Six Sigma benchmark:** 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO).
> - **TQM philosophy:** Customer focus, continuous improvement, employee involvement, fact-based decision making, supplier partnership.
> - **Pareto Principle:** ~80% of problems stem from ~20% of causes — a foundational prioritization tool.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET611 - INDUSTRIAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Function (Quality Function Deployment – QFD)** is a structured, cross-functional planning and product development methodology used to systematically translate the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** — the latent and stated needs, wants, and expectations of end users — into precise, measurable **technical engineering characteristics** at every stage of product design, manufacturing, and service delivery.

Originated in Japan in **1972** at the Kobe Shipyard of Mitsubishi Heavy Industries by quality pioneers **Akao Yoji** and **Mizuno Shigeru**, QFD is also formally known as the **"House of Quality" (HoQ)** framework because the primary planning matrix resembles the cross-section of a house when diagrammed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** QFD is a customer-driven quality management technique that ensures product/service engineering decisions are anchored to verified customer requirements, thereby minimizing design rework, shortening time-to-market, and maximizing customer satisfaction (CSAT).

The phrase **"Quality Function"** literally means *deploying the customer's quality expectations as a function* into every functional and engineering level of an organisation — from marketing to design to manufacturing to after-sales.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**The Restaurant Translation Analogy:**

Imagine a customer walks into a restaurant and says:
> *"I want something light, refreshing, and not too spicy for tonight."*

That sentence is the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** — fuzzy, qualitative, emotional.

A poor chef will randomly pick a dish. A **QFD-trained chef**, however, will perform the following translation:

| Customer Said (Whats) | Chef Translates To (Hows) |
|---|---|
| Light | Oil content ≤ 5 g, calories ≤ 250 kcal |
| Refreshing | Served cold (≤ 8 °C), citrus-based, garnished with mint |
| Not too spicy | Capsaicin level ≤ mild, no red chili paste |

This **translation from sentiment to specification** is the essence of the Quality Function. The chef has *deployed* the customer's quality wish into measurable kitchen specifications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** *"Listen to the Customer's Whisper, then Engineer the Roar."*

---

## 1.3 Key Standardised Terminology (KTU Standardised Glossary)

The following terms are **high-frequency KTU exam vocabulary** and must be memorised verbatim:

- **VOC** — Voice of the Customer: raw, unfiltered customer expectations gathered via surveys, focus groups, complaint logs.
- **CTQ** — Critical to Quality: the filtered, prioritised subset of VOC that has measurable impact on satisfaction.
- **Whats** — Customer Requirements (CRs); typically placed on the left wall of the HoQ.
- **Hows** — Technical Requirements (TRs) / Engineering Characteristics (ECs); placed across the ceiling.
- **Roof** — the inter-relationship matrix among Hows (technical correlations: strong positive, positive, negative, strong negative).
- **Relationship Matrix** — the central core of HoQ showing strength of correlation between each What and each How (typically 9 = strong, 3 = moderate, 1 = weak, 0 = none).
- **Weight (Absolute & Relative)** — priority ranking of customer requirements.
- **Target Values** — engineering benchmarks for each How.

---

## 1.4 Why the Quality Function Matters in Engineering

| Strategic Benefit | Engineering Outcome |
|---|---|
| Customer-driven design | Reduced warranty cost |
| Cross-functional integration | Shorter design cycle |
| Quantitative prioritisation | Optimal R&D spend |
| Benchmarking vs. competitors | Market differentiation |
| Documentation discipline | ISO 9001 / Six Sigma compliance |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Phases of Quality Function Deployment (The "4 Houses")

QFD is not a single matrix — it is a **cascade of four interconnected matrices**, each translating requirements to the next lifecycle stage.

| Phase | House Name | Translates | Into |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1** | House of Quality | Customer Requirements | Technical (Engineering) Characteristics |
| **Phase 2** | House of Design / Parts Deployment | Technical Characteristics | Component / Subsystem Specifications |
| **Phase 3** | House of Process / Manufacturing Deployment | Component Specs | Manufacturing Process Parameters |
| **Phase 4** | House of Production / Quality Control | Process Parameters | Operator-level Work Instructions & Control Plans |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite:** The first house (House of Quality) carries **~70 %** of the marks whenever QFD appears in ESE questions. Memorise its 7 blocks thoroughly.

---

## 2.2 Architecture of the House of Quality (HoQ) — The Seven Blocks

The HoQ is the master matrix of QFD. It is composed of **seven inter-locking blocks**:

```
Block 1  → Customer Requirements (Whats)            [Left Wall]
Block 2  → Planning Matrix (Importance Weights)     [Left, attached to Block 1]
Block 3  → Technical Requirements (Hows)            [Ceiling]
Block 4  → Relationship Matrix                     [Central Core]
Block 5  → Technical Correlations (Roof)            [Above the Ceiling]
Block 6  → Technical Benchmarking / Targets        [Right Wall]
Block 7  → Competitive Benchmarking                [Bottom Row]
```

### Block-by-Block Logical Walk-Through

**Block 1 — Customer Requirements (Whats):**
Captured as declarative sentences ("Easy to use", "Durable", "Affordable") ranked by importance on a 1–5 scale during VOC interviews. A typical QFD project distils **20–40** CRs.

**Block 2 — Planning Matrix:**
The importance rating $I_j$ (for customer requirement $j$) is multiplied by the **Sales Point** $S_j$ (strategic business weighting) to obtain the **Absolute Weight** $W_j$:

$$W_j = I_j \times S_j$$

The relative weight $w_j$ (used to rank CRs) is then:

$$w_j = \frac{W_j}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} W_k} \times 100 \ \%$$

**Block 3 — Technical Requirements (Hows):**
Engineered specifications expressed as **measurable quantities** (e.g., "Handle Temperature ≤ 45 °C", "Battery Life ≥ 8 hours"). Each How should be **independent** and **measurable**.

**Block 4 — Relationship Matrix (Centre):**
The quantitative heart. Each cell $r_{ij}$ relates How $i$ to What $j$ using a symbolic numerical scale:

| Symbol | Numerical Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| ⊙ (Strong) | **9** | Major contribution to satisfying the requirement |
| ○ (Moderate) | **3** | Some contribution |
| △ (Weak) | **1** | Minor / indirect influence |
| Blank | **0** | No relationship |

**Block 5 — Technical Correlations (Roof):**
Captures **synergy or conflict** between Hows. For example, increasing "Motor Power" and increasing "Battery Capacity" are positively correlated (+), while increasing "Motor Power" and reducing "Weight" are negatively correlated (–).

**Block 6 — Targets / Engineering Benchmarks:**
Each How is assigned a **target value** $T_i$ derived from competitive analysis and engineering feasibility.

**Block 7 — Competitive Benchmarking:**
A scale of 1–5 captures how well the proposed design satisfies each CR **relative to competitors A, B, C**. This drives strategic positioning.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Notation | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_j = I_j \cdot S_j$ | Absolute weight of customer req. $j$ | Prioritise customer demands |
| 2 | $w_j = \dfrac{W_j}{\sum W_k}\cdot 100\%$ | Relative weight (%) | Rank CRs for engineering focus |
| 3 | $P_i = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{ij} \cdot W_j}{\sum_{i} \sum_{j} r_{ij} \cdot W_j}\cdot 100\%$ | Technical priority of How $i$ | Rank engineering characteristics |
| 4 | $\overline{P_i} = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{ij} \cdot W_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{ij}}$ | Mean weighted importance of How $i$ | Normalised view of technical priority |
| 5 | $R_{ij} \in \{0,1,3,9\}$ | Relationship cell value | Standard QFD relationship scale |
| 6 | $C_{ij} \in \{-3,-1,0,1,3\}$ | Roof correlation matrix entry | Detect conflicts among Hows |
| 7 | $T_i^{new} = T_i^{old} \cdot \dfrac{\sum_{j} r_{ij}\cdot B_{j}^{comp}}{\sum_{j} r_{ij}}$ | Revised technical target | Continuous improvement of targets |
| 8 | $CSAT = \sum_{j} w_j \cdot B_j^{ours}$ | Weighted customer satisfaction | Validate predicted satisfaction |
| 9 | $CSAT_{gap} = CSAT_{ours} - CSAT_{competitor}$ | Satisfaction gap | Strategic positioning metric |
| 10 | $TGR = \dfrac{\text{Achieved Sales}}{\text{Target Sales}}\cdot 100\%$ | Target-Go-Rate | Validation after market launch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Marker:** In all KTU answer scripts, explicitly state the **units** of every calculated quantity. Loss of unit marks is the **#1 valuation pitfall** in QFD numerical questions.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of QFD |
|---|---|
| Automotive (Toyota) | Translating driver comfort into chassis tuning specs |
| Software (Microsoft) | Mapping user-experience feedback into UI performance metrics |
| Healthcare | Translating patient comfort into medical device ergonomics |
| Civil Engineering | Mapping client requirements into structural design parameters |
| Electronics | VOC for smartphones → battery capacity, screen resolution, weight specs |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Building a House of Quality for an Electric Kettle

This exhaustive worked example is the **most common 14-mark KTU question format**. Work through every step.

### Step 1 — List the Customer Requirements (Whats)

A market survey on electric kettles yields **four prioritised CRs**:

| j | Customer Requirement (CR) | Importance $I_j$ (1–5) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Boils water quickly | 5 |
| 2 | Easy to pour | 4 |
| 3 | Safe to touch (cool exterior) | 5 |
| 4 | Low energy consumption | 3 |

### Step 2 — Assign Sales Points

Marketing assigns strategic sales points $S_j$ (1–5 scale) based on competitive analysis.

| j | $S_j$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 4 |
| 2 | 3 |
| 3 | 5 |
| 4 | 3 |

### Step 3 — Compute Absolute Weights

Apply $W_j = I_j \cdot S_j$:

| j | $I_j$ | $S_j$ | $W_j = I_j \cdot S_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 4 | **20** |
| 2 | 4 | 3 | **12** |
| 3 | 5 | 5 | **25** |
| 4 | 3 | 3 | **9** |

Total absolute weight:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{4} W_k = 20 + 12 + 25 + 9 = 66$$

### Step 4 — Compute Relative Weights

$$w_j = \frac{W_j}{66} \times 100\ \%$$

| j | $W_j$ | $w_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 30.30 % |
| 2 | 12 | 18.18 % |
| 3 | 25 | 37.88 % |
| 4 | 9 | 13.64 % |

### Step 5 — Define the Technical Requirements (Hows)

| i | Technical Requirement (TR) | Unit | Direction of Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heating element wattage | W (Watt) | Maximise |
| 2 | Spout angle | degrees (°) | Target = 45° |
| 3 | Double-wall insulation thickness | mm | Maximise |
| 4 | Thermostat cut-off accuracy | °C deviation | Minimise |

### Step 6 — Populate the Relationship Matrix

| How \ What | CR₁ (Weight 20) | CR₂ (12) | CR₃ (25) | CR₄ (9) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H₁ (Wattage) | **9** | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| H₂ (Spout angle) | 0 | **9** | 0 | 0 |
| H₃ (Insulation) | 1 | 0 | **9** | 1 |
| H₄ (Thermostat) | 3 | 0 | **3** | **9** |

### Step 7 — Compute Technical Priority $P_i$

$$P_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{4} r_{ij} \cdot W_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{4}\sum_{j=1}^{4} r_{ij} \cdot W_j} \times 100\ \%$$

**Numerator for H₁ (Wattage):**

$$\sum_{j} r_{1j}\cdot W_j = (9)(20) + (1)(12) + (0)(25) + (0)(9) = 180 + 12 + 0 + 0 = 192$$

**Numerator for H₂ (Spout angle):**

$$\sum_{j} r_{2j}\cdot W_j = (0)(20) + (9)(12) + (0)(25) + (0)(9) = 0 + 108 + 0 + 0 = 108$$

**Numerator for H₃ (Insulation):**

$$\sum_{j} r_{3j}\cdot W_j = (1)(20) + (0)(12) + (9)(25) + (1)(9) = 20 + 0 + 225 + 9 = 254$$

**Numerator for H₄ (Thermostat):**

$$\sum_{j} r_{4j}\cdot W_j = (3)(20) + (0)(12) + (3)(25) + (9)(9) = 60 + 0 + 75 + 81 = 216$$

**Total denominator:**

$$\sum_{i}\sum_{j} r_{ij}\cdot W_j = 192 + 108 + 254 + 216 = 770$$

**Final technical priorities:**

| i | How | $\sum r_{ij} W_j$ | $P_i$ (%) | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wattage | 192 | **24.94 %** | 3 |
| 2 | Spout angle | 108 | **14.03 %** | 4 |
| 3 | Insulation | 254 | **32.99 %** | 1 |
| 4 | Thermostat | 216 | **28.05 %** | 2 |

### Step 8 — Interpretation (Engineering Decision Output)

$$\text{Engineering Priority Order: } H_3 > H_4 > H_1 > H_2$$

Engineering should allocate the **largest R&D budget** to **double-wall insulation thickness**, followed by thermostat accuracy, heating wattage, and finally spout angle.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — QFD vs. Other Quality Tools

| Dimension | QFD | Benchmarking | FMEA | Six Sigma DMAIC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Focus | Translate VOC to specs | Compare with competitors | Risk reduction | Variation reduction |
| Stage | Pre-design / design | Throughout | Design / process | Existing process |
| Output | Prioritised TRs | Performance gaps | RPN scores | $\sigma$ improvement |
| Customer Voice? | **Central** | Indirect | Indirect | Indirect |
| Quantitative Output | Weights $w_j$ and priorities $P_i$ | Gap analysis | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | $\text{DPMO}$, $C_p$ |
| KTU Marks Weight | High | Medium | High | Medium |

---

## 3.3 The Kano Model — Optional but High-Value KTU Add-on

The **Kano Model** (Professor Noriaki Kano, 1984) classifies customer requirements into three categories that interact powerfully with QFD:

| Kano Category | Definition | QFD Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Must-Be (Basic)** | Expected; absence causes dissatisfaction | Set minimum thresholds in target values |
| **One-Dimensional (Performance)** | Satisfaction scales linearly | Allocate R&D via $P_i$ priority |
| **Attractive (Excitement)** | Unexpected delighters | Risk R&D investment for differentiation |

> [!NOTE]
> Kano's model is **not separately required** by the KTU 2024 Module-1 syllabus, but examiners often award bonus marks for students who cite it while building the House of Quality.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation — The Mathematical Foundation of $P_i$

For the formal record (KTU values rigorous derivation in 14-mark answers):

$$P_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{ij} W_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{ij} W_j}\cdot 100\%$$

$$\equiv P_i = \frac{\mathbf{r}_i \cdot \mathbf{W}^T}{\mathbf{1}^T R\, \mathbf{W}^T} \cdot 100\%$$

where $\mathbf{r}_i$ is the $i$-th row of the relationship matrix $R$, and $\mathbf{W}$ is the column vector of absolute weights. The denominator equals the total weighted-relationship mass of the entire matrix, ensuring $\sum P_i = 100\ \%$ (conservation property — useful for KTU cross-verification).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The House of Quality — Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph roof["ROOF BLOCK 5: Technical Correlations"]
        corr["+/- Synergy Matrix among Hows"]
    end

    subgraph ceiling["CEILING BLOCK 3: Technical Requirements (Hows)"]
        how1["H1: Wattage W"]
        how2["H2: Spout Angle deg"]
        how3["H3: Insulation mm"]
        how4["H4: Thermostat Accuracy C"]
    end

    subgraph core["CENTRAL CORE BLOCK 4: Relationship Matrix R_ij"]
        rel["Cells in 9, 3, 1, 0"]
    end

    subgraph leftwall["LEFT WALL BLOCK 1 + BLOCK 2: Whats and Weights"]
        cr1["CR1 Boils Quickly I=5"]
        cr2["CR2 Easy Pour I=4"]
        cr3["CR3 Safe Touch I=5"]
        cr4["CR4 Low Energy I=3"]
        wt["Block 2 Importance + Sales Point → Wj"]
    end

    subgraph rightwall["RIGHT WALL BLOCK 6: Technical Targets T_i"]
        tgt["Targets and Direction of Improvement"]
    end

    subgraph bottom["BOTTOM ROW BLOCK 7: Competitive Benchmarking"]
        bench["Scale 1 to 5 vs Competitor A B C"]
    end

    roof --> ceiling
    ceiling --> core
    leftwall --> core
    core --> rightwall
    bottom --> core
```

---

## 4.2 The Four-Phase QFD Cascade (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VOC["VOC: Customer Says WHAT"] --> HOQ["PHASE 1: House of Quality"]
    HOQ -->|Translated into Hows| HOD["PHASE 2: House of Design"]
    HOD -->|Component Specs| HOP["PHASE 3: House of Process"]
    HOP -->|Process Parameters| HOC["PHASE 4: House of Production Control"]
    HOC --> SAT["Customer Satisfaction CSAT"]

    VOC -. Importance Survey .-> HOQ
    HOQ -. Engineering Priorities Pi .-> HOD
    HOD -. Part Characteristics .-> HOP
    HOP -. Work Instructions .-> HOC
    SAT -. Feedback Loop .-> VOC
```

---

## 4.3 Input–Process–Output Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["INPUT: Voice of Customer VOC raw data"] --> B["PROCESS 1: Affinity Diagram"]
    B --> C["PROCESS 2: Kano Classification"]
    C --> D["PROCESS 3: Tree Diagram CTQ Drill-Down"]
    D --> E["PROCESS 4: House of Quality Construction"]
    E --> F["OUTPUT: Prioritised Engineering Characteristics + Targets"]
    F --> G["Validation via CSAT Post Launch"]
    G -->|Feedback| A
```

---

## 4.4 Conflict-Detection Logic in the Roof Block (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["H1 Higher Wattage"] -->|positive| H2["H2 Faster Boil Time"]
    H1 -->|negative| H3["H3 Higher Energy Use"]
    H2 -->|positive| H3
    H4["H4 Thicker Insulation"] -->|positive| H5["H5 Lower Heat Loss"]
    H4 -->|negative| H6["H6 Higher Weight"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each link in the diagram is annotated by the **roof correlation value** $C_{ij} \in \{-3,-1,0,+1,+3\}$. Negative correlations require engineering trade-off studies before finalising targets.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**"Define Quality Function Deployment. List any four components of the House of Quality."**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a systematic, customer-driven quality planning methodology that translates the Voice of the Customer (VOC) into measurable technical engineering requirements throughout the product development lifecycle.

**Four Components of the House of Quality (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Customer Requirements (Whats)** — the prioritised list of customer needs placed on the left wall.
2. **Technical Requirements (Hows)** — the engineering specifications placed on the ceiling.
3. **Relationship Matrix** — the central core quantifying the strength of linkage between each What and each How (using 9, 3, 1, 0).
4. **Technical Correlations (Roof)** — the inter-relationship matrix among the Hows identifying synergies and conflicts.

*(Alternative accepted: Planning Matrix, Competitive Benchmarking, Target Values.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**"Differentiate between 'Whats' and 'Hows' in the House of Quality with one example each."**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Whats | Hows |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Voice of Customer | Engineering translation |
| Nature | Qualitative, declarative | Quantitative, measurable |
| Owner | Marketing / Customer Insights | R&D / Design Engineering |
| Placement in HoQ | Left wall | Ceiling |
| Example (Electric Kettle) | "Boils water fast" | "Wattage ≥ 1500 W" |
| Example (Smartphone) | "Long battery life" | "Battery capacity ≥ 4500 mAh" |

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark distinction table + 1 mark for the example pair — any defensible pair accepted.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of Quality Function Deployment. Describe the seven blocks of the House of Quality with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a washing machine, the following VOC-derived data is collected. Construct the relationship matrix, compute the absolute and relative weights of customer requirements, and determine the technical priorities of the engineering characteristics. **(7 marks)**

| j | Customer Requirement | Importance $I_j$ | Sales Point $S_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cleans clothes effectively | 5 | 5 |
| 2 | Low water consumption | 4 | 3 |
| 3 | Quiet operation | 3 | 4 |
| 4 | Affordable price | 4 | 5 |

| i \ j | CR₁ | CR₂ | CR₃ | CR₄ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H₁ Drum RPM | 9 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| H₂ Water-level sensor accuracy | 0 | 9 | 0 | 1 |
| H₃ Noise-dampening foam | 1 | 0 | 9 | 0 |
| H₄ Material cost index | 0 | 1 | 0 | 9 |

---

#### Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Concept of QFD (2 marks):** QFD is a customer-driven methodology that translates VOC into technical specifications using a matrix-based planning tool. Originated by **Akao Yoji (1972)** at Mitsubishi Heavy Industries.

**2. Seven Blocks of HoQ (4 marks):**
1. Customer Requirements (Whats)
2. Planning Matrix (Importance × Sales Point)
3. Technical Requirements (Hows)
4. Relationship Matrix (9-3-1-0 scale)
5. Roof (Technical Correlations: ±)
6. Target Values / Technical Benchmarks
7. Competitive Benchmarking (1–5 scale vs. competitors)

**3. Block Diagram (1 mark):** *(Draw the standard 7-block House of Quality with roof on top, ceiling below, left wall, central core, right wall, and bottom row.)*

---

#### Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Absolute Weights $W_j = I_j \cdot S_j$:**

| j | $I_j$ | $S_j$ | $W_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 5 | **25** |
| 2 | 4 | 3 | **12** |
| 3 | 3 | 4 | **12** |
| 4 | 4 | 5 | **20** |

Total = 25 + 12 + 12 + 20 = **69**

**Step 2 — Relative Weights $w_j = (W_j / 69)\times 100\%$:**

| j | $w_j$ (%) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 36.23 |
| 2 | 17.39 |
| 3 | 17.39 |
| 4 | 28.99 |

**Step 3 — Technical Priorities $P_i$:**

| i | How | $\sum_j r_{ij} W_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drum RPM | $(9)(25)+(1)(12)+(0)(12)+(0)(20) = 225+12 = 237$ |
| 2 | Water sensor | $(0)(25)+(9)(12)+(0)(12)+(1)(20) = 108+20 = 128$ |
| 3 | Noise foam | $(1)(25)+(0)(12)+(9)(12)+(0)(20) = 25+108 = 133$ |
| 4 | Material cost | $(0)(25)+(1)(12)+(0)(12)+(9)(20) = 12+180 = 192$ |

**Total = 237 + 128 + 133 + 192 = 690**

| i | $P_i$ (%) | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **34.35** | **1** |
| 2 | 18.55 | 4 |
| 3 | 19.28 | 3 |
| 4 | 27.83 | 2 |

**Engineering Priority Order:** $H_1 > H_4 > H_3 > H_2$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The drum RPM deserves the highest engineering investment, followed by material cost optimisation, then noise dampening, then water-sensor accuracy.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Module Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the four phases of QFD (the four houses). Explain how customer requirements flow from Phase 1 to Phase 4 with a flow diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-wheeler manufacturer collects the following data for QFD analysis. Compute (i) absolute weights, (ii) relative weights, and (iii) the technical priority percentages. Identify which engineering characteristic should receive the highest R&D focus. **(7 marks)**

| j | Customer Requirement | $I_j$ | $S_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | High mileage | 5 | 4 |
| 2 | Comfortable ride | 4 | 3 |
| 3 | Stylish appearance | 3 | 4 |
| 4 | Low maintenance cost | 4 | 5 |

| i \ j | CR₁ | CR₂ | CR₃ | CR₄ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H₁ Engine efficiency (%) | 9 | 1 | 0 | 3 |
| H₂ Suspension travel (mm) | 1 | 9 | 0 | 0 |
| H₃ Aerodynamic body design | 0 | 3 | 9 | 0 |
| H₄ Service interval (km) | 3 | 0 | 0 | 9 |

---

#### Solution (a) — 7 Marks

| Phase | House | Input → Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1** | House of Quality | VOC → Engineering characteristics |
| **Phase 2** | House of Design | Engineering chars → Component specs |
| **Phase 3** | House of Process | Component specs → Manufacturing process parameters |
| **Phase 4** | House of Production Control | Process params → Operator instructions and quality control plans |

*(4 marks)*

**Flow Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a left-to-right cascade: VOC → HoQ → HoD → HoP → HoC → Customer Satisfaction. Mention feedback loop from post-launch CSAT back to VOC.

---

#### Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Absolute Weights $W_j = I_j \cdot S_j$:**

| j | $W_j$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | (5)(4) = **20** |
| 2 | (4)(3) = **12** |
| 3 | (3)(4) = **12** |
| 4 | (4)(5) = **20** |

Total = **64**

**Step 2 — Relative Weights $w_j$:**

| j | $w_j$ (%) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 31.25 |
| 2 | 18.75 |
| 3 | 18.75 |
| 4 | 31.25 |

**Step 3 — $\sum_j r_{ij} W_j$:**

| i | How | Calculation | Sum |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Engine efficiency | (9)(20) + (1)(12) + (0)(12) + (3)(20) | 180 + 12 + 0 + 60 = **252** |
| 2 | Suspension travel | (1)(20) + (9)(12) + (0)(12) + (0)(20) | 20 + 108 + 0 + 0 = **128** |
| 3 | Aerodynamic body | (0)(20) + (3)(12) + (9)(12) + (0)(20) | 0 + 36 + 108 + 0 = **144** |
| 4 | Service interval | (3)(20) + (0)(12) + (0)(12) + (9)(20) | 60 + 0 + 0 + 180 = **240** |

Total = 252 + 128 + 144 + 240 = **764**

**Step 4 — Technical Priorities $P_i$:**

| i | $P_i$ (%) | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 32.98 | **1** |
| 2 | 16.75 | 4 |
| 3 | 18.85 | 3 |
| 4 | 31.41 | 2 |

**Engineering Decision (1 mark):** **H₁ — Engine Efficiency** receives the highest R&D focus (32.98 %), followed closely by **H₄ — Service Interval** (31.41 %). Together these two engineering characteristics account for ~64 % of the technical effort.

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons KTU examiners deduct marks in QFD questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to sum the weights first** before computing relative weights → division error cascades → loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "Importance" with "Sales Point"** — these are multiplied, not added. Marks lost for showing $W_j = I_j + S_j$.
> 3. **Skipping the unit declaration** for technical requirements (e.g., writing "rpm" without stating "revolutions per minute"). KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 4. **Mixing the relationship scale** — using 1, 3, 5 instead of 1, 3, 9. Always quote the **standard 9-3-1-0** scale.
> 5. **Not interpreting the result** — the final 1-mark interpretation sentence ("which How receives the highest R&D investment?") is mandatory. Listing priorities without a concluding engineering statement costs a full mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **QFD = Quality Function Deployment** — translates **Voice of Customer (VOC)** into **technical engineering specifications**.
- **Origin:** Akao Yoji, Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, Japan, **1972**.
- **Master tool:** The **House of Quality (HoQ)** — a 7-block matrix.
- **Seven blocks:** CRs (Whats), Planning Matrix, TRs (Hows), Relationship Matrix, Roof, Target Values, Competitive Benchmarking.
- **Relationship scale:** **9 (strong), 3 (moderate), 1 (weak), 0 (none)**.
- **Roof scale:** **+3, +1 (synergy)** and **−3, −1 (conflict)**.
- **Core equations:**
  * $W_j = I_j \cdot S_j$
  * $w_j = (W_j / \sum W_k) \times 100\ \%$
  * $P_i = (\sum_j r_{ij} W_j / \sum_i \sum_j r_{ij} W_j) \times 100\ \%$
- **Conservation property:** $\sum P_i = 100\ \%$ (always verify).
- **Four-phase cascade:** HoQ → House of Design → House of Process → House of Production Control.
- **Engineering priority** is set by **largest $P_i$**, which becomes the focus of R&D budget.
- **Must include in every answer:** a labelled block diagram of the HoQ, explicit units, the relationship scale, and a final interpretive sentence.
- **Avoid pitfalls:** wrong scale, missing units, no conclusion, no conservation check.
- **Bonus vocabulary:** Kano Model (Must-Be / One-Dimensional / Attractive), VOC, CTQ, RPN, CSAT.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Control: Aims and Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET611 - INDUSTRIAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Control: Aims and Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Quality Control

**Quality Control (QC)** is the operational technique and set of activities used by an organization to **fulfill quality requirements and ensure that products or services conform to established standards, specifications, and customer expectations**. It is a management function that involves the systematic measurement, comparison with standards, and the implementation of corrective actions to maintain desired levels of quality throughout the production process.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEPET611)**, Quality Control is formally defined as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Control (QC):** *The regulatory process through which an organization measures actual product performance, compares it against defined standards, and takes corrective action to eliminate the causes of unsatisfactory performance at every stage of the production cycle.*

In simpler terms, Quality Control is the **"watchdog"** of production — it constantly monitors, measures, and ensures that nothing defective slips out of the factory gate.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine you are running a fine-dining restaurant. The **head chef** tastes every dish before it leaves the kitchen. If the soup is too salty, it's rejected and remade. If the steak is undercooked, it goes back to the pan. The chef is essentially performing **Quality Control**.

In this analogy:
| Kitchen Role | Industrial Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Recipe (Standard) | Product Specification |
| Head Chef | Quality Inspector |
| Tasting the dish | Inspection & Testing |
| Sending dish back | Corrective Action |
| Customer feedback | Quality Feedback Loop |

Just as the chef ensures **every plate leaving the kitchen is perfect**, Quality Control ensures **every product leaving the factory meets the promised standard**.

---

## 1.3 Quality vs. Quality Control vs. Quality Assurance

These three terms are **commonly confused** in exams. Understanding their distinction is **critical for scoring marks** in KTU 2024 ESE.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality** is the *degree to which a product meets requirements*.
> **Quality Control (QC)** is the *operational technique of monitoring and recording results* of execution activities to assess performance and recommend corrective action.
> **Quality Assurance (QA)** is the *planned, systematic set of activities implemented within the quality system* that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product will satisfy quality requirements.

**Key Difference:** QC is **reactive & product-oriented** (finds defects), while QA is **proactive & process-oriented** (prevents defects).

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Syllabus Highlights)

The following foundational terms must be memorized for KTU 2024 exams:

- **Quality:** The totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs.
- **Grade:** A category assigned to products having the same functional use but different technical characteristics.
- **Specification:** A document stating requirements for a product or service.
- **Conformance:** Meeting the requirements of specifications, standards, or contract.
- **Defect:** Non-conformance to a requirement or reasonable expectation for use.
- **Inspection:** Examination of a product, process, or installation to determine conformity with specified criteria.
- **Test:** Determination of one or more characteristics of a product according to a procedure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Aims of Quality Control

Quality Control operates under **eight fundamental aims** that every manufacturing or service-based organization must pursue:

### Aim 1: **Ensuring Customer Satisfaction**
The ultimate aim of QC is to deliver products that satisfy — or exceed — customer expectations. This is measured through customer feedback, returns, and warranty claims.

### Aim 2: **Conformance to Specifications**
Every product must conform to pre-defined engineering specifications, dimensional tolerances, material standards, and functional criteria.

### Aim 3: **Minimizing Variability**
QC aims to reduce variation in product characteristics. **Variation** is the enemy of quality, and the goal is to bring every product as close to the **target value** as possible.

### Aim 4: **Cost Reduction**
Defective products cost money — in rework, scrap, warranty, recalls, and lost reputation. QC reduces these costs by catching defects early.

### Aim 5: **Waste Minimization**
By preventing defects at the source, QC reduces material waste, time waste, and human effort waste. This aligns with **Lean Manufacturing** principles.

### Aim 6: **Process Improvement**
QC generates data that drives continuous improvement (Kaizen), enabling organizations to refine processes iteratively.

### Aim 7: **Reliability and Durability**
QC ensures that products perform reliably throughout their intended lifespan, reducing field failures.

### Aim 8: **Regulatory & Standards Compliance**
QC ensures adherence to national and international standards such as **ISO 9001**, **BIS**, **ASTM**, and industry-specific regulations.

---

## 2.2 The Core Objectives of Quality Control

While *aims* are broad goals, *objectives* are specific, measurable targets. The **primary objectives** of QC are:

1. **To detect and eliminate defective products** before they reach the customer.
2. **To establish acceptable levels of quality** through sampling plans and inspection criteria.
3. **To provide feedback** to production and design teams on quality issues.
4. **To ensure that inspection and testing equipment** is calibrated and reliable.
5. **To document quality data** for traceability and audit purposes.
6. **To support continuous improvement** through Statistical Process Control (SPC) and root cause analysis.

---

## 2.3 Quality Control Activities (The QC Process Loop)

The QC function operates through a continuous **five-step loop**:

1. **Establish Standards:** Define quality benchmarks based on customer needs, regulations, and engineering specs.
2. **Measure Performance:** Inspect, test, and collect data on actual product characteristics.
3. **Compare with Standards:** Analyze the gap between measured performance and standards.
4. **Take Corrective Action:** Eliminate the cause of non-conformance.
5. **Follow-Up:** Verify that corrective actions are effective and update standards if needed.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Quality Control Metrics

The following formulas are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 ESE numerical problems. Practice these rigorously.

| Metric | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Defect Rate (DR)** | $DR = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units Inspected}} \times 100$ | Expressed as **percentage** |
| **Yield (Y)** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100$ | Higher yield = better quality |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times \ldots \times Y_n$ | Product of yields at each process step |
| **Parts Per Million (PPM)** | $PPM = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 10^6$ | Used in Six Sigma |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$ | Core Six Sigma metric |
| **Process Capability Index (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Measures process spread |
| **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$ | Accounts for centering |
| **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $CoQ = P_c + A_c + IF_c + EF_c$ | $P_c$ = Prevention, $A_c$ = Appraisal, $IF_c$ = Internal Failure, $EF_c$ = External Failure |
| **Quality Level (QL)** | $QL = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defectives}}{\text{Total Inspected}} \times 100$ | Also called **Percent Defective** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
