> **Syllabus Highlight:** The **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** framework is heavily tested in KTU 2024. Remember that Prevention and Appraisal costs are **value-added** (you choose to spend them), while Internal and External Failure costs are **non-value-added** (you are forced to bear them).

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Quality Control

In real-world engineering, Quality Control is the backbone of:
- **Automotive Industry:** Every component (engine, brakes, electronics) undergoes QC to prevent life-threatening failures.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Drug purity, dosage accuracy, and shelf-life are all QC-controlled.
- **Aerospace:** Zero-defect tolerance for critical components.
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Solder joint inspection, PCB testing, and burn-in tests.
- **Civil Engineering:** Concrete compressive strength tests, steel tensile tests, and soil bearing capacity verification.

Quality Control is not optional — it is the **non-negotiable foundation** upon which trustworthy engineering products are built.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Process Capability Index ($C_p$)

The **Process Capability Index ($C_p$)** quantifies how well a process can produce output within specification limits.

### Step 1: Identify the Specification Limits
Every product has an **Upper Specification Limit (USL)** and a **Lower Specification Limit (LSL)**. These are engineering-defined boundaries.

### Step 2: Identify the Process Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)
The **standard deviation** measures the natural spread of the process. In a stable process, $\pm 3\sigma$ covers **99.73%** of all output (the empirical rule).

### Step 3: Compute the Total Specification Width
The total allowable spread of variation is:

$$\text{Total Spread} = USL - LSL$$

### Step 4: Compute the Natural Process Spread
For a stable, normally distributed process, the natural spread is **6 standard deviations** (i.e., $\pm 3\sigma$ on either side of the mean).

### Step 5: Formulate the Capability Index
$C_p$ is the ratio of the specification width to the natural process spread:

$$\boxed{C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}}$$

### Step 6: Interpret $C_p$
- $C_p \geq 1.33$: Process is **capable** (industry standard).
- $C_p = 1.00$: Process is **barely capable**.
- $C_p < 1.00$: Process is **not capable**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)

$C_p$ assumes the process is perfectly **centered** between USL and LSL. In reality, processes drift. $C_{pk}$ accounts for this **off-centering**.

### Step 1: Define Process Mean ($\bar{X}$)
The average of sample measurements.

### Step 2: Compute the Upper Capability
Measures how many $\sigma$'s the mean is below the USL:

$$\text{Upper Capability} = \frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Lower Capability
Measures how many $\sigma$'s the mean is above the LSL:

$$\text{Lower Capability} = \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

### Step 4: Take the Minimum
$C_{pk}$ is the **smaller** of the two — because the binding constraint is whichever side is closer to its limit:

$$\boxed{C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]}$$

### Step 5: Interpret $C_{pk}$
- $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$: **Excellent** — Six Sigma-level performance requires $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$.
- $C_{pk} = 1.00$: **Marginal**.
- $C_{pk} < 1.00$: **Process produces defects**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A CNC machining process produces shafts with a target diameter of $\bar{X} = 25.00$ mm. The specification limits are $USL = 25.10$ mm and $LSL = 24.90$ mm. The process standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.02$ mm. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

**Solution:**

### Step A: Compute $C_p$

Using the formula:

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

Substituting the values:

$$C_p = \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.02}$$

$$C_p = \frac{0.20}{0.12}$$

$$C_p = 1.667$$

**Interpretation:** $C_p = 1.667 > 1.33$, so the process is **capable**.

### Step B: Compute $C_{pk}$

Compute the upper capability:

$$\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 25.00}{3 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.10}{0.06} = 1.667$$

Compute the lower capability:

$$\frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.00 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.10}{0.06} = 1.667$$

Take the minimum:

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.667, 1.667) = 1.667$$

**Interpretation:** Since $C_p = C_{pk}$, the process is **perfectly centered**. A $C_{pk}$ of 1.667 indicates excellent capability with minimal defect risk.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Cost of Quality (CoQ) Breakdown

The **Cost of Quality** is the total cost incurred to ensure quality plus the cost of failures due to lack of quality.

### Step 1: Categorize All Quality-Related Costs

$$\boxed{CoQ = P_c + A_c + IF_c + EF_c}$$

Where:
- $P_c$ = **Prevention Costs** (training, quality planning, design reviews)
- $A_c$ = **Appraisal Costs** (inspection, testing, calibration)
- $IF_c$ = **Internal Failure Costs** (rework, scrap, re-inspection)
- $EF_c$ = **External Failure Costs** (warranty claims, returns, recalls, loss of reputation)

### Step 2: Show the Optimal Cost Relationship

As prevention costs **increase**, failure costs **decrease dramatically**. The **optimum** is the point where total CoQ is minimized.

$$\frac{d(CoQ)}{d(P_c)} = 0 \implies \text{Optimal } P_c \text{ exists}$$

This is shown schematically in Section 4.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Quality Control Process Flow (PDCA + Inspection Loop)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete **Quality Control operational flow**, integrating the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle of Deming with the inspection-corrective action loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["A1: Establish Quality Standards"] --> B["A2: Define Specifications and Tolerances"]
    B --> C["A3: Design Inspection Plan"]
    C --> D["A4: Produce Product in Process"]
    D --> E["A5: Inspect and Test Output"]
    E --> F{"A6: Conformance Check"}
    F -->|Yes / Pass| G["A7: Approve and Release to Customer"]
    F -->|No / Fail| H["A8: Identify Root Cause of Defect"]
    H --> I["A9: Implement Corrective Action"]
    I --> J["A10: Update Standards and Process"]
    J --> A
    G --> K["A11: Collect Customer Feedback"]
    K --> L["A12: Analyze Field Data for Improvement"]
    L --> A
```

### Diagram Interpretation:
- **Nodes A1–A4** correspond to the **Plan** phase of the Deming Cycle.
- **Nodes A4–A5** correspond to the **Do** phase.
- **Nodes E–F** correspond to the **Check** phase.
- **Nodes H–J** correspond to the **Act** phase.
- **Nodes G–L** form the **Customer Feedback Loop**, closing the continuous improvement cycle.

---

## 4.2 Quality Cost Optimization Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Prevention Cost - Increases with Quality Investment"] --> T["Total Cost of Quality"]
    A["Appraisal Cost - Inspection and Testing"] --> T
    IF["Internal Failure Cost - Rework and Scrap"] --> T
    EF["External Failure Cost - Warranty and Returns"] --> T
    T --> O{"Optimum Point"}
    O --> M["Minimum Total Quality Cost"]
    IF -.->|Decreases as prevention rises| P
    EF -.->|Decreases as prevention rises| P
```

### Diagram Interpretation:
- **Prevention Cost ($P_c$)** rises linearly as the organization invests more in quality.
- **Internal Failure Cost ($IF_c$)** and **External Failure Cost ($EF_c$)** drop sharply as prevention rises.
- **The Optimum Point** is where the total cost curve is at its minimum — beyond this, spending more on prevention yields diminishing returns.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Quality Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["INPUT DOMAIN"]
        IN1["Customer Requirements"]
        IN2["Engineering Specifications"]
        IN3["Regulatory Standards"]
    end

    subgraph QCcore["QUALITY CONTROL ENGINE"]
        QC1["Standards Module - Defines tolerances and acceptance criteria"]
        QC2["Measurement Module - Sensors, gauges, and inspection tools"]
        QC3["Analysis Module - SPC charts, capability indices, trend analysis"]
        QC4["Decision Module - Accept, Reject, or Rework"]
        QC5["Feedback Module - Corrective action and continuous improvement"]
    end

    subgraph Output["OUTPUT DOMAIN"]
        OUT1["Conforming Products to Customer"]
        OUT2["Quality Reports to Management"]
        OUT3["Process Improvement Recommendations"]
    end

    IN1 --> QC1
    IN2 --> QC1
    IN3 --> QC1
    QC1 --> QC2
    QC2 --> QC3
    QC3 --> QC4
    QC4 --> QC5
    QC5 --> QC1
    QC4 --> OUT1
    QC3 --> OUT2
    QC5 --> OUT3
```

### Diagram Interpretation:
- The **Input Domain** collects all requirements.
- The **Quality Control Engine** (QC core) processes these through five functional modules in a loop.
- The **Output Domain** delivers conforming products, management reports, and improvement recommendations.
- The loop **QC5 → QC1** represents the **continuous improvement feedback** — ensuring the system evolves with every cycle.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define Quality Control. List any four aims of Quality Control.
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Quality Control (QC)** is the operational technique of monitoring and recording results of execution activities to assess performance and recommend corrective action, ensuring products conform to established standards and specifications. **(1 Mark)**
>
> **Four Aims of QC (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Customer Satisfaction:** Deliver products that meet or exceed customer expectations.
> 2. **Conformance to Specifications:** Ensure every product lies within defined engineering tolerances.
> 3. **Cost Reduction:** Minimize costs arising from rework, scrap, warranty, and recalls.
> 4. **Waste Minimization:** Prevent material, time, and effort waste by detecting defects early.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between Quality Control (QC) and Quality Assurance (QA).
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Parameter | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Focus** | Product-oriented | Process-oriented |
> | **Approach** | Reactive — finds defects | Proactive — prevents defects |
> | **Timing** | After production / during inspection | Throughout the entire process |
> | **Goal** | Identify and remove defective products | Build quality into the process from the start |
> | **Tools Used** | Inspection, testing, gauges | Audits, process design, documentation |
>
> *(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for any 4 valid distinguishing points)*

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** *Quality Control — Aims, Objectives, and Process Capability*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q(a).** Explain in detail the **aims and objectives of Quality Control**. How do these aims translate into measurable engineering outcomes?

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Quality Control is a management function that aims to ensure products and services meet specified requirements. Its aims and objectives are not abstract ideals but are translated into measurable engineering outcomes.

**Aims of QC (3 Marks — 0.5 each for any 6):**
1. **Customer Satisfaction:** Measured via Net Promoter Score (NPS), return rates, and customer complaint frequency.
2. **Conformance to Standards:** Measured via First Pass Yield (FPY), rejection rates at inspection.
3. **Cost Reduction:** Measured via Cost of Quality (CoQ) trends over fiscal periods.
4. **Waste Minimization:** Measured via scrap percentage and rework hours.
5. **Process Improvement:** Measured via $C_pk$ trends, defect density per unit.
6. **Reliability:** Measured via Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) in field performance.
7. **Regulatory Compliance:** Measured via audit pass rate and zero non-conformance reports.

**Objectives of QC (2 Marks):**
The specific objectives include detection and elimination of defective products, establishment of acceptable quality levels, providing feedback to production and design teams, calibration of inspection equipment, and documentation for traceability.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
The aims and objectives of QC form a hierarchy — from broad customer-focused aims down to specific, measurable operational objectives — ensuring that quality is not an accident but a deliberate engineering outcome.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q(b).** A manufacturing process has the following parameters:
- Mean diameter: $\bar{X} = 50.00$ mm
- Standard deviation: $\sigma = 0.05$ mm
- Upper Specification Limit: $USL = 50.20$ mm
- Lower Specification Limit: $LSL = 49.80$ mm

Calculate: **(i)** Process Capability Index $C_p$, **(ii)** Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$, and **(iii)** Comment on process capability.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Calculate $C_p$ (3 Marks):**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_p = \frac{50.20 - 49.80}{6 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.40}{0.30} = 1.333$$

**(ii) Calculate $C_{pk}$ (3 Marks):**

$$\text{Upper Capability} = \frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} = \frac{50.20 - 50.00}{3 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.20}{0.15} = 1.333$$

$$\text{Lower Capability} = \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{50.00 - 49.80}{3 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.20}{0.15} = 1.333$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.333, 1.333) = 1.333$$

**(iii) Comment (1 Mark):**
Since $C_p = C_{pk} = 1.333 \geq 1.33$, the process is **capable** and **perfectly centered**. The process meets the industry benchmark for capability. However, for **Six Sigma** performance, $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$ is required, indicating room for improvement to achieve world-class quality.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** *Cost of Quality and PDCA Cycle*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q(a).** What is the **Cost of Quality (CoQ)**? Explain its four components with suitable examples. How does preventive investment reduce overall CoQ?

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
**Cost of Quality (CoQ)** is the total cost incurred by an organization to ensure product quality plus the cost of failures resulting from poor quality. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$CoQ = P_c + A_c + IF_c + EF_c$$

**Four Components (4 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **Prevention Costs ($P_c$):** Costs incurred to *prevent* defects. Examples: quality training programs, supplier capability audits, design reviews, quality planning.
2. **Appraisal Costs ($A_c$):** Costs of *detecting* defects through inspection and testing. Examples: incoming material inspection, in-process inspection, final product testing, calibration of gauges.
3. **Internal Failure Costs ($IF_c$):** Costs of defects found *before* delivery to the customer. Examples: scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime due to quality issues.
4. **External Failure Costs ($EF_c$):** Costs of defects discovered *after* delivery. Examples: warranty claims, product recalls, field repair costs, legal liability, loss of reputation.

**Preventive Investment Effect (2 Marks):**
As prevention costs ($P_c$) increase, the organization invests in training, better design, and robust processes. This dramatically reduces both internal and external failure costs. The **Total CoQ** curve has an **optimum point** — beyond which additional prevention spending yields diminishing returns. The ideal strategy is to shift the cost mix from **failure costs** (reactive) to **prevention costs** (proactive).

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q(b).** Explain the **Deming Cycle (PDCA)** in detail. How does it support the objectives of Quality Control?

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The **Deming Cycle**, also known as the **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act), is a four-stage iterative management methodology developed by **W. Edwards Deming** for continuous improvement of processes and products.

**The Four Phases (4 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **PLAN:** Identify the problem, analyze current process, and design a plan for improvement. Define quality objectives, metrics, and acceptance criteria. Tools: Cause-and-effect diagrams, Pareto charts.
2. **DO:** Execute the plan on a small scale (pilot run). Collect data on process performance and quality outcomes. Implement the changes as designed.
3. **CHECK:** Evaluate the results by comparing actual performance against planned objectives. Use Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts and capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) to validate improvement.
4. **ACT:** If the change is successful, standardize it across the organization. If not, revise the plan and re-enter the cycle. This phase ensures that lessons learned are institutionalized.

**Support to QC Objectives (2 Marks):**
The PDCA cycle directly supports QC objectives by providing a **structured, repeatable framework** for:
- Detecting and eliminating defects (Check phase),
- Providing feedback to production (Act phase),
- Documenting quality data (Plan and Do phases), and
- Driving continuous process improvement (re-iteration of the cycle).

By embedding PDCA into the QC system, organizations achieve the **aim of continuous improvement** — making quality not a one-time achievement but an ongoing engineering discipline.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse QC with QA** — students frequently lose 1–2 marks by stating definitions interchangeably. QC is *product-focused and reactive*; QA is *process-focused and proactive*.
> 2. **Always show units in numerical answers** — forgetting mm, %, or σ units may cost 0.5 marks.
> 3. **In $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ problems, always compute both** — examiners often award partial marks only for showing both calculations correctly.
> 4. **CoQ questions require all four categories** — missing even one (especially External Failure) leads to loss of 1 mark.
> 5. **PDCA cycle questions must include all four stages with examples** — vague answers lose marks; specific tool references (SPC, Pareto, Fishbone) earn bonus marks.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Quality Control (QC)** is the *reactive, product-focused* operational function of monitoring, inspecting, and correcting products to meet specifications.
- **Quality Assurance (QA)** is the *proactive, process-focused* system of planned activities designed to prevent defects.
- **Aims of QC** are broad strategic goals: customer satisfaction, conformance, cost reduction, waste minimization, reliability, and regulatory compliance.
- **Objectives of QC** are specific, measurable targets: defect detection, feedback provision, equipment calibration, documentation, and continuous improvement.
- **Key Metrics:** Defect Rate, Yield, RTY, PPM, DPMO, $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, CoQ, and Quality Level.
- **Process Capability Indices:**
  - $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ (centered process measure)
  - $C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ (accounts for off-centering)
  - Industry benchmark: $C_p \geq 1.33$; Six Sigma benchmark: $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$.
- **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure costs.
- **PDCA Cycle (Deming):** Plan → Do → Check → Act — the backbone of continuous quality improvement.
- **Quality Gurus to Remember:** Deming (PDCA), Juran (Quality Trilogy), Crosby (Zero Defects), Feigenbaum (Total Quality Control).
- **Golden Rule:** Prevention costs are **investments**; failure costs are **penalties**. The goal of QC is to maximize prevention and minimize failure costs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Quality
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET611 - INDUSTRIAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cost of Quality

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the total financial expenditure incurred by an organization to ensure that its products and services conform to specified quality requirements, plus the financial losses resulting from non-conformance to those requirements. It is a comprehensive economic measurement tool used in Total Quality Management (TQM) to quantify the monetary impact of quality-related activities across the entire value chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Official KTU Definition:** *"The cost of quality is the sum of all costs incurred in the prevention, detection, and rectification of defects, plus the losses arising from defective products reaching or failing within the customer environment."*

The COQ is conventionally classified into **four major cost categories** following the universally adopted **PAF (Prevention-Appraisal-Failure) Model**:

| # | Category | Symbol | Nature of Cost |
|---|----------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Prevention Costs | $C_P$ | Proactive (planned) |
| 2 | Appraisal Costs | $C_A$ | Detection (planned) |
| 3 | Internal Failure Costs | $C_{IF}$ | Reactive (unplanned) |
| 4 | External Failure Costs | $C_{EF}$ | Reactive (unplanned) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The British Standard **BS 6143** (Parts 1 & 2) is the official guideline referenced in the KTU syllabus for the **Process Cost Model** and the **Quality Cost Model**. Students must remember that the optimal quality cost is reached when the **marginal cost of prevention equals the marginal saving from reduced failures**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine you are driving a car. The **Cost of Quality** is everything you spend to keep the car running smoothly AND every penalty you pay when it breaks down:

- **Prevention Costs** = Regular servicing, oil changes, tire rotations (you spend before any problem occurs).
- **Appraisal Costs** = Mechanic inspections, computer diagnostics, emission tests (you spend to detect hidden problems early).
- **Internal Failure Costs** = Engine failure caught inside the garage, brake-pad replacement after a squeal (defects detected *before* you drive out).
- **External Failure Costs** = Car breaks down on the highway, accident, warranty claims, towing charges, customer compensation (defects detected *after* delivery).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Rule of Thumb:** *Every rupee saved on Prevention usually saves 10–100 rupees in Failure costs.* This is the fundamental economic argument for quality.

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used in COQ reporting:

- **COPQ** – Cost of Poor Quality (the failure-component subset of COQ).
- **COGQ** – Cost of Good Quality (the prevention+appraisal subset).
- **COQ as a % of Sales** – Industry benchmark metric.
- **Quality Cost per Unit** – $\dfrac{C_{total}}{N_{units}}$.
- **Defect Rate (DPMO)** – Defects Per Million Opportunities.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** *Cost of Quality is NOT the cost of making a high-quality product.* It is the cost associated with **achieving** and **not achieving** quality. Many students confuse "Quality Cost" with "Product Cost."

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PAF Model – Four Pillars of Quality Cost

The **PAF Model**, originally developed by Armand V. Feigenbaum in 1956 and later refined by Juran, classifies every quality-related rupee into one of four logical buckets.

### 2.1.1 Prevention Costs ($C_P$)
These are the costs of all activities specifically designed to **prevent poor quality** from occurring in the first place. They are the most strategic and value-generating investments in the quality domain.

**Typical Examples:**
- Quality planning and administration
- New-product quality engineering
- Supplier capability surveys and audits
- Training in Statistical Quality Control (SQC)
- Quality improvement projects (Kaizen, Six-Sigma DMAIC)
- Design of Experiments (DoE) studies
- Field testing
- Preventive equipment maintenance

### 2.1.2 Appraisal Costs ($C_A$)
These are the costs incurred to **detect** non-conforming products *before* shipment to the customer. They are the cost of "looking for problems."

**Typical Examples:**
- Incoming inspection and testing
- In-process inspection
- Final product acceptance testing
- Calibration of measuring equipment
- Quality audits of operating processes
- Cost of inspection equipment depreciation

### 2.1.3 Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$)
These are the costs of products that **failed to meet quality requirements** but were detected *before* being shipped to the customer.

**Typical Examples:**
- Scrap and rework
- Re-inspection and re-testing
- Material review and disposition
- Downtime caused by quality defects
- Failure-mode analysis
- Design changes due to internal defects

### 2.1.4 External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$)
These are the costs of products that **reached the customer** but failed to meet quality requirements. This is the most damaging category and includes **intangible costs** like loss of goodwill.

**Typical Examples:**
- Warranty claims and field service
- Returned materials and product recalls
- Complaint handling and customer service
- Liability insurance and product-liability lawsuits
- Lost customer goodwill and market share
- Penalty payments and product-liability settlements

## 2.2 The Master Equation of Cost of Quality

The **Total Cost of Quality** is the sum of all four categories:

$$C_{TQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative Industry Convention:** Some firms (especially those using BS 6143 Part 2) report only two categories: **Conformance Costs** $= C_P + C_A$ and **Non-Conformance Costs** $= C_{IF} + C_{EF}$. Both models are accepted in KTU examinations, but the four-category PAF model is the **default expected answer**.

## 2.3 COPQ – Cost of Poor Quality (Failure-Only View)

Many TQM practitioners prefer the focused metric of **COPQ** because it directly measures the financial damage caused by poor quality:

$$C_{COPQ} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

Equivalently:
$$C_{COPQ} = C_{TQ} - C_P - C_A$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles every equation, definition, and standard index you are likely to need for a KTU 14-mark derivation or numerical problem on Cost of Quality.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Remarks |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Total Quality Cost | $C_{TQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Currency (₹, \$, €) |
| 2 | Cost of Poor Quality | $C_{COPQ} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Currency |
| 3 | Conformance Cost | $C_{Conf} = C_P + C_A$ | Currency |
| 4 | Non-Conformance Cost | $C_{NonConf} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Currency |
| 5 | COQ as % of Sales | $\%COQ = \dfrac{C_{TQ}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | % (typical: 2.5–25 %) |
| 6 | Quality Cost per Unit | $Q_{unit} = \dfrac{C_{TQ}}{N_{units}}$ | Currency / unit |
| 7 | Optimum (Min) Total Cost | $\dfrac{dC_{TQ}}{dC_P} = 0 \;\;\text{or}\;\; \dfrac{\partial C_{IF} + \partial C_{EF}}{\partial C_P} = -1$ | Optimization |
| 8 | Marginal Trade-off | $\Delta C_P = -(\Delta C_{IF} + \Delta C_{EF})$ at optimum | Ratio test |
| 9 | Quality Investment ROI | $ROI_Q = \dfrac{C_{COPQ,\text{before}} - C_{COPQ,\text{after}}}{C_{PI}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Indirect / Hidden Costs | $C_{Hidden} \approx 4 \times C_{EF}$ (industry heuristic) | Multiplier |
| 11 | Standard PAF Ratio (Target) | $C_P : C_A : C_{IF} : C_{EF} = 30 : 25 : 20 : 25$ | World-class target |
| 12 | Quality Cost Index (QCI) | $QCI = \dfrac{C_{IF} + C_{EF}}{C_{TQ}}$ | Healthy if $< 40\%$ |
| 13 | DPMO to Sigma Level | $\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}(1 - DPMO/10^6) + 1.5$ | Sigma scale |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always state the **units** of every numerical answer. A correct number without units loses 0.5–1 mark.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Cost of Quality framework is the **financial language** of quality management. It is used in industry for:

1. **Justifying quality investments** to top management and shareholders.
2. **Benchmarking** quality performance across plants, divisions, and competitors.
3. **Identifying cost-reduction opportunities** (where to spend and where to save).
4. **Tracking improvement** after Six-Sigma, Lean, or TQM deployments.
5. **Setting strategic quality budgets** for the upcoming fiscal year.
6. **Supplier evaluation and rating** in supply-chain management.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Optimum Quality Cost – Full Calculus Derivation

This is the most frequently asked 14-mark derivation in KTU Module 1. The objective is to find the level of prevention investment $C_P$ that **minimises** the total quality cost $C_{TQ}$.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Model

Assume the following empirical (and well-verified) relationships:

- As $C_P$ increases linearly, $C_A$ rises slowly (engineers need better gauges): $C_A = a_0 + a_1 C_P$.
- As $C_P$ increases, $C_{IF}$ decreases exponentially: $C_{IF} = b_0 \, e^{-b_1 C_P}$.
- As $C_P$ increases, $C_{EF}$ decreases even more sharply (defects prevent at the source): $C_{EF} = c_0 \, e^{-c_1 C_P}$.

Where $a_0, a_1, b_0, b_1, c_0, c_1$ are all **positive constants** determined by regression on historical data.

### 3.1.2 Substituting into the Master Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{TQ}(C_P) &= C_P + C_A(C_P) + C_{IF}(C_P) + C_{EF}(C_P) \\[4pt]
&= C_P + (a_0 + a_1 C_P) + b_0 e^{-b_1 C_P} + c_0 e^{-c_1 C_P} \\[4pt]
&= (1 + a_1)\,C_P + a_0 + b_0 e^{-b_1 C_P} + c_0 e^{-c_1 C_P}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Total Cost Function** in terms of the decision variable $C_P$.

### 3.1.3 First-Order Condition for Optimum

To find the minimum, differentiate with respect to $C_P$ and set equal to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d\,C_{TQ}}{d\,C_P} &= 0 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{d}{d C_P}\left[(1+a_1)C_P + a_0 + b_0 e^{-b_1 C_P} + c_0 e^{-c_1 C_P}\right] &= 0 \\[6pt]
(1 + a_1) - b_0 b_1 e^{-b_1 C_P^*} - c_0 c_1 e^{-c_1 C_P^*} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging yields the **optimum-prevention-cost equation**:

$$
b_0 b_1 e^{-b_1 C_P^*} + c_0 c_1 e^{-c_1 C_P^*} = 1 + a_1
$$

This transcendental equation is solved numerically (Newton-Raphson) for $C_P^*$.

### 3.1.4 Economic Interpretation of the Optimum

At the optimum, the **marginal cost** of one more rupee spent on prevention equals the **marginal saving** in failure costs:

$$
\underbrace{1 + a_1}_{\text{Marginal Cost of Prevention}} = \underbrace{b_0 b_1 e^{-b_1 C_P^*} + c_0 c_1 e^{-c_1 C_P^*}}_{\text{Marginal Saving in Failure Costs}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Economic Rule of Quality Investment:** *Spend on prevention up to the point where the last rupee of prevention yields exactly one rupee of failure-cost saving.* Beyond that, prevention is over-investment; below it, the firm is leaving "money on the table."

### 3.1.5 Second-Order Sufficient Condition

We confirm the critical point is a minimum by checking the second derivative:

$$
\dfrac{d^2 C_{TQ}}{d C_P^2} = b_0 b_1^2 e^{-b_1 C_P^*} + c_0 c_1^2 e^{-c_1 C_P^*} \;>\; 0
$$

Since the exponentials and constants are all positive, the second derivative is strictly positive. Hence $C_P^*$ is a **confirmed global minimum**. 

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A 7-mark numerical problem typically supplies 3–4 cost-category values and asks you to compute Total COQ, COPQ, % of Sales, and Quality Cost Index. The matrix below is the model answer template.

### Given Data
A medium-scale pump-manufacturing unit reports the following annual quality costs:

| Cost Category | Amount (₹ Lakhs) |
|---------------|------------------|
| Prevention ($C_P$) | 4.5 |
| Appraisal ($C_A$) | 3.2 |
| Internal Failure ($C_{IF}$) | 6.8 |
| External Failure ($C_{EF}$) | 5.5 |
| Net Annual Sales | 80.0 |
| Units Produced per Year | 4 000 |

### Solution

**Step 1 – Total Quality Cost:**
$$
C_{TQ} = 4.5 + 3.2 + 6.8 + 5.5 = 20.0 \;\;\text{lakhs}
$$

**Step 2 – Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ):**
$$
C_{COPQ} = C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 6.8 + 5.5 = 12.3 \;\;\text{lakhs}
$$

**Step 3 – Conformance Cost:**
$$
C_{Conf} = C_P + C_A = 4.5 + 3.2 = 7.7 \;\;\text{lakhs}
$$

**Step 4 – COQ as % of Sales:**
$$
\%COQ = \dfrac{20.0}{80.0} \times 100 = 25\%
$$

**Step 5 – Quality Cost per Unit:**
$$
Q_{unit} = \dfrac{20.0 \times 10^{5}}{4000} = ₹500 \;\;\text{per pump}
$$

**Step 6 – Quality Cost Index (QCI):**
$$
QCI = \dfrac{C_{IF} + C_{EF}}{C_{TQ}} = \dfrac{12.3}{20.0} = 0.615 \;\;\text{i.e.,}\;\; 61.5\%
$$

### 3.2.1 Interpretation Matrix (for the model answer's "discussion" part)

| Metric | Calculated | Benchmark | Diagnostic Verdict |
|--------|------------|-----------|--------------------|
| $C_{TQ}$ as % of Sales | 25 % | World-class: 2.5–6 % | **Severely over-spending** on quality |
| $C_P$ share of $C_{TQ}$ | 22.5 % | World-class: ≥ 30 % | **Under-investing in prevention** |
| $C_{EF}$ share of $C_{TQ}$ | 27.5 % | World-class: < 15 % | **Critical – many defects reaching customer** |
| QCI | 61.5 % | World-class: < 40 % | **Failure-dominated cost structure** |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** Whenever the QCI > 50 %, the answer's recommendation must be: *"Increase prevention spending, train staff in SPC, and shift the cost structure from reactive to proactive."*

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PAF Model – Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Total Cost of Quality CTQ"] --> nodeB["Conformance Costs"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Non Conformance Costs"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Prevention Costs CP"]

    nodeC --> nodeF["Internal Failure Costs CIF"]

    nodeE --> nodeI["Incoming Inspection, In process Check, Calibration, Audits"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Scrap, Rework, Re inspection, Downtime, Material Review"]
    nodeG --> nodeK["Warranty, Returns, Recalls, Complaints, Liability, Lost Goodwill"]
    nodeH --> nodeL["Proactive Strategic Investment"]
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeM["Reactive Unplanned Loss"]
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeN["Customer Satisfaction and Profit"]
    nodeM --> nodeN
```

```

    nodeC["Appraisal Cost CA"] --> nodeB

    nodeG["External Failure CEF"] --> nodeF

```

```mermaid

    nodeA["Need to Quantify Quality Cost"] --> nodeB{"Is process stable and measurable?"}
    nodeB --> nodeY["Yes"]

```

```mermaid

    nodeA["Identify Quality Costs in PAF Format"] --> nodeB["Collect Data from Finance and Quality Departments"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Categorise into CP CA CIF CEF"]

    nodeD --> nodeE["Compare with Benchmarks and Prior Period"]
    nodeE --> nodeF{"Is QCI greater than 40 percent?"}
    nodeF --> nodeY["Yes Shift to Prevention"]
    nodeF --> nodeN["No Maintain and Monitor"]
    nodeY --> nodeG["Invest in Training SPC and Supplier Development"]

    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeA
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Cost of Quality. List its four categories. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (Concise, 3-mark style):**

Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total financial expenditure incurred by an organization on the prevention, detection, and correction of defects, together with the losses arising from defective products reaching the customer.

The four categories under the **PAF Model** are:

1. **Prevention Costs ($C_P$):** Costs of activities to *prevent* defects (e.g., training, quality planning).
2. **Appraisal Costs ($C_A$):** Costs of *detecting* defects (e.g., inspection, testing).
3. **Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$):** Costs of defects found *before* shipment (e.g., scrap, rework).
4. **External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$):** Costs of defects found *after* shipment (e.g., warranty, returns).

### Q2. What is COPQ? Why is it considered the most actionable quality metric? `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality) is the sum of Internal Failure Costs and External Failure Costs:

$$
C_{COPQ} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

It represents the financial *damage* caused by poor quality. It is considered the most actionable metric because:
- It isolates the **waste** component that is *directly reducible* through process improvement.
- It is the single most powerful lever for **justifying** quality investments to top management.
- A reduction in COPQ translates **directly into profit**, making it a universally understood business KPI.
- It is highly responsive to Six-Sigma, Lean, and Kaizen interventions.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** *(1) Do not present a "definition-only" answer for 14 marks; you must include numerical analysis, interpretation, and recommendation. (2) Always state the units of every computed value. (3) For derivation questions, skipping the second-derivative test costs you 1–2 marks even if the first-order optimum is correctly derived.*

### Question A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

A precision components manufacturing firm recorded the following quality costs for the year 2023–24:

| Category | Cost (₹ in Lakhs) |
|----------|------------------|
| Quality planning and training | 1.8 |
| Supplier audits and certification | 0.7 |
| Inspection of incoming materials | 1.2 |
| In-process inspection | 1.5 |
| Final product testing | 0.8 |
| Scrap and rework | 3.4 |
| Re-inspection of reworked items | 0.6 |
| Warranty claims and field service | 4.2 |
| Returned materials handling | 1.1 |
| Customer complaint resolution | 0.9 |
| **Net annual sales** | **60.0** |
| **Units produced** | **3 000** |

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**Classify the above costs into the four PAF categories. Hence compute the Total Cost of Quality and its percentage of sales. Comment on the cost structure.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Classification into PAF categories:**

| Original Item | Amount (₹ L) | Category |
|---------------|--------------|----------|
| Quality planning & training | 1.8 | $C_P$ |
| Supplier audits | 0.7 | $C_P$ |
| Incoming-material inspection | 1.2 | $C_A$ |
| In-process inspection | 1.5 | $C_A$ |
| Final product testing | 0.8 | $C_A$ |
| Scrap & rework | 3.4 | $C_{IF}$ |
| Re-inspection of rework | 0.6 | $C_{IF}$ |
| Warranty & field service | 4.2 | $C_{EF}$ |
| Returned materials | 1.1 | $C_{EF}$ |
| Complaint resolution | 0.9 | $C_{EF}$ |

**Step 2 – Subtotals:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= 1.8 + 0.7 = 2.5 \;\;\text{lakhs} \\
C_A &= 1.2 + 1.5 + 0.8 = 3.5 \;\;\text{lakhs} \\
C_{IF} &= 3.4 + 0.6 = 4.0 \;\;\text{lakhs} \\
C_{EF} &= 4.2 + 1.1 + 0.9 = 6.2 \;\;\text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Total Quality Cost:**

$$
C_{TQ} = 2.5 + 3.5 + 4.0 + 6.2 = 16.2 \;\;\text{lakhs}
$$

**Step 4 – COQ as % of Sales:**

$$
\%COQ = \dfrac{16.2}{60.0} \times 100 = 27\%
$$

**Step 5 – Quality Cost Index:**

$$
QCI = \dfrac{C_{IF} + C_{EF}}{C_{TQ}} = \dfrac{4.0 + 6.2}{16.2} = \dfrac{10.2}{16.2} = 0.630 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 63\%
$$

**Comments on the Cost Structure:**

- The QCI of **63 %** is far above the world-class benchmark of **< 40 %**, indicating a **failure-dominated** cost structure.
- Prevention spending (15.4 % of CTQ) is below the recommended ≥ 30 % world-class target.
- External failure costs (38.3 % of CTQ) are alarmingly high → the firm is *paying* for poor quality in the field.
- The total COQ at **27 % of sales** is unsustainable; industry leaders operate at 2.5–6 % of sales.

**Recommendation:** Increase $C_P$ (training, SPC, supplier development) by ~50 %, which will reduce $C_{IF}$ and $C_{EF}$ by an estimated 30–40 %, lowering CTQ to ~11–12 lakhs (≈ 18–20 % of sales) within one year.

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**Define COPQ. Compute the COPQ for the above firm. If the firm implements a Six-Sigma project that is expected to reduce external failure costs by 60 % and internal failure costs by 40 %, calculate the new CTQ and the annual savings. Comment on the strategic value of this intervention.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Definition of COPQ:**

COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality) is the total of all costs arising from poor quality – namely Internal Failure Costs plus External Failure Costs:

$$
C_{COPQ} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

**Step 2 – Current COPQ:**

$$
C_{COPQ} = 4.0 + 6.2 = 10.2 \;\;\text{lakhs}
$$

**Step 3 – Post-Intervention Costs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{IF}^{new} &= 4.0 \times (1 - 0.40) = 4.0 \times 0.60 = 2.4 \;\;\text{lakhs} \\
C_{EF}^{new} &= 6.2 \times (1 - 0.60) = 6.2 \times 0.40 = 2.48 \;\;\text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming $C_P$ and $C_A$ remain constant at 2.5 and 3.5 lakhs (since the project is funded out of savings):

**Step 4 – New Total Quality Cost:**

$$
C_{TQ}^{new} = 2.5 + 3.5 + 2.4 + 2.48 = 10.88 \;\;\text{lakhs}
$$

**Step 5 – Annual Savings:**

$$
\text{Savings} = 16.20 - 10.88 = 5.32 \;\;\text{lakhs per year}
$$

**Step 6 – New CTQ as % of Sales:**

$$
\%COQ^{new} = \dfrac{10.88}{60.0} \times 100 = 18.13\%
$$

**Strategic Value:**

- The intervention reduces CTQ by **~33 %** in one year.
- QCI drops from 63 % to $(2.4+2.48)/10.88 = 44.9\%$, shifting the firm closer to a *balanced* quality cost structure.
- The hidden savings (goodwill, market share, liability avoidance) are estimated at 4× the visible $C_{EF}$ reduction ≈ ₹ 9.92 lakhs.
- **Conclusion:** A Six-Sigma project is a *high-ROI strategic investment* that pays for itself within the first quarter of implementation.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**Derive the optimum level of prevention cost that minimises the total quality cost, using the standard PAF model. State clearly the economic interpretation of the first-order condition.**

**Model Solution:**

Refer to Section 3.1 above. The full derivation is reproduced here in the *answer-script format* expected by KTU examiners:

**Model Setup:**
Assume the empirical cost functions:
$$
C_A = a_0 + a_1 C_P, \quad C_{IF} = b_0 e^{-b_1 C_P}, \quad C_{EF} = c_0 e^{-c_1 C_P}
$$
where all coefficients are positive.

**Total Cost Function:**
$$
C_{TQ}(C_P) = C_P + a_0 + a_1 C_P + b_0 e^{-b_1 C_P} + c_0 e^{-c_1 C_P}
$$
$$
C_{TQ}(C_P) = (1 + a_1) C_P + a_0 + b_0 e^{-b_1 C_P} + c_0 e^{-c_1 C_P}
$$

**First-Order Condition (FOC):**

$$
\dfrac{d\,C_{TQ}}{d\,C_P} = (1 + a_1) - b_0 b_1 e^{-b_1 C_P^*} - c_0 c_1 e^{-c_1 C_P^*} = 0
$$

**Solving for the Optimum:**
$$
b_0 b_1 e^{-b_1 C_P^*} + c_0 c_1 e^{-c_1 C_P^*} = 1 + a_1
$$

This implicit equation is solved numerically for $C_P^*$.

**Economic Interpretation:**

At $C_P^*$, the **marginal cost of one additional rupee of prevention** exactly equals the **marginal saving** in internal and external failure costs:

$$
\underbrace{(1 + a_1)}_{\text{MC of Prevention}} = \underbrace{\left( -\dfrac{d C_{IF}}{d C_P} \right) + \left( -\dfrac{d C_{EF}}{d C_P} \right)}_{\text{MS from Reduced Failures at } C_P^*}
$$

* If $C_P < C_P^*$: the firm is **under-investing** in prevention → increasing $C_P$ by ₹1 saves more than ₹1 in failures → $C_{TQ}$ falls.
* If $C_P > C_P^*$: the firm is **over-investing** in prevention → the last rupee of prevention saves less than ₹1 in failures → $C_{TQ}$ rises.
* At $C_P = C_P^*$: the firm is at the **minimum-cost operating point**.

**Second-Order Sufficient Condition:**
$$
\dfrac{d^2 C_{TQ}}{d C_P^2} = b_0 b_1^2 e^{-b_1 C_P^*} + c_0 c_1^2 e^{-c_1 C_P^*} > 0
$$

Hence $C_P^*$ is a confirmed **global minimum**.

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**The quality department of a brake-pad manufacturer observes that 25 % of all products are reworked. Each reworked unit costs ₹180 in labour and material, while selling at ₹ 2 500. After a process improvement, the rework rate drops to 10 %. The improvement costs ₹ 5 00 000 in training and tooling. Calculate the annual savings if the company produces 1 00 000 brake pads per year. Should the improvement be implemented?**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Annual production | $N$ | 1 00 000 units |
| Initial rework rate | $r_1$ | 25 % |
| New rework rate | $r_2$ | 10 % |
| Cost per reworked unit | $C_{rework}$ | ₹ 180 |
| Selling price per unit | $P$ | ₹ 2 500 |
| Process improvement cost | $C_{PI}$ | ₹ 5 00 000 |

**Step 1 – Annual Number of Reworked Units (Before):**

$$
N_{1} = N \times r_1 = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = 25{,}000 \;\;\text{units}
$$

**Step 2 – Annual Number of Reworked Units (After):**

$$
N_{2} = N \times r_2 = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = 10{,}000 \;\;\text{units}
$$

**Step 3 – Reduction in Reworked Units:**

$$
\Delta N = N_{1} - N_{2} = 25{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 15{,}000 \;\;\text{units}
$$

**Step 4 – Direct Annual Savings in Internal Failure Cost:**

$$
\text{Savings}_{direct} = \Delta N \times C_{rework} = 15{,}000 \times 180 = ₹27{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5 – Net Annual Savings (after Improvement Cost):**

$$
\text{Net Savings} = \text{Savings}_{direct} - C_{PI} = 27{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹22{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 6 – Payback Period:**

$$
\text{Payback} = \dfrac{C_{PI}}{\text{Savings}_{direct}} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{27{,}00{,}000} \times 12 \;\;\text{months} \approx 2.22 \;\;\text{months}
$$

**Step 7 – ROI on Quality Investment:**

$$
ROI_Q = \dfrac{22{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 440\%
$$

**Decision & Justification:**

> **YES – the improvement should be implemented.** The investment yields a net annual saving of **₹ 22 lakhs** with a payback period of **~2.2 months** and an ROI of **440 %**. The hidden savings (reduced warranty claims, lower scrap disposal, improved customer goodwill, higher line throughput) further strengthen the business case. From a COQ perspective, the investment moves the firm from a reactive (failure-dominated) cost structure toward a proactive (prevention-dominated) one, satisfying the **first-order optimum condition** of cost minimisation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Preparation**

- [ ] **COQ** = the *sum* of all quality-related costs, **not** the cost of high-quality products.
- [ ] The **four PAF categories** are: Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure.
- [ ] **Master Equation:** $C_{TQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$.
- [ ] **COPQ** = $C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ (the waste component).
- [ ] **Conformance Cost** = $C_P + C_A$ (the investment component).
- [ ] Prevention is the **only** category that *decreases* total cost when increased (up to a point).
- [ ] The **optimum** $C_P^*$ occurs where $\dfrac{dC_{TQ}}{dC_P} = 0$, equivalently MC of prevention = MS from reduced failures.
- [ ] **Second-order test** ($\dfrac{d^2 C_{TQ}}{dC_P^2} > 0$) confirms the minimum.
- [ ] World-class target ratio: $C_P : C_A : C_{IF} : C_{EF} = 30 : 25 : 20 : 25$.
- [ ] QCI $< 40\%$ = healthy; QCI $> 50\%$ = failure-dominated and urgent intervention needed.
- [ ] COQ as % of sales: world-class is **2.5–6 %**; > 20 % is a red flag.
- [ ] **BS 6143 Part 1** = Process Cost Model; **BS 6143 Part 2** = PAF Quality Cost Model.
- [ ] Hidden / indirect costs ≈ 4 × visible external failure cost (industry heuristic).
- [ ] In numerical answers, **always carry units** (₹ lakhs, %, units/year).
- [ ] Definition-only answers are insufficient for 14-mark questions; **always include classification, calculation, interpretation, and recommendation**.
- [ ] For derivation questions, the **first-order + second-order condition** is mandatory; skipping the SOC test costs marks.
- [ ] Justifying quality projects in monetary terms to management requires computing **net savings** (gross savings − project cost) and **payback period**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seven QC Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET611 - INDUSTRIAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Seven QC Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Seven Quality Control (QC) Tools** are a standardized set of basic statistical and graphical techniques developed primarily by **Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa** (Japan, 1968) to assist quality professionals in identifying, analyzing, and solving quality-related problems at the shop-floor and process level.

The official Seven QC Tools as recognized by KTU and universally adopted in ISO 9001-based Quality Management Systems are:

| # | Tool | Primary Function |
|---|------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** | Identifies root causes of a problem |
| 2 | **Check Sheet** | Systematic data collection |
| 3 | **Control Chart** | Process monitoring over time |
| 4 | **Histogram** | Frequency distribution visualization |
| 5 | **Pareto Chart** | Prioritization of problems (80/20 rule) |
| 6 | **Scatter Diagram** | Relationship between two variables |
| 7 | **Flowchart** | Process mapping and standardization |

> [!NOTE]
> **About 90% of quality problems in an organization can be solved using these seven tools** — a statistic widely cited by JUSE (Japanese Union of Scientists and Engineers).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Doctor's Toolkit" for a Factory**
> Think of the Seven QC Tools as the **stethoscope, thermometer, blood-test report, and X-ray** of a factory floor. Just like a doctor cannot diagnose a patient using only one instrument, a quality engineer cannot solve problems using a single tool. Each of the seven tools serves a distinct diagnostic purpose:
> - **Check Sheet** = Stethoscope (collects raw symptoms)
> - **Histogram** = Blood report (shows the shape of the data)
> - **Pareto Chart** = Diagnosis report (says which problem is most critical)
> - **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** = Brainstorming with specialists (finds the disease cause)
> - **Scatter Diagram** = X-ray (reveals relationships between factors)
> - **Control Chart** = Heart-rate monitor (tracks if the patient is stable)
> - **Flowchart** = Treatment plan (standardizes the cure)

These seven tools are so fundamental that KTU examiners often dedicate an entire 14-mark question to their comparative study and application.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Tool

#### 2.1.1 Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa / Fishbone Diagram)
- **Purpose:** Identifies potential root causes of a quality problem.
- **Structure:** A horizontal "spine" (the effect/problem statement) with diagonal "bones" representing major cause categories.
- **Standard 6M Categories (Manufacturing):**
  - **Man** (Workforce)
  - **Machine** (Equipment)
  - **Method** (Process)
  - **Material** (Raw inputs)
  - **Measurement** (Inspection systems)
  - **Mother Nature / Environment**
- **Why it works:** Forces structured brainstorming so that team members consider causes across all functional dimensions, avoiding tunnel vision.

#### 2.1.2 Check Sheet
- **Purpose:** A simple, structured form for **recording data** as it is generated, making data collection easy and consistent.
- **Design:** Tally marks or count cells arranged so that patterns emerge naturally.
- **Application:** Defect tracking, frequency of machine breakdowns, supplier defect counts, etc.

#### 2.1.3 Control Chart (Statistical Process Control - SPC)
- **Purpose:** Distinguishes **common cause variation** (inherent to process) from **special cause variation** (due to external disturbances).
- **Structure:** Time-series plot with three horizontal lines — UCL, CL, LCL.

$$UCL = \bar{X} + 3\sigma$$
$$CL = \bar{X}$$
$$LCL = \bar{X} - 3\sigma$$

- **The 3σ rule:** Based on the empirical observation that under a Normal Distribution, **99.73%** of data falls within ±3 standard deviations.

#### 2.1.4 Histogram
- **Purpose:** Displays the **frequency distribution** of a continuous variable by dividing data into bins (intervals).
- **Reveals:** Central tendency, spread, skewness, modality of the process.
- **Relationship to Control Chart:** A histogram shows data at a single point in time, while a control chart shows variation over time.

#### 2.1.5 Pareto Chart
- **Purpose:** Separates the "vital few" causes from the "trivial many."
- **The 80/20 Rule (Pareto Principle):** Roughly **80% of problems** are caused by **20% of causes**.
- **Construction:** A bar chart (descending) with a cumulative percentage curve overlaid.

#### 2.1.6 Scatter Diagram
- **Purpose:** Plots paired data $(x_i, y_i)$ to reveal the **correlation** between two variables.
- **Types of correlation:**
  - **Positive correlation** — both variables increase together
  - **Negative correlation** — one increases, the other decreases
  - **No correlation** — scattered randomly

#### 2.1.7 Flowchart
- **Purpose:** Represents the sequence of steps in a process using standard symbols:
  - **Oval** — Start/End
  - **Rectangle** — Process step
  - **Diamond** — Decision
  - **Parallelogram** — Input/Output
  - **Arrow** — Flow direction
- **Application:** Standardizes procedures, identifies non-value-adding steps, supports ISO documentation.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Tool | Key Formula / Metric | Boundary / Standard Value | Application |
|------|---------------------|---------------------------|-------------|
| Control Chart (X-bar) | $UCL = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $A_2$ varies with $n$ | Variable data |
| Control Chart (R-chart) | $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_4 \vert D_3$ from table | Subgroup range |
| Control Chart (p-chart) | $UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $n$ = sample size | Defective items |
| Control Chart (c-chart) | $UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c}$ = avg defects/unit | Defects per unit |
| Histogram | $\text{Number of bins} \approx \sqrt{N}$ | Sturges' Rule: $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10} N$ | Binning data |
| Pareto | Cumulative % = $\sum \frac{f_i}{\sum f} \times 100$ | 80% threshold | Prioritization |
| Scatter | $r = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2 \sum(y_i-\bar{y})^2}}$ | $\vert r \vert \le 1$ | Correlation |
| Normal Distribution | $99.73\%$ within $\pm 3\sigma$ | $68.27\%$ within $\pm 1\sigma$ | Control limits |
| Shewhart Rule | Out-of-control if 1 point $> 3\sigma$ | Western Electric Rules | SPC interpretation |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application Snapshot:**
> - **Automotive (Toyota):** Used the seven tools so extensively that every shop-floor worker (gemba) is trained to interpret them.
> - **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** Control charts monitor wafer defect rates at nanometer tolerances.
> - **Software Engineering:** Adapted into "Root Cause Analysis" using Fishbone diagrams during post-mortems.
> - **Healthcare:** Used for clinical pathway standardization and infection-rate reduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of 3-Sigma Control Limits

The 3-sigma control limit arises from the **Empirical Rule** of the Normal Distribution. Below is the full mathematical derivation.

**Step 1: State the Normal Probability Density Function**

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2: Standardize using Z-transformation**

$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

This converts any normal distribution into the Standard Normal Distribution $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$.

**Step 3: Probability within ±kσ limits**

$$P(\mu - k\sigma \le X \le \mu + k\sigma) = \int_{\mu - k\sigma}^{\mu + k\sigma} f(x)\, dx = P(\vert Z \vert \le k)$$

**Step 4: Evaluate numerically for $k = 1, 2, 3$**

Using standard normal tables (or error function tables):

$$P(\vert Z \vert \le 1) \approx 0.6827 = 68.27\%$$
$$P(\vert Z \vert \le 2) \approx 0.9545 = 95.45\%$$
$$P(\vert Z \vert \le 3) \approx 0.9973 = 99.73\%$$

**Step 5: Apply to Control Chart**

For a process mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$:

$$UCL = \mu + 3\sigma$$
$$CL = \mu$$
$$LCL = \mu - 3\sigma$$

Hence, approximately **0.27%** of points (1 in 370) fall outside these limits by chance alone — a rare event used as the **out-of-control signal**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Correlation Coefficient (r)

**Step 1: Given paired observations $(x_1,y_1), (x_2,y_2), \ldots, (x_n,y_n)$**

Compute sample means:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$
$$\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$$

**Step 2: Covariance numerator**

$$\text{Cov}(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$$

**Step 3: Variance denominators**

$$S_{xx} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$$
$$S_{yy} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2$$

**Step 4: Pearson Correlation Coefficient**

$$r = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{S_{xx} \cdot S_{yy}}}$$

The bounds are $-1 \le r \le 1$.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for KTU Answers)

| Criterion | Histogram | Pareto Chart | Control Chart | Scatter Diagram |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Data Type** | Continuous | Categorical | Continuous over time | Paired (X, Y) |
| **Time Component** | No | No | Yes (essential) | No |
| **Statistical Basis** | Frequency distribution | 80/20 principle | 3σ SPC | Correlation r |
| **Output Type** | Distribution shape | Ranking + Cumulative | Stability signal | Strength of relationship |
| **Best Used For** | Capability assessment | Prioritization | Monitoring | Causal hypothesis |
| **Typical Use Stage** | Analyze | Identify | Control | Investigate |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Construction of a Pareto Chart

1. **List categories** of problems (e.g., Defect types: Scratch, Dent, Color, Dimension, Other).
2. **Count frequency** $f_i$ for each category.
3. **Rank** in descending order of frequency.
4. **Compute totals:** $\sum f_i$ (Total defects).
5. **Calculate cumulative percentage:** $C_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j}{\sum f} \times 100$.
6. **Plot dual axis:**
   - Left axis: Bars (frequency).
   - Right axis: Line (cumulative %).
7. **Identify "vital few"** — categories contributing to ~80% of problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — How the Seven Tools Work Together

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Problem Identified"] --> B["Step 1: Check Sheet\nData Collection"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Histogram\nUnderstand Distribution"]
    B --> D["Step 3: Pareto Chart\nPrioritize Issues"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Cause Effect Diagram\nFind Root Causes"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Scatter Diagram\nVerify Cause Effect Link"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Flowchart\nStandardize Solution"]
    G --> H["Step 7: Control Chart\nMonitor Process Stability"]
    H --> I{"Out of Control?"}
    I -->|Yes| J["Trigger Corrective Action"]
    I -->|No| K["Process Stable Continue Production"]
    J --> B
```

### 4.2 Cause-and-Effect Diagram Structure (Ishikawa 6M)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROBLEM["EFFECT / PROBLEM\n(Surface Defect)"] --> M1["MAN\n(Operator Skill)"]
    PROBLEM --> M2["MACHINE\n(Equipment)"]
    PROBLEM --> M3["METHOD\n(Process)"]
    PROBLEM --> M4["MATERIAL\n(Raw Input)"]
    PROBLEM --> M5["MEASUREMENT\n(Inspection)"]
    PROBLEM --> M6["MOTHER NATURE\n(Environment)"]
    M1 --> M1a["Training"]
    M1 --> M1b["Fatigue"]
    M2 --> M2a["Wear"]
    M2 --> M2b["Calibration"]
    M3 --> M3a["Parameters"]
    M3 --> M3b["SOP"]
    M4 --> M4a["Quality"]
    M4 --> M4b["Supplier"]
    M5 --> M5a["Gage R R"]
    M5 --> M5b["Method"]
    M6 --> M6a["Temp"]
    M6 --> M6b["Humidity"]
```

### 4.3 Control Chart Schematic (X-bar with 3σ Limits)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CC["CONTROL CHART ARCHITECTURE"]
        UCL["UCL = Mean + 3 sigma\nUpper Control Limit"]
        CL["CL = Mean\nCenter Line (Process Average)"]
        LCL["LCL = Mean - 3 sigma\nLower Control Limit"]
        UCL --- CL
        CL --- LCL
        CL --> P1["Sample 1"]
        CL --> P2["Sample 2"]
        CL --> P3["Sample 3"]
        CL --> P4["Sample n"]
    end
    ZONE["ZONES\nZone A: 2 sigma to 3 sigma\nZone B: 1 sigma to 2 sigma\nZone C: 0 sigma to 1 sigma"] -.-> CC
```

### 4.4 Tool Selection Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"What do you need to do?"}
    Q1 -->|Collect raw data| T2["Check Sheet"]
    Q1 -->|See data shape| T4["Histogram"]
    Q1 -->|Find top problems| T5["Pareto Chart"]
    Q1 -->|Find root causes| T1["Cause Effect Diagram"]
    Q1 -->|Check two factors| T6["Scatter Diagram"]
    Q1 -->|Map a process| T7["Flowchart"]
    Q1 -->|Monitor over time| T3["Control Chart"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define the Seven QC Tools. State the primary purpose of any three.**

**Model Answer:**
The Seven QC Tools are a set of basic statistical techniques developed by **Kaoru Ishikawa** (1968) to solve **90% of quality problems** at the workplace level. The seven tools are: Cause-and-Effect Diagram, Check Sheet, Control Chart, Histogram, Pareto Chart, Scatter Diagram, and Flowchart.

Primary purpose of three tools:
1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram:** Identifies and organizes potential root causes of a problem across categories like Man, Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, and Environment.
2. **Pareto Chart:** Applies the 80/20 rule to separate the "vital few" causes from the "trivial many," enabling prioritization of corrective action.
3. **Control Chart:** Monitors process stability over time using statistical control limits (UCL, CL, LCL) to distinguish common-cause from special-cause variation.

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Explain the Pareto Principle. How is a Pareto Chart constructed?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**, named after economist Vilfredo Pareto, states that **roughly 80% of the effects come from 20% of the causes**. In quality control, this means a small number of defect types account for the majority of total defects.

**Construction Steps:**
1. List defect categories and their frequencies $f_i$.
2. Rank categories in descending order of frequency.
3. Calculate the cumulative percentage $C_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j}{\sum f} \times 100$.
4. Plot bars (frequency) on the left Y-axis in descending order.
5. Overlay a line graph (cumulative %) on the right Y-axis.
6. Identify the "vital few" categories that account for ~80% of problems.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Ishikawa Fishbone). With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **6M categories** used in manufacturing industries. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing process has the following defect data collected over 5 weeks. Construct a **Pareto Chart** and identify the "vital few" categories. **(7 marks)**

| Defect Type | Frequency |
|-------------|-----------|
| Scratch | 42 |
| Dent | 18 |
| Color Mismatch | 10 |
| Dimension Error | 25 |
| Surface Crack | 5 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cause-and-Effect Diagram (7 marks):**
The Ishikawa Fishbone Diagram is a structured brainstorming tool used to identify the potential root causes of a quality problem. It resembles a fish skeleton:
- The **head (right side)** represents the **problem/effect** (e.g., "Surface Defect").
- The **spine (horizontal line)** connects the effect to the cause categories.
- The **diagonal bones** represent major cause categories.

**6M Categories:**
1. **Man** — Workforce-related causes (skill, training, fatigue, supervision).
2. **Machine** — Equipment issues (wear, calibration, maintenance).
3. **Method** — Process issues (SOPs, parameters, work instructions).
4. **Material** — Input quality (raw material, supplier, storage).
5. **Measurement** — Inspection system (calibration, Gage R&R, method).
6. **Mother Nature** — Environment (temperature, humidity, dust, lighting).

**(b) Pareto Chart Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Rank defects in descending order:**

| Rank | Defect Type | Frequency $f_i$ | Cumulative Frequency | Cumulative % |
|------|-------------|-----------------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Scratch | 42 | 42 | 42.0% |
| 2 | Dimension Error | 25 | 67 | 67.0% |
| 3 | Dent | 18 | 85 | 85.0% |
| 4 | Color Mismatch | 10 | 95 | 95.0% |
| 5 | Surface Crack | 5 | 100 | 100.0% |

**Step 2: Total defects**

$$\sum f = 42 + 25 + 18 + 10 + 5 = 100$$

**Step 3: Identify Vital Few**

The first **3 categories** (Scratch, Dimension Error, Dent) account for **85%** of total defects. These are the **"vital few"** that should be addressed first for maximum impact.

**Step 4: Conclusion**
Quality improvement efforts should focus on eliminating **Scratch, Dimension Error, and Dent defects** to achieve the largest reduction in overall defect rate.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** with the help of a **Control Chart**. Define UCL, CL, and LCL and explain the role of the **3-sigma limits**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine produces shafts with a mean diameter of $\bar{X} = 25.00$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.05$ mm. Calculate the **UCL, CL, and LCL** for the X-chart. If a sample mean of 25.20 mm is observed, comment on the process state. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SPC and Control Chart (7 marks):**
**Statistical Process Control (SPC)** is the application of statistical methods to monitor and control a process, ensuring it operates at its full potential capability. A **Control Chart** is the primary SPC tool.

**Structure:**
- **Center Line (CL):** Represents the process mean $\bar{\bar{X}}$ (average of sample means).
- **Upper Control Limit (UCL):** $\bar{\bar{X}} + 3\sigma$ — the upper boundary of acceptable variation.
- **Lower Control Limit (LCL):** $\bar{\bar{X}} - 3\sigma$ — the lower boundary.

**Role of 3σ limits:**
Under a normal distribution, **99.73%** of process output falls within $\pm 3\sigma$ of the mean. Hence, only **0.27%** (1 in 370 points) falls outside by chance. Any point outside the limits indicates **special cause variation**, signaling that the process is **out of statistical control**.

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\bar{X} = 25.00$ mm, $\sigma = 0.05$ mm, Observed $\bar{x} = 25.20$ mm

**Step 1: Calculate control limits**

$$CL = \bar{X} = 25.00 \text{ mm}$$

$$UCL = \bar{X} + 3\sigma = 25.00 + 3(0.05) = 25.15 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL = \bar{X} - 3\sigma = 25.00 - 3(0.05) = 24.85 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compare observed value**

Observed $\bar{x} = 25.20$ mm.

Check against UCL: $25.20 > 25.15$ → The observed mean is **above the UCL**.

**Step 3: Conclusion**

Since $25.20$ mm exceeds the UCL of $25.15$ mm, the process is **out of statistical control**. There is a **special cause variation** present, and **immediate corrective action** must be initiated to identify and eliminate the assignable cause.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students confuse **"Control Limits"** (statistical, derived from data) with **"Specification Limits"** (USL/LSL, customer-defined). Control limits reflect *process capability*, while specifications reflect *design intent*.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In Pareto charts, students forget to **rank in descending order** before plotting — a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** For 3σ limits, students write $2\sigma$ instead of $3\sigma$, missing the fundamental SPC premise.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** In Cause-and-Effect diagrams, students list only 4M instead of 6M categories, losing completeness marks.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Pareto chart answers often skip the cumulative percentage calculation — always show $C_i = (\text{cumulative} \div \text{total}) \times 100$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **Originator:** Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa, 1968, Japan (JUSE). Coverage: ~90% of shop-floor quality issues.
> - **Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa):** Fishbone; 6M categories (Man, Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, Mother Nature). Used to find root causes.
> - **Check Sheet:** Custom-designed form for systematic data tallying.
> - **Histogram:** Visualizes distribution shape; uses Sturges' rule $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10} N$ for binning.
> - **Pareto Chart:** 80/20 rule; bars + cumulative line; rank before plotting.
> - **Scatter Diagram:** Correlation coefficient $r$, bounded by $-1 \le r \le 1$.
> - **Control Chart:** UCL/CL/LCL based on $\mu \pm 3\sigma$; 99.73% within limits under normality.
> - **Flowchart:** Process mapping; standard symbols (oval, rectangle, diamond, parallelogram).
> - **Remember:** Control limits ≠ Specification limits.
> - **Remember:** Tools work in **sequence** — Identify → Analyze → Prioritize → Investigate → Standardize → Monitor.
> - **Remember:** KTU frequently asks comparative study between two tools (e.g., Histogram vs Control Chart).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Historical Perspective of Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEPET611 - INDUSTRIAL QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Historical Perspective of Quality Control ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quality Control (QC)** is the operational technique and systematic set of activities used to fulfill the **requirements for quality** by monitoring processes, identifying defects, and ensuring that products or services conform to predefined specifications, standards, and customer expectations. Historically, it represents the evolution of human craftsmanship from individual artisan inspection to statistically-driven, organization-wide quality management systems. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context for Industrial Quality Control, the *Historical Perspective* establishes the foundational timeline, philosophical schools of thought, and management paradigms that have shaped modern quality engineering. Understanding this evolution is mandatory because every contemporary tool (Six Sigma, TQM, ISO 9000) is a direct descendant of ideas forged during the 20th century Quality Revolution. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Imagine a small traditional Kerala restaurant in the 1900s. The *chef personally tastes every dish* before it leaves the kitchen — this is **Inspection-Based Quality**, equivalent to the craftsmanship era. As the restaurant grows into a chain, the chef cannot taste every dish, so he writes down a **recipe (standard)**, trains cooks, and occasionally checks samples — this is **Quality Control**. Eventually, the chain monitors customer complaints, supplier freshness, and cook performance metrics — this is **Total Quality Management (TQM)**. The journey from one chef tasting food to a global data-driven system is exactly the *Historical Perspective of Quality Control*. ### 1.3 The Four Philosophical Eras of Quality > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition — Quality Eras:** > 1. **Inspection Era (Pre-1900 – 1930):** Quality ensured by visual/physical inspection of finished goods. > 2. **Quality Control Era (1930 – 1950):** Introduction of **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** by Walter Shewhart. > 3. **Quality Assurance Era (1950 – 1980):** System-wide prevention, *Quality is built in, not inspected in.* > 4. **Strategic Quality Management Era (1980 – Present):** TQM, Six Sigma, Lean, ISO 9000 family. ### 1.4 Key Terminology (Bold Standard Metrics) - **Defect (D):** Any non-conformance to specification. - **Conformance to Specifications (C):** Degree to which a product meets design parameters. - **Fitness for Use (FFU):** Juran's customer-centric definition of quality. - **Cost of Quality (COQ):** Sum of prevention, appraisal, and failure costs. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Phase 1 — The Craftsmanship Era (Before 1900) In pre-industrial societies, a single **artisan** manufactured the entire product (e.g., a swordsmith, a tailor). Quality was guaranteed by: - Personal pride of the craftsman. - Direct customer-artisan relationship. - Apprenticeship-based skill transfer. **Why this failed at scale:** The Industrial Revolution introduced interchangeable parts and mass production, destroying the artisan's ownership of the complete product. Quality could no longer rely on a single person's skill. ### 2.2 Phase 2 — The Inspection Era (1900 – 1930) **Frederick Winslow Taylor (1911)** introduced *Scientific Management* and the concept of the "Inspection Function" as a separate department. Workers produced; inspectors sorted good from bad. - **Logic Step 1:** Separate production from quality verification. - **Logic Step 2:** Use gauges, measurement tools, and AQL (Acceptable Quality Level) sampling. - **Logic Step 3:** Defective products are either reworked or scrapped. **Limitation:** This is a *reactive* approach — defects are detected after they occur, not prevented. Cost of failure is high. ### 2.3 Phase 3 — The Statistical Quality Control Era (1930 – 1950) **Walter A. Shewhart (1924)** at Bell Telephone Laboratories developed the **Control Chart**, marking the birth of modern SQC. He distinguished between: - **Common Cause Variation:** Inherent to the process. - **Special Cause Variation:** Due to external disturbances. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight (Shewhart):** *"Quality is not inspected into a product; it must be built into it by controlling the process."* This single idea transformed quality from a sorting activity to a process-control science. During **World War II (1940s)**, the U.S. War Department mandated the use of Shewhart's control charts, training thousands of engineers in SQC — spreading the methodology globally, including to **Japan** (Deming & Juran lectures, 1950). ### 2.4 Phase 4 — The Quality Assurance Era (1950 – 1980) Three giants reshaped the field: | Guru | Core Contribution | Year | |------|-------------------|------| | **W. Edwards Deming** | 14 Points for Management; PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) / Deming Cycle; System thinking | 1950 (Japan lectures) | | **Joseph M. Juran** | Quality Trilogy (Quality Planning, Control, Improvement); *Fitness for Use* | 1951 (Japan) | | **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Total Quality Control (TQC) — Quality is everyone's responsibility | 1956 | | **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Quality Circles; Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram; Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC) | 1962 | | **Philip B. Crosby** | *Quality is Free*; Zero Defects; Four Absolutes of Quality | 1979 | | **Genichi Taguchi** | Taguchi Loss Function; Robust Design | 1960s–80s | > [!NOTE] > **Deming's 14 Points (Summary):** Create constancy of purpose, adopt the new philosophy, cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality, end price-only purchasing, improve constantly, institute training, institute leadership, drive out fear, break down barriers, eliminate slogans, eliminate numerical quotas, remove barriers to pride of workmanship, institute vigorous education, put everybody to work on transformation. ### 2.5 Phase 5 — Strategic Quality Era (1980 – Present) - **1986:** Motorola develops **Six Sigma** (Bill Smith, Mikel Harry) — targets **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. - **1987:** **ISO 9000** published by International Organization for Standardization — first global quality management standard. - **1990s:** **Total Quality Management (TQM)** becomes a global corporate strategy. - **1990s:** **Lean Manufacturing** (Toyota Production System, Taiichi Ohno) merges with quality. - **2000s:** **Six Sigma + Lean = Lean Six Sigma**. - **2015:** **ISO 9001:2015** — risk-based thinking, process approach, leadership focus. - **Present:** Industry 4.0, AI-driven quality control, predictive analytics. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table | Dimension | Inspection | QC (Statistical) | QA (System) | TQM (Strategic) | |-----------|-----------|------------------|-------------|-----------------| | **Era** | 1900–1930 | 1930–1950 | 1950–1980 | 1980–Present | | **Focus** | Sort good vs. bad | Control process variation | Build quality in | Customer satisfaction | | **Approach** | Reactive | Statistical | Preventive | Strategic | | **Responsibility** | Inspection Dept. | QC Engineers | All departments | Everyone | | **Key Tool** | Gauges, AQL | Control Charts | SOPs, Audits | TQM, Six Sigma, ISO | | **Cost Focus** | Failure cost | Appraisal cost | Prevention cost | Optimization | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility The historical perspective is not merely academic — it dictates **which tool to apply in which industry context**. A garment export unit in Kerala today operates across all four eras simultaneously: hand-inspection (craftsmanship), AQL sampling (inspection), ISO 9001 audits (QA), and Six Sigma projects (TQM). Mastering the timeline enables a quality engineer to diagnose which level of maturity an organization is at. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Historical Cost-of-Quality Evolution ### 3.1 Mathematical Representation of Quality Evolution While history is qualitative, the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** can be quantified as it evolved: $$ \text{Total COQ} = C_{\text{Prevention}} + C_{\text{Appraisal}} + C_{\text{Internal Failure}} + C_{\text{External Failure}} $$ **Evolution of COQ emphasis across eras:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Era 1 (Inspection):} \quad \text{COQ} &\approx 0 \cdot C_{P} + 0 \cdot C_{A} + 0 \cdot C_{IF} + \text{High } C_{EF} \\ \text{Era 2 (SQC):} \quad \text{COQ} &\approx 0 \cdot C_{P} + \text{High } C_{A} + \text{Medium } C_{IF} + \text{Medium } C_{EF} \\ \text{Era 3 (QA):} \quad \text{COQ} &\approx \text{Medium } C_{P} + \text{Medium } C_{A} + \text{Low } C_{IF} + \text{Low } C_{EF} \\ \text{Era 4 (TQM):} \quad \text{COQ} &\approx \text{High } C_{P} + \text{Low } C_{A} + 0 \cdot C_{IF} + 0 \cdot C_{EF} \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $C_{P}$ = Prevention Cost (training, design review) - $C_{A}$ = Appraisal Cost (inspection, testing) - $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure Cost (rework, scrap) - $C_{EF}$ = External Failure Cost (warranty, recalls) **Interpretation Row 1 (Inspection Era):** Zero investment in prevention or appraisal at the process level — defects are sorted out *after* production, leading to massive external failure costs (customer returns, warranty). **Interpretation Row 4 (TQM Era):** Heavy upfront investment in prevention (training, robust design, process capability studies) eliminates downstream failure costs — a Pareto-optimal configuration. ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Six Sigma Maturity Metric Six Sigma represents the strategic culmination of the quality movement.
