# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Explain the **3-sigma control limit philosophy** as adopted by Shewhart for variables control charts.

**Model Answer:**
The 3-sigma control limit philosophy, proposed by Walter A. Shewhart (1924), establishes control limits at **±3 standard deviations** from the process central line. Assuming the quality characteristic follows a normal distribution, this captures **99.73%** of the inherent random (common-cause) variation of the process, leaving only **0.27%** probability of a point falling outside the limits purely by chance. This 0.27% false-alarm rate represents an **economic compromise** — narrow enough to detect real process shifts quickly, yet wide enough to avoid excessive false triggers from routine noise. Hence, if a plotted point falls beyond these limits, the engineer concludes a **special (assignable) cause** is present and initiates corrective action.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Why is it necessary to plot **both** the $\bar{X}$ chart and the $R$ chart together instead of just one?

**Model Answer:**
The two charts monitor **different aspects** of the process and provide complementary information:

1. **$\bar{X}$ chart** detects shifts in the **process mean (central tendency)** — e.g., a tool gradually wearing and producing larger diameters.
2. **$R$ chart** detects changes in **process variability (dispersion)** — e.g., a raw-material batch that increases piece-to-piece inconsistency.

A process can have a perfectly stable average yet highly erratic subgroups (or vice versa). If only the $\bar{X}$ chart is plotted, an increase in variability will go unnoticed, leading to **defective units** even though the mean appears normal. Moreover, the $R$ chart's stability is a **prerequisite** for valid interpretation of the $\bar{X}$ chart — the $\pm 3\sigma$ limits on $\bar{X}$ are calculated using $\bar{R}$, so if $R$ is out of control, the $\bar{X}$ limits themselves are unreliable.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — $\bar{X}$ and $R$ Chart Construction

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

> **(a)** The following data represent the inside diameters (in mm) of **25 samples of size $n = 5$** taken from a bearing manufacturing process. The grand mean is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50.10$ mm and the average range is $\bar{R} = 0.30$ mm. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(i)** Construct the **trial control limits** for the $\bar{X}$ and $R$ charts.
> 
> **(ii)** A particular subgroup has mean $\bar{X}_{20} = 50.45$ mm. Determine whether this point is in statistical control.

**(b)** A drilling operation produces holes with a target diameter of **10.00 ± 0.05 mm**. After process stabilization, $\bar{\bar{X}} = 10.005$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.018$ mm, with subgroup size $n = 4$. Calculate the **process capability indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$** and comment on whether the process is capable. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a)(i) — Trial Control Limits

For $n = 5$, from the standard constants table:
$A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$

**$\bar{X}$ Chart:**

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 50.10 + (0.577)(0.30) = 50.10 + 0.1731 = 50.2731 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} = 50.10 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 50.10 - 0.1731 = 49.9269 \text{ mm}
$$

**$R$ Chart:**

$$
UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(0.30) = 0.6342 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
CL_R = \bar{R} = 0.30 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

#### Part (a)(ii) — Check Subgroup 20

The trial $\bar{X}$ limits are $[49.9269, 50.2731]$ mm.

Compare $\bar{X}_{20} = 50.45$ mm:

$$
50.45 \;>\; UCL_{\bar{X}} = 50.2731
$$

The point **lies outside the upper control limit**. Conclusion: subgroup 20 represents a **special cause** of variation. **Action required:** Investigate root cause (e.g., tool wear, material variation), correct it, and recompute revised control limits after excluding this subgroup from the dataset.

#### Part (b) — Process Capability

For $n = 4$, the constant $d_2 = 2.059$. Estimate process standard deviation:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.018}{2.059} = 0.008742 \text{ mm}
$$

Compute $C_p$:

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{10.05 - 9.95}{6 \times 0.008742} = \frac{0.10}{0.052452} = 1.906
$$

Compute $C_{pk}$:

$$
C_{pk,upper} = \frac{USL - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{10.05 - 10.005}{3 \times 0.008742} = \frac{0.045}{0.026226} = 1.716
$$

$$
C_{pk,lower} = \frac{\bar{\bar{X}} - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{10.005 - 9.95}{0.026226} = \frac{0.055}{0.026226} = 2.097
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.716, \, 2.097) = 1.716
$$

**Comments:**
- $C_p = 1.906 > 1.33 \Rightarrow$ process spread is well within specifications.
- $C_{pk} = 1.716 \approx C_p \Rightarrow$ process is well-centered (mean is near target 10.00 mm).
- The process is **highly capable** — comfortable margin for variation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Individual & Moving Range Chart with Interpretation

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

> **(a)** Define **Moving Range** and explain how the **$I$–$MR$ chart** is constructed when subgroup size is $n = 1$. Mention the formula for the **3-sigma control limits of the Individual chart** using $\overline{MR}$. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A pharmaceutical tablet press records the weight (in mg) of **15 consecutive tablets**. The individual values are:
> 
> 250, 252, 248, 251, 249, 253, 250, 247, 251, 252, 246, 250, 249, 252, 251.
> 
> **(i)** Calculate the **moving ranges** $MR_i$.
> 
> **(ii)** Determine the **trial control limits** for the $I$ and $MR$ charts using $d_2 = 1.128$, $D_4 = 3.267$.
> 
> **(iii)** Comment on process stability. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Definition and Formulae

**Moving Range ($MR$):** The **Moving Range** is the absolute difference between two consecutive individual measurements:

$$
MR_i = \vert X_i - X_{i-1}\vert, \quad i = 2, 3, \dots, k
$$

When subgroup size $n = 1$, no internal-subgroup statistics can be computed. The **Individual ($I$) chart** plots each measurement $X_i$ versus time, and the **Moving Range ($MR$) chart** plots $MR_i$ to estimate short-term variability. For $n = 1$, the moving range of span 2 is used, and its expected value is $E(MR) = d_2 \sigma$ with $d_2 = 1.128$.

**Trial control limits:**

$$
CL_I = \bar{X} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}X_i
$$

$$
UCL_I = \bar{X} + \frac{3\overline{MR}}{d_2}, \qquad LCL_I = \bar{X} - \frac{3\overline{MR}}{d_2}
$$

$$
CL_{MR} = \overline{MR} = \frac{1}{k-1}\sum_{i=2}^{k}MR_i
$$

$$
UCL_{MR} = D_4 \overline{MR}, \qquad LCL_{MR} = D_3 \overline{MR} = 0 \quad (\text{since } D_3 = 0 \text{ for } n = 2)
$$

#### Part (b)(i) — Moving Ranges

Using $MR_i = \vert X_i - X_{i-1}\vert$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MR_2 &= \vert 252 - 250\vert = 2 \\
MR_3 &= \vert 248 - 252\vert = 4 \\
MR_4 &= \vert 251 - 248\vert = 3 \\
MR_5 &= \vert 249 - 251\vert = 2 \\
MR_6 &= \vert 253 - 249\vert = 4 \\
MR_7 &= \vert 250 - 253\vert = 3 \\
MR_8 &= \vert 247 - 250\vert = 3 \\
MR_9 &= \vert 251 - 247\vert = 4 \\
MR_{10} &= \vert 252 - 251\vert = 1 \\
MR_{11} &= \vert 246 - 252\vert = 6 \\
MR_{12} &= \vert 250 - 246\vert = 4 \\
MR_{13} &= \vert 249 - 250\vert = 1 \\
MR_{14} &= \vert 252 - 249\vert = 3 \\
MR_{15} &= \vert 251 - 252\vert = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum: $2+4+3+2+4+3+3+4+1+6+4+1+3+1 = 41$
Number of moving ranges: $k - 1 = 14$

#### Part (b)(ii) — Control Limits

Grand mean of individuals:

$$
\bar{X} = \frac{250+252+248+251+249+253+250+247+251+252+246+250+249+252+251}{15} = \frac{3751}{15} = 250.067 \text{ mg}
$$

Average moving range:

$$
\overline{MR} = \frac{41}{14} = 2.929 \text{ mg}
$$

**Individual Chart Limits** (using $d_2 = 1.128$):

$$
UCL_I = 250.067 + \frac{3 \times 2.929}{1.128} = 250.067 + 7.789 = 257.856 \text{ mg}
$$

$$
LCL_I = 250.067 - 7.789 = 242.278 \text{ mg}
$$

**Moving Range Chart Limits** (using $D_4 = 3.267$, $D_3 = 0$):

$$
UCL_{MR} = (3.267)(2.929) = 9.569 \text{ mg}
$$

$$
LCL_{MR} = 0 \text{ mg}
$$

#### Part (b)(iii) — Stability Assessment

- **Individual chart:** All 15 measurements lie between $242.278$ mg and $257.856$ mg. No point exceeds limits. ✓
- **Moving range chart:** All $MR_i$ values lie between $0$ and $9.569$ mg. Maximum $MR_{11} = 6$ mg is within $UCL_{MR}$. ✓
- **Conclusion:** The tablet-pressing process is **in statistical control** at the 3-sigma level.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to plot the $R$ chart** when only $\bar{X}$ is asked — examiners deduct up to **3 marks** because the two charts must be interpreted jointly.
> 2. **Using wrong constant** ($A_3$ instead of $A_2$ or vice versa). Always check whether the question gives $\bar{R}$ (→ use $A_2, D_3, D_4$) or $\bar{s}$ (→ use $A_3, B_3, B_4$).
> 3. **Not stating units** in the final limits — losing **½ mark** for each unlabeled answer.
> 4. **Forgetting that $LCL_R = 0$** for $n \le 6$. Writing a negative $LCL_R$ is a sign of incomplete understanding.
> 5. **Interpreting every small fluctuation as out-of-control** — apply the *Western Electric / Nelson rules* systematically, not emotionally.
> 6. **Skipping step-3 derivation** in 14-mark questions — the derivation of the constant $A_2 = 3/(d_2\sqrt{n})$ carries **2–3 marks** by itself.
> 7. **Confusing $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$** — $C_p$ measures spread only; $C_{pk}$ also accounts for centering.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core Philosophy:** Shewhart's ±3σ limits — **99.73% in-control probability**, **0.27% false-alarm rate**.
- **Pairs to Remember:** $\bar{X}$–$R$, $\bar{X}$–$s$, $I$–$MR$. Always interpret as a *pair*, never alone.
- **Key Constants (for $n = 5$):** $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$, $d_2 = 2.326$, $d_4 = 0.864$.
- **Subgroup Size Rule of Thumb:** Use $\bar{X}$–$R$ for $2 \le n \le 10$; use $\bar{X}$–$s$ for $n > 10$; use $I$–$MR$ for $n = 1$.
- **Sample Size for Preliminary Study:** $k = 20$ to $25$ subgroups (AIAG recommendation).
- **Order of Interpretation:** $R$ chart FIRST, then $\bar{X}$ chart.
- **Trial vs. Revised Limits:** Always plot trial limits, remove special-cause points, then compute revised limits for production use.
- **Process Capability Benchmarks:** $C_p \ge 1.33$ is acceptable; $C_p \ge 1.67$ is excellent; $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ is the typical industry minimum.
- **Western Electric Rules** identify non-random patterns even when no point crosses 3σ.
- **Assumption:** Underlying distribution must be approximately **normal** (verifiable via histogram, normality test, or probability plot).
- **Cost Implication:** SPC reduces *appraisal* and *failure* costs, supports Six-Sigma and ISO 9001 quality frameworks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Large Sample Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET611 - INDUSTRIAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Large Sample Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Large Sample Theory (Asymptotic Theory)** is the branch of statistical inference that applies the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** to draw conclusions about population parameters when the sample size $n \geq 30$. In **Industrial Quality Control (IQC)**, large sample tests are used to make decisions about process means, proportions, and variabilities of production batches based on sample observations, where the sampling distribution of the test statistic approaches the **Standard Normal Distribution $N(0,1)$**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, **Large Sample Data** refers to statistical hypothesis testing procedures applied when the sample size $n$ is sufficiently large (conventionally $n \geq 30$) such that:

1. The sample mean $\bar{X}$ is approximately normally distributed regardless of the parent population's distribution (by **CLT**).
2. The sample standard deviation $S$ can be used as a reliable estimate of the population standard deviation $\sigma$.
3. The standardized statistic $Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$ follows a **Standard Normal Distribution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course OEPET611 treats Large Sample Tests as the foundation for **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charts (X-bar, R, p, np, c, u charts), acceptance sampling plans, and process capability studies. Every quality decision in manufacturing — from setting control limits to validating supplier claims — is rooted in large sample inference.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Factory Weighing Machine

Imagine a pharmaceutical company producing 10,000 tablets per day. Checking *every* tablet's weight is impossible (this is the **population**, infinite in practice). Instead, the quality inspector pulls out a **sample of 100 tablets** (a "large sample," $n = 100$) and weighs them.

- The inspector wants to know: *"Is the average tablet weight equal to the labeled 500 mg?"*
- The sample mean $\bar{x} = 498$ mg — but is this difference of 2 mg **real** (a process problem) or just **random noise** (normal fluctuation)?

This is precisely where **Large Sample Tests** come in. They provide a mathematical "ruler" (the **Z-statistic**) to measure *how many standard errors* the sample result is from the claimed value. If the Z-value crosses a critical threshold (e.g., 1.96 for 95% confidence), we conclude the process has shifted and corrective action is needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Examples of Large Sample Data Applications:**
> - **Automotive Industry:** Defect rate monitoring across thousands of cars per shift
> - **Electronics:** Solder joint inspection in PCB production lines
> - **Textile:** Tensile strength testing of fabric rolls
> - **Food & Beverage:** Fill-level accuracy of bottles on a high-speed line
> - **Telecommunications:** Call drop rate analysis across millions of subscribers

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Constants

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Population Mean | $\mu$ | True average of the entire production process |
| Sample Mean | $\bar{x}$ | Average of the sampled observations |
| Population Standard Deviation | $\sigma$ | True variability of the process |
| Sample Standard Deviation | $s$ | Variability observed in the sample |
| Population Proportion | $P$ | True fraction of defective/non-conforming items |
| Sample Proportion | $p$ | Fraction of defectives in the sample |
| Significance Level | $\alpha$ | Probability of Type I error (typically **0.05** or 5%) |
| Critical Z-value | $Z_{\alpha/2}$ | Threshold for rejection (1.96 at $\alpha=0.05$) |
| Standard Error | $SE$ | Standard deviation of the sampling distribution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Sacred Constants in Large Sample Testing:**
> - $Z_{0.05} = 1.96$ (for 95% confidence / 5% significance)
> - $Z_{0.01} = 2.58$ (for 99% confidence / 1% significance)
> - $Z_{0.10} = 1.645$ (for 90% confidence / 10% significance)

These values come from the **Standard Normal Table** and form the backbone of every large sample decision in quality control.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The Four Pillars of Large Sample Testing

### Pillar 1: The Central Limit Theorem (CLT)

The **Central Limit Theorem** is the philosophical bedrock of large sample theory. It states:

> For a random sample of size $n$ drawn from a population with finite mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$, the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X}$ approaches a **normal distribution** with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2/n$ as $n \to \infty$.

**Why this matters in IQC:**
- Even if the underlying quality characteristic (e.g., bolt diameter) is **not normally distributed** in the population, the **average** of a large sample behaves normally.
- This universality is what makes large sample tests so powerful and widely applicable in industry.

### Pillar 2: Standard Error Concept

The **Standard Error (SE)** is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution. It quantifies *how much* the sample statistic is expected to vary from sample to sample.

- Small SE → Sample statistic is a precise estimate
- Large SE → Sample statistic is a noisy, unreliable estimate

In manufacturing, reducing SE means either increasing $n$ (more inspection) or reducing $\sigma$ (improving process consistency).

### Pillar 3: Hypothesis Testing Framework

Every large sample test follows the **NHST (Null Hypothesis Significance Testing)** protocol:

1. **State Hypotheses:**
   - $H_0$ (Null Hypothesis): Status quo — process is operating as claimed
   - $H_1$ (Alternative Hypothesis): Process has shifted
2. **Choose Significance Level $\alpha$**
3. **Compute Test Statistic (Z)**
4. **Compare with Critical Value OR compute p-value**
5. **Decision: Reject or Fail to Reject $H_0$**

### Pillar 4: Two Types of Errors

| Error Type | Description | Industrial Consequence |
|------------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Type I Error ($\alpha$)** | Rejecting $H_0$ when it is true | Stopping a good process (lost production) |
| **Type II Error ($\beta$)** | Failing to reject $H_0$ when it is false | Letting defective products reach customer (recall, warranty cost) |

> [!NOTE]
> In IQC, $\alpha$ is often called the **Producer's Risk** (probability of rejecting a good lot), while $\beta$ relates to the **Consumer's Risk** (probability of accepting a bad lot). The complement $(1-\beta)$ is the **Power of the Test**.

## 2.2 Types of Large Sample Tests (KTU Exam-Relevant)

### Test 1: Test for Single Mean

Used when comparing a **sample mean** to a **specified/hypothesized population mean**.

**Use Case in IQC:** Verifying whether a machine is producing components at the specified dimension (e.g., is the average bolt diameter = 10 mm?).

### Test 2: Test for Difference Between Two Means

Used when comparing the means of **two independent large samples**.

**Use Case in IQC:** Comparing the output of two machines, two shifts, two suppliers, or before-and-after a process improvement.

### Test 3: Test for Single Proportion

Used when comparing a **sample proportion** of defectives/non-conforming items to a **specified population proportion**.

**Use Case in IQC:** Checking if the defective rate of a production line equals the historical 2% benchmark.

### Test 4: Test for Difference Between Two Proportions

Used when comparing the **defect rates** of two processes, two suppliers, or two time periods.

**Use Case in IQC:** Supplier A claims 1% defects, Supplier B claims 2% — are they significantly different?

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THESE FORMULAS — They appear in nearly every KTU ESE question on this topic.**

### Table 2.1: Complete Large Sample Test Formulas

| # | Test Scenario | Test Statistic Formula | Standard Error (SE) | Confidence Interval |
|---|---------------|------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Single Mean** (σ known) | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$ | $SE(\bar{x}) = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{x} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ |
| 2 | **Single Mean** (σ unknown, $n \geq 30$) | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu}{s/\sqrt{n}}$ | $SE(\bar{x}) = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{x} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ |
| 3 | **Difference of Two Means** | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}}$ | $SE(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$ | $(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot SE$ |
| 4 | **Single Proportion** | $Z = \dfrac{p - P}{\sqrt{PQ/n}}$ where $Q = 1 - P$ | $SE(p) = \sqrt{\dfrac{PQ}{n}}$ | $p \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{PQ}{n}}$ |
| 5 | **Difference of Two Proportions** | $Z = \dfrac{p_1 - p_2}{SE_{pooled}}$ | $SE_{pooled} = \sqrt{PQ\left(\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}\right)}$ | $(p_1 - p_2) \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot SE_{pooled}$ |

### Table 2.2: Critical Values Reference

| Significance Level ($\alpha$) | Two-Tailed $Z_{\alpha/2}$ | One-Tailed $Z_{\alpha}$ | Confidence Level |
|:----------------------------:|:------------------------:|:-----------------------:|:----------------:|
| 0.10 | 1.645 | 1.282 | 90% |
| 0.05 | **1.96** | 1.645 | 95% |
| 0.01 | **2.58** | 2.326 | 99% |

### Table 2.3: Pooled Proportion Formula (for Test 5)

$$P_{pooled} = \frac{n_1 p_1 + n_2 p_2}{n_1 + n_2} = \frac{x_1 + x_2}{n_1 + n_2}$$

$$Q_{pooled} = 1 - P_{pooled}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule (Two-Tailed Test):**
> - If $\vert Z_{calculated} \vert > Z_{critical}$ → **Reject $H_0$** (significant difference exists)
> - If $\vert Z_{calculated} \vert \leq Z_{critical}$ → **Fail to reject $H_0$** (no significant difference)

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where These Formulas Power Industry

| Industrial Application | Large Sample Test Used |
|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Six Sigma DMAIC — Measure Phase** | Single mean test to validate baseline process capability |
| **Supplier Quality Audits** | Difference of two means / proportions |
| **Pre-shipment Inspection (PSI)** | Single proportion test against AQL (Acceptable Quality Level) |
| **Process Validation (PV)** | Difference of two means (before vs. after process change) |
| **Warranty Claim Analysis** | Proportion test comparing claim rates across vehicle models |
| **Machine Capability Studies ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$)** | Large sample normality assumption underlies all capability indices |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Significance:** Without large sample tests, modern mass production would be impossible. Every **AQL-based acceptance plan**, every **Statistical Process Control (SPC) chart**, and every **Six Sigma project** relies on the normal approximation provided by the CLT for $n \geq 30$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Core Test Statistics

### Derivation 1: Standard Error of the Mean (Foundation Stone)

We start with a population with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. When we draw a sample of size $n$, the sample mean $\bar{X}$ is itself a **random variable** with its own distribution.

**Step 1: Define the sample mean.**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i = \frac{X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n}{n}$$

**Step 2: Compute the expected value of $\bar{X}$ (i.e., the mean of the sampling distribution).**

$$E(\bar{X}) = E\left(\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i) = \frac{1}{n} \cdot n \cdot \mu = \mu$$

**Step 3: Compute the variance of $\bar{X}$.**

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i) = \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n \cdot \sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Step 4: Take the square root to obtain the Standard Error.**

$$SE(\bar{X}) = \sqrt{\text{Var}(\bar{X})} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma^2}{n}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The factor $1/\sqrt{n}$ tells us that to **halve** the standard error, we must **quadruple** the sample size. This is why quality inspectors cannot reduce inspection error indefinitely by taking more samples — diminishing returns set in rapidly.

### Derivation 2: Standard Error of a Proportion

A proportion $p$ is a special case of a mean, where $X_i \in \{0, 1\}$ (defective or non-defective).

**Step 1: Define the sample proportion.**

$$p = \frac{X}{n} = \frac{\text{Number of successes}}{\text{Sample size}}$$

**Step 2: Recognize that $X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, P)$** with $\text{Var}(X) = nPQ$.

**Step 3: Compute the variance of $p$.**

$$\text{Var}(p) = \text{Var}\left(\frac{X}{n}\right) = \frac{1}{n^2} \text{Var}(X) = \frac{nPQ}{n^2} = \frac{PQ}{n}$$

**Step 4: Take the square root.**

$$SE(p) = \sqrt{\frac{PQ}{n}}$$

**Step 5: The Z-statistic for single proportion test.**

$$Z = \frac{p - P}{SE(p)} = \frac{p - P}{\sqrt{PQ/n}}$$

### Derivation 3: Pooled Standard Error for Difference of Two Proportions

When testing $H_0: P_1 = P_2$, we cannot use both $p_1$ and $p_2$ to estimate the common proportion because that would inflate the degrees of freedom artificially.

**Step 1: Combine the data from both samples.**

$$P_{pooled} = \frac{\text{Total defectives}}{\text{Total sample size}} = \frac{n_1 p_1 + n_2 p_2}{n_1 + n_2} = \frac{x_1 + x_2}{n_1 + n_2}$$

**Step 2: Set $Q_{pooled} = 1 - P_{pooled}$.**

**Step 3: Derive the pooled standard error.**

$$SE_{pooled} = \sqrt{P_{pooled} \cdot Q_{pooled} \left(\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}\right)}$$

**Step 4: Form the test statistic.**

$$Z = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{SE_{pooled}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pooling is necessary:** Under $H_0$, both samples come from populations with the **same** proportion $P$. Using separate estimates $p_1$ and $p_2$ would violate the null hypothesis assumption. The pooled estimate honors $H_0$ and produces an unbiased standard error.

### Derivation 4: Confidence Interval for a Mean

Starting from the Z-statistic:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \sim N(0,1)$$

For a two-tailed test at significance $\alpha$:

$$P\left(-Z_{\alpha/2} \leq \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \leq Z_{\alpha/2}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Rearranging:

$$P\left(\bar{X} - Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \mu \leq \bar{X} + Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Therefore, the **$(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ Confidence Interval** for $\mu$ is:

$$\mu \in \left[\bar{X} - Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}, \quad \bar{X} + Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right]$$

## 3.2 Worked-Out Analytical Examples (KTU Pattern)

### Example 1: Single Mean Test (The Foundational Case)

> **Problem:** A machine is supposed to produce bolts with a mean diameter of 10 mm. A random sample of 100 bolts yields $\bar{x} = 10.2$ mm and $s = 0.5$ mm. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the machine is operating correctly.

**Step 1: State the hypotheses.**

$$H_0: \mu = 10 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(machine is operating correctly)}$$
$$H_1: \mu \neq 10 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(machine is not operating correctly — two-tailed test)}$$

**Step 2: Choose significance level and critical value.**

$$\alpha = 0.05 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_{critical} = Z_{0.025} = 1.96$$

**Step 3: Compute the test statistic.**

Given: $n = 100$, $\bar{x} = 10.2$, $s = 0.5$ (used as estimate of $\sigma$ since $n \geq 30$).

$$Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{s/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{10.2 - 10.0}{0.5/\sqrt{100}} = \frac{0.2}{0.5/10} = \frac{0.2}{0.05} = 4.0$$

**Step 4: Decision rule.**

$$\text{Reject } H_0 \text{ if } \vert Z_{calc} \vert = 4.0 > 1.96 = Z_{critical}$$

**Step 5: Conclusion.**

Since $4.0 > 1.96$, we **reject $H_0$** at the 5% significance level. There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the machine is **not** producing bolts with mean diameter 10 mm. The process has shifted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** The foreman should immediately stop the machine, recalibrate the cutting tool, and conduct a **process capability study** to find the root cause of the diameter drift.

### Example 2: Difference Between Two Means (Supplier Comparison)

> **Problem:** A company sources ball bearings from two suppliers. A sample of 50 bearings from Supplier A has mean diameter 12.5 mm with $s_1 = 0.8$ mm. A sample of 60 bearings from Supplier B has mean diameter 12.3 mm with $s_2 = 0.6$ mm. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether there is a significant difference in the mean diameters.

**Step 1: State the hypotheses.**

$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 \quad \text{(no difference between suppliers)}$$
$$H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2 \quad \text{(significant difference exists — two-tailed)}$$

**Step 2: Critical value.**

$$Z_{0.025} = 1.96$$

**Step 3: Compute the standard error of the difference.**

$$SE(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) = \sqrt{\frac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{s_2^2}{n_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{(0.8)^2}{50} + \frac{(0.6)^2}{60}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{0.64}{50} + \frac{0.36}{60}} = \sqrt{0.0128 + 0.006} = \sqrt{0.0188} = 0.1371$$

**Step 4: Compute the Z-statistic.**

$$Z = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{SE} = \frac{12.5 - 12.3}{0.1371} = \frac{0.2}{0.1371} = 1.459$$

**Step 5: Decision.**

$$\vert Z_{calc} \vert = 1.459 < 1.96 = Z_{critical}$$

**Step 6: Conclusion.**

We **fail to reject $H_0$**. At the 5% significance level, there is **no significant difference** between the mean diameters of bearings from the two suppliers. The purchasing manager may consider other factors (price, delivery, reliability) in the supplier selection.

### Example 3: Single Proportion Test (AQL Validation)

> **Problem:** A production line has a historical defect rate of 3%. After a process improvement, a sample of 400 items is inspected and 8 are found defective. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the defect rate has significantly reduced.

**Step 1: State the hypotheses.**

$$H_0: P = 0.03 \quad \text{(no change in defect rate)}$$
$$H_1: P < 0.03 \quad \text{(defect rate has decreased — left-tailed test)}$$

**Step 2: Critical value for one-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$.**

$$Z_{0.05} = 1.645$$

**Step 3: Compute sample proportion.**

$$p = \frac{8}{400} = 0.02$$

**Step 4: Compute standard error under $H_0$.**

$$SE(p) = \sqrt{\frac{PQ}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{(0.03)(0.97)}{400}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0291}{400}} = \sqrt{0.00007275} = 0.00853$$

**Step 5: Compute Z-statistic.**

$$Z = \frac{p - P}{SE(p)} = \frac{0.02 - 0.03}{0.00853} = \frac{-0.01}{0.00853} = -1.172$$

**Step 6: Decision.**

For a left-tailed test, reject $H_0$ if $Z_{calc} < -Z_{critical} = -1.645$.

$$Z_{calc} = -1.172 > -1.645$$

**Step 7: Conclusion.**

We **fail to reject $H_0$**. The observed reduction from 3% to 2% is **not statistically significant** at the 5% level. The process improvement has not yet produced a meaningful defect rate reduction (or the sample size is insufficient to detect the small change).

> [!NOTE]
> **Power of the Test Insight:** To detect a change from 3% to 2%, a much larger sample is needed. Solving for $n$ when $Z = 1.96$ and $p = 0.02$ gives $n \approx 1900$ — illustrating the practical challenge of detecting small process improvements.

### Example 4: Difference of Two Proportions (Shift Comparison)

> **Problem:** In a textile factory, Shift A produces 600 items with 18 defectives, while Shift B produces 500 items with 25 defectives. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the two shifts have significantly different defect rates.

**Step 1: Hypotheses.**

$$H_0: P_1 = P_2 \quad \text{(no difference in defect rates)}$$
$$H_1: P_1 \neq P_2 \quad \text{(two-tailed test)}$$

**Step 2: Critical value.**

$$Z_{0.025} = 1.96$$

**Step 3: Compute sample proportions.**

$$p_1 = \frac{18}{600} = 0.030 \quad ; \quad p_2 = \frac{25}{500} = 0.050$$

**Step 4: Compute pooled proportion.**

$$P = \frac{n_1 p_1 + n_2 p_2}{n_1 + n_2} = \frac{18 + 25}{600 + 500} = \frac{43}{1100} = 0.03909$$

$$Q = 1 - P = 0.96091$$

**Step 5: Compute pooled standard error.**

$$SE = \sqrt{PQ\left(\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}\right)} = \sqrt{(0.03909)(0.96091)\left(\frac{1}{600} + \frac{1}{500}\right)}$$

$$= \sqrt{(0.03756)\left(0.001667 + 0.002000\right)} = \sqrt{(0.03756)(0.003667)} = \sqrt{0.0001377} = 0.01174$$

**Step 6: Compute Z-statistic.**

$$Z = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{SE} = \frac{0.030 - 0.050}{0.01174} = \frac{-0.020}{0.01174} = -1.704$$

**Step 7: Decision.**

$$\vert Z_{calc} \vert = 1.704 < 1.96 = Z_{critical}$$

**Step 8: Conclusion.**

We **fail to reject $H_0$** at $\alpha = 0.05$. Although Shift B has a numerically higher defect rate (5% vs 3%), the difference is **not statistically significant** at the 5% level. However, it **is** significant at $\alpha = 0.10$ (since $1.704 > 1.645$), suggesting the quality manager should monitor Shift B closely.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hypothesis Testing Decision Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Quality Problem] --> B[State H0 and H1]
    B --> C{Choose Test Type}
    C --> D1[Single Mean Test]
    C --> D2[Two Means Test]
    C --> D3[Single Proportion Test]
    C --> D4[Two Proportions Test]
    D1 --> E[Compute Z statistic]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Select Alpha Level]
    F --> G[Find Z critical from table]
    G --> H{Compare Z calc vs Z crit}
    H -->|Z calc > Z crit| I[Reject H0]
    H -->|Z calc leq Z crit| J[Fail to Reject H0]
    I --> K[Process has shifted Corrective action needed]
    J --> L[Process in control Continue monitoring]
    K --> M[End Decision]
    L --> M[End Decision]
```

## 4.2 Large Sample Test Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        S1[Sample Data n ge 30]
        S2[Claimed Population Parameter]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Test Computation Engine]
        P1[Compute Sample Statistic]
        P2[Calculate Standard Error]
        P3[Form Z Statistic]
    end
    subgraph DECISION[Decision Stage]
        D1[Look up Z critical at chosen alpha]
        D2[Apply Decision Rule]
        D3[Output Conclusion]
    end
    INPUT --> PROCESS
    PROCESS --> DECISION
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Central Limit Theorem — Conceptual Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Population may be skewed or uniform] --> B[Draw Samples of size n]
    B --> C[Compute sample means xbar1 xbar2 xbarN]
    C --> D[Distribution of xbar approaches Normal]
    D --> E[Mean of xbar distribution equals mu]
    D --> F[SD of xbar distribution equals sigma over sqrt n]
    E --> G[Apply Z test for large samples]
    F --> G
```

## 4.4 Four Types of Large Sample Tests — Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Large Sample Tests] --> A1[Tests on Means]
    ROOT --> A2[Tests on Proportions]
    A1 --> B1[Single Mean vs mu]
    A1 --> B2[Two Means mu1 vs mu2]
    A2 --> C1[Single Proportion vs P]
    A2 --> C2[Two Proportions P1 vs P2]
    B1 --> D1[Use sigma or s in SE formula]
    B2 --> D2[Use pooled or unpooled SE]
    C1 --> D3[Uses PQ by n in SE]
    C2 --> D4[Uses pooled P in SE]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DECISION[Quality Decision]
        E1[Accept Process]
        E2[Reject Process]
    end
    subgraph REALITY[Actual Process State]
        R1[Process is OK H0 true]
        R2[Process is BAD H0 false]
    end
    R1 -->|E1| OK1[Correct Decision Confidence 1 minus alpha]
    R1 -->|E2| ERR1[Type I Error alpha Producer Risk]
    R2 -->|E1| ERR2[Type II Error beta Consumer Risk]
    R2 -->|E2| OK2[Correct Decision Power 1 minus beta]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Q1.** State the Central Limit Theorem and explain its significance in large sample testing for industrial quality control. Why is a sample size of $n \geq 30$ considered "large"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** states that for a random sample of size $n$ drawn from a population with finite mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$, the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X}$ approaches a **normal distribution** with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ as $n$ becomes large, *irrespective* of the population's distribution. (1.5 marks)

> **Significance in IQC:** The CLT allows quality engineers to apply the standard normal (Z) test to any quality characteristic — even when the underlying distribution is non-normal (e.g., skewed defect sizes, exponential failure times). This is essential because most real-world manufacturing processes do not follow a perfectly normal distribution. (1 mark)

> A sample size of $n \geq 30$ is conventionally termed "large" because empirical studies show that for $n \geq 30$, the sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$ is sufficiently close to normal for practical applications, satisfying the conditions of the Z-test. (0.5 mark)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q2.** Distinguish between **Standard Deviation** and **Standard Error**. A random sample of 64 steel rods has a mean tensile strength of 520 MPa with standard deviation 16 MPa. Calculate the standard error of the mean and construct a 95% confidence interval for the true mean tensile strength.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Distinction:** Standard deviation measures the **variability of individual observations** within a single sample, while standard error measures the **variability of a sample statistic** (e.g., the mean) across repeated samples. (1 mark)

> **Calculation:** Standard error of the mean: (1 mark)

$$SE(\bar{x}) = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{16}{\sqrt{64}} = \frac{16}{8} = 2 \text{ MPa}$$

> **95% Confidence Interval:** $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$ (0.5 mark)

$$CI = \bar{x} \pm Z_{0.025} \cdot SE = 520 \pm 1.96 \times 2 = 520 \pm 3.92$$

$$\boxed{CI: [516.08, 523.92] \text{ MPa}}$$

(0.5 mark for the final interval)

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions — Module Internal Choice (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]

> **Q(A).** *(a)* Explain the procedure for testing the **equality of means of two large samples** with a neat formula for the test statistic. Mention the conditions under which the pooled variance is used. (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* A random sample of 200 items from Factory A has a mean weight of 50 kg with standard deviation 4 kg. A sample of 250 items from Factory B has a mean weight of 49 kg with standard deviation 5 kg. Test at the 5% significance level whether the two factories have significantly different mean weights. Also construct a 99% confidence interval for the difference of means. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Testing Equality of Two Means — Procedure:**

**Step 1: State Hypotheses.**

$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 \quad \text{(no significant difference)}$$
$$H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2 \quad \text{(significant difference — two-tailed)}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Standard Error of the Difference.**

When both sample sizes are large ($n_1, n_2 \geq 30$) and both population standard deviations are known (or estimated by $s_1, s_2$):

$$SE(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}} \approx \sqrt{\frac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{s_2^2}{n_2}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Test Statistic.**

$$Z = \frac{(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) - 0}{SE(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2)} = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}}}$$

**Step 4: Decision Rule.**

Compare $\vert Z_{calc} \vert$ with $Z_{\alpha/2}$. Reject $H_0$ if $\vert Z_{calc} \vert > Z_{\alpha/2}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **When to use the Pooled Variance Formula** (a common variation in KTU textbooks):
> The pooled standard deviation is used when we *assume* $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = \sigma^2$ (homoscedasticity). Then:
>
> $$s_p^2 = \frac{n_1 s_1^2 + n_2 s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}$$
>
> $$SE_{pooled} = s_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}}$$
>
> For the **Welch's version** (unequal variances), the unpooled formula in Step 2 above is preferred and is the one we use in part (b).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Factory A | Factory B |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Sample size | $n_1 = 200$ | $n_2 = 250$ |
| Sample mean | $\bar{x}_1 = 50$ kg | $\bar{x}_2 = 49$ kg |
| Std. deviation | $s_1 = 4$ kg | $s_2 = 5$ kg |

**Step 1: Hypotheses.**

$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 \quad ; \quad H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$$

**Step 2: Critical Value (two-tailed, $\alpha = 0.05$).**

$$Z_{0.025} = 1.96$$

**Step 3: Compute Standard Error.**

$$SE = \sqrt{\frac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{s_2^2}{n_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{(4)^2}{200} + \frac{(5)^2}{250}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{16}{200} + \frac{25}{250}} = \sqrt{0.08 + 0.10} = \sqrt{0.18} = 0.4243 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Compute Z-statistic.**

$$Z = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{SE} = \frac{50 - 49}{0.4243} = \frac{1}{0.4243} = 2.357$$

**Step 5: Decision.**

$$\vert Z_{calc} \vert = 2.357 > 1.96 = Z_{critical}$$

**Reject $H_0$** at the 5% level.

**Step 6: Conclusion.**

There is a **statistically significant difference** in the mean weights produced by the two factories. Factory A's output is heavier on average. The quality manager should investigate whether this difference is due to raw material variation, machine calibration, or operator technique.

**Step 7: 99% Confidence Interval for $(\mu_1 - \mu_2)$.**

$$Z_{0.005} = 2.58$$

$$CI = (\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm Z_{0.005} \cdot SE = 1 \pm 2.58 \times 0.4243$$

$$= 1 \pm 1.0947 = [-0.0947, 2.0947] \text{ kg}$$

> **Interpretation:** We are 99% confident that the true difference in mean weights lies between **$-0.09$ kg and $+2.09$ kg**. Since this interval **contains zero** (at the lower end), the difference is borderline significant at the 1% level — confirming the result at the 5% level but indicating that with stricter criteria, the evidence is marginal.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]

> **Q(B).** *(a)* What is a **Standard Error**? Derive the standard error of a sample proportion and the standard error of the difference between two sample proportions. (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* In a quality audit, 150 items from Lot 1 were inspected and 12 were found defective. From Lot 2, 200 items were inspected and 30 were found defective. Test at the 5% significance level whether there is a significant difference in the proportion of defectives between the two lots. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition of Standard Error:**

> A **Standard Error (SE)** is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of a statistic. It quantifies the precision of a sample statistic as an estimate of the corresponding population parameter. (1 mark)

**Derivation 1: Standard Error of a Sample Proportion.**

Let $X$ be the number of defectives in a sample of size $n$, with $P$ being the true population proportion. Then $X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, P)$, so $\text{Var}(X) = nPQ$ where $Q = 1-P$.

The sample proportion is $p = X/n$. Therefore:

$$\text{Var}(p) = \text{Var}\left(\frac{X}{n}\right) = \frac{1}{n^2} \text{Var}(X) = \frac{nPQ}{n^2} = \frac{PQ}{n}$$

Taking the square root:

$$\boxed{SE(p) = \sqrt{\frac{PQ}{n}}}$$

(2 marks for derivation)

**Derivation 2: Standard Error of Difference Between Two Proportions.**

For two independent samples, we want $SE(p_1 - p_2)$. Under $H_0: P_1 = P_2 = P$, we use the **pooled** proportion:

$$P_{pooled} = \frac{n_1 p_1 + n_2 p_2}{n_1 + n_2}$$

The variance of the difference is:

$$\text{Var}(p_1 - p_2) = \text{Var}(p_1) + \text{Var}(p_2) = \frac{P_{pooled} Q_{pooled}}{n_1} + \frac{P_{pooled} Q_{pooled}}{n_2}$$

$$= P_{pooled} Q_{pooled} \left(\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}\right)$$

Taking the square root:

$$\boxed{SE(p_1 - p_2) = \sqrt{PQ\left(\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}\right)}}$$

where $P = P_{pooled}$ and $Q = 1 - P_{pooled}$. (3 marks for derivation)

**Significance Note:** (1 mark) The pooled proportion is used because under the null hypothesis $H_0: P_1 = P_2$, both samples are assumed to come from populations with the *same* true proportion $P$. Using separate estimates $p_1$ and $p_2$ would overestimate the variance and reduce test power.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Lot 1 | Lot 2 |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| Sample size | $n_1 = 150$ | $n_2 = 200$ |
| Defectives | $x_1 = 12$ | $x_2 = 30$ |
| Proportion | $p_1 = 12/150 = 0.08$ | $p_2 = 30/200 = 0.15$ |

**Step 1: Hypotheses.**

$$H_0: P_1 = P_2 \quad ; \quad H_1: P_1 \neq P_2 \quad \text{(two-tailed)}$$

**Step 2: Critical Value.**

$$Z_{0.025} = 1.96 \text{ at } \alpha = 0.05$$

**Step 3: Compute Pooled Proportion.**

$$P = \frac{x_1 + x_2}{n_1 + n_2} = \frac{12 + 30}{150 + 200} = \frac{42}{350} = 0.12$$

$$Q = 1 - 0.12 = 0.88$$

**Step 4: Compute Pooled Standard Error.**

$$SE = \sqrt{PQ\left(\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}\right)} = \sqrt{(0.12)(0.88)\left(\frac{1}{150} + \frac{1}{200}\right)}$$

$$= \sqrt{(0.1056)\left(0.006667 + 0.005\right)} = \sqrt{(0.1056)(0.011667)} = \sqrt{0.001232} = 0.03510$$

**Step 5: Compute Z-statistic.**

$$Z = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{SE} = \frac{0.08 - 0.15}{0.03510} = \frac{-0.07}{0.03510} = -1.994$$

**Step 6: Decision.**

$$\vert Z_{calc} \vert = 1.994 > 1.96 = Z_{critical}$$

**Reject $H_0$** at the 5% significance level.

**Step 7: Conclusion.**

There is a **statistically significant difference** in the defective proportions of the two lots. Lot 2 has a significantly higher defect rate (15%) compared to Lot 1 (8%). The quality manager should:

1. Place Lot 2 on **100% inspection** before shipping.
2. Investigate root cause (supplier quality, handling damage, storage conditions).
3. Issue a **Supplier Corrective Action Request (SCAR)** if Lot 2 is from an external vendor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use the POOLED proportion** in the difference of proportions test. Using $p_1$ and $p_2$ separately in the SE formula is a **2-mark deduction**.
>
> 2. **Wrong choice of $Z_{critical}$**: Using 1.645 (one-tailed) when the test is two-tailed loses **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Sign error in $Z$ calculation**: Not taking absolute value before comparison. $\vert -1.994 \vert = 1.994$ must be compared to 1.96.
>
> 4. **Not stating hypotheses explicitly**: KTU examiners require formal $H_0$ and $H_1$ statements. Omission = **1 mark lost**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units or interpretation**: The final conclusion must state *what action* the engineer should take, not just whether $H_0$ is rejected.
>
> 6. **Arithmetic errors in $n \cdot p$ and $n \cdot q$ checks**: The test is valid only if $n_1 P \geq 5$, $n_1 Q \geq 5$, $n_2 P \geq 5$, $n_2 Q \geq 5$. Always verify this.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Large Sample Data**

### Foundation Concepts
- [ ] **Large Sample**: $n \geq 30$ (rule of thumb)
- [ ] **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**: Sample mean is approximately normal for large $n$ regardless of population distribution
- [ ] **Z-statistic**: Always $Z = \frac{\text{point estimate} - \text{hypothesized value}}{\text{standard error}}$

### Critical Values (Memorize!)
- [ ] $Z_{0.05/2} = 1.96$ (95% confidence, two-tailed)
- [ ] $Z_{0.01/2} = 2.58$ (99% confidence, two-tailed)
- [ ] $Z_{0.10/2} = 1.645$ (90% confidence, two-tailed)
- [ ] $Z_{0.05} = 1.645$ (one-tailed, $\alpha = 0.05$)

### Formula Arsenal
- [ ] $SE(\bar{x}) = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (mean)
- [ ] $SE(p) = \sqrt{PQ/n}$ (proportion)
- [ ] $SE(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2/n_1 + \sigma_2^2/n_2}$ (two means)
- [ ] $SE_{pooled}(p_1 - p_2) = \sqrt{P_{pooled} Q_{pooled}(1/n_1 + 1/n_2)}$ (two proportions)
- [ ] Pooled proportion: $P = (x_1 + x_2)/(n_1 + n_2)$

### Decision Rules
- [ ] **Two-tailed test:** Reject $H_0$ if $\vert Z_{calc} \vert > Z_{\alpha/2}$
- [ ] **Right-tailed test:** Reject $H_0$ if $Z_{calc} > Z_{\alpha}$
- [ ] **Left-tailed test:** Reject $H_0$ if $Z_{calc} < -Z_{\alpha}$

### Industrial Context Anchors
- [ ] **Single mean test** → Compare sample mean to specification
- [ ] **Two means test** → Compare two machines, suppliers, shifts
- [ ] **Single proportion test** → Compare defect rate to AQL/benchmark
- [ ] **Two proportions test** → Compare defect rates across sources

### Error Awareness
- [ ] **Type I error ($\alpha$):** Rejecting a good process (Producer's Risk)
- [ ] **Type II error ($\beta$):** Accepting a bad process (Consumer's Risk)
- [ ] **Power of test** = $1 - \beta$ → Higher power = better detection of true shifts

### Validity Conditions
- [ ] Sample must be **random** and **independent**
- [ ] $nP \geq 5$ and $nQ \geq 5$ for proportion tests
- [ ] $n \geq 30$ for mean tests (CLT applicability)

### Confidence Interval Construction (Universal Recipe)
- [ ] **Point Estimate** $\pm$ **Z-critical** $\times$ **Standard Error**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In the 14-mark questions, always:
> 1. State $H_0$ and $H_1$ explicitly (1 mark)
> 2. Show formula substitution with units (2 marks)
> 3. Calculate SE separately (1 mark)
> 4. Calculate Z with proper rounding (2 marks)
> 5. State decision rule and compare (2 marks)
> 6. Write **engineering conclusion** (not just statistical) (2 marks)
> 7. Mention assumptions/limitations (1 mark)
> 8. Suggest follow-up action (1 mark)
>
> This 8-step structure typically secures full marks in the ESE valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Warning Limits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET611 - INDUSTRIAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Warning Limits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Statistical Quality Control (SQC), **Warning Limits** (also known as **Inner Control Limits** or **2-Sigma Limits**) are auxiliary statistical boundaries established on a control chart at a distance of **±2 standard deviations (2σ)** from the process mean (Central Line, CL). They lie *inside* the conventional Upper and Lower Control Limits (UCL/LCL at ±3σ) and serve as an **early-warning monitoring zone**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Warning Limits are part of the *Zone-Based Control Chart Interpretation* methodology. They demarcate the boundary of **Zone C** (the inner-most zone bounded by the mean and 2σ) from **Zone B** (between 2σ and 3σ). Their primary function is to trigger preventive investigation *before* a process actually violates the 3σ control limits.

The formal mathematical representation of warning limits for a Shewhart $\bar{X}$ chart (process average chart) is:

$$\text{Upper Warning Limit (UWL)} = \overline{\overline{X}} + 2\sigma_{\bar{X}}$$

$$\text{Lower Warning Limit (LWL)} = \overline{\overline{X}} - 2\sigma_{\bar{X}}$$

For an $R$-chart (process range chart), the warning limits are:

$$\text{UWL}_R = \bar{R} + 2\sigma_R \approx D_4\bar{R} - (D_4 - d_2)\sigma_R$$

which is typically simplified using the standard factor $D_4$ as a reference, but the **2-sigma conceptual framework remains identical**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Highway Speed Indicator System**
>
> Imagine a highway with three critical markers:
> 1. **Green signal at the legal speed limit (Central Line, CL):** The process is operating in its natural, stable state.
> 2. **Yellow caution board at 2σ (Warning Limit, UWL/LWL):** You are still within legal bounds, but the system is **politely alerting** you to slow down. A point landing here isn't an *out-of-control* signal, but it demands *attention*.
> 3. **Red signal at 3σ (Control Limit, UCL/LCL):** You have officially crossed the boundary — a stop-the-line investigation is mandatory.
>
> Just as a single yellow signal does not mean an accident, a single point beyond the warning limit does not mean the process is defective. However, **2 out of 3 consecutive points in the warning zone, or 7 points trending in a row toward the warning limit**, are classic red flags.

### 1.3 Standard Statistical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Value (3-Sigma Standard) |
|---|---|---|
| Warning Limit Coefficient | $W$ | **2.00** |
| Control Limit Coefficient | $k$ | **3.00** |
| Area under Normal Curve within ±2σ | $P(\vert Z \vert \leq 2)$ | **0.9545 (95.45%)** |
| Area under Normal Curve within ±3σ | $P(\vert Z \vert \leq 3)$ | **0.9973 (99.73%)** |
| Probability of falling in Zone C (0 to 2σ) | $P(0 \leq Z \leq 2)$ | **0.4772 (47.72%)** |
| Probability of falling in Zone B (2σ to 3σ) | $P(2 \leq Z \leq 3)$ | **0.0214 (2.14%)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Interpretation:** When the process is in statistical control, approximately **4.55% of all points** will naturally fall *outside* the warning limits (i.e., in Zone B or beyond). This is why warning limits are **statistical guidelines** and not absolute pass/fail rules — they are most powerful when used in conjunction with **run rules** (e.g., Western Electric or Nelson rules).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The 2-Sigma Principle

The warning limit concept is derived from the **Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule)** of the Normal Distribution. The boundaries are placed at **±2σ** rather than ±1σ or ±3σ for a specific engineering reason:

- **At ±1σ:** Too sensitive — false alarms would be excessive (~32% of points lie beyond).
- **At ±3σ:** Identical to control limits — no early warning benefit.
- **At ±2σ:** Optimum balance — ~4.55% of points cross this boundary, providing a **manageable false-alarm rate** with meaningful predictive power when combined with run rules.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1 — Establish the Central Line (CL):**
The process mean is computed from preliminary samples taken when the process is known to be in control:
$$\text{CL} = \overline{\overline{X}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} \bar{X}_i}{m}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate the Process Variability ($\sigma_{\bar{X}}$):**
$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$$
where $n$ = subgroup size, $m$ = number of subgroups, $d_2$ = a tabulated constant based on $n$.

**Step 3 — Construct the Warning Limits:**
Apply the **2-sigma multiplier** symmetrically around the central line:
$$\text{UWL} = \overline{\overline{X}} + 2\sigma_{\bar{X}}$$
$$\text{LWL} = \overline{\overline{X}} - 2\sigma_{\bar{X}}$$

**Step 4 — Establish Control Limits (Outer 3σ Limits):**
$$\text{UCL} = \overline{\overline{X}} + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$$
$$\text{LCL} = \overline{\overline{X}} - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret via Zone Analysis:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zone Classification (KTU Board Standard):**
> - **Zone A** (between 2σ and 3σ limits) → Points here = "Caution Zone"
> - **Zone B** (between 1σ and 2σ limits) → "Watch Zone"
> - **Zone C** (between mean and 1σ) → "Safe Operating Zone"

**Step 6 — Apply the Western Electric Run Rules:**
A single point in Zone A is **not** an out-of-control signal. However, the following patterns trigger corrective action:
- **Rule 1:** 1 point beyond Zone A (beyond 3σ) → Investigate.
- **Rule 2:** 2 out of 3 consecutive points in Zone A or beyond → Investigate.
- **Rule 3:** 4 out of 5 consecutive points in Zone B or beyond → Investigate.
- **Rule 5:** 7 or more consecutive points on one side of the CL → Investigate.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Parameter | Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Upper Warning Limit ($\bar{X}$ chart) | $\text{UWL} = \overline{\overline{X}} + 2\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ | Detects upward process drift |
| 2 | Lower Warning Limit ($\bar{X}$ chart) | $\text{LWL} = \overline{\overline{X}} - 2\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ | Detects downward process drift |
| 3 | Warning Limits for $R$-chart | $\text{UWL}_R = \bar{R} + 2\sigma_R$ | Early detection of variability increase |
| 4 | Warning Limits for $p$-chart | $\text{UWL}_p = \bar{p} + 2\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | Attribute data monitoring |
| 5 | Probability in Warning Zone (one side) | $P(2\sigma \leq \vert X-\mu\vert \leq 3\sigma) = 0.0214$ | Basis for run rule design |
| 6 | Probability outside Warning Limits | $P(\vert X-\mu\vert \geq 2\sigma) = 0.0455$ | Expected false-alarm rate |
| 7 | Central Line | $\text{CL} = \overline{\overline{X}}$ | Process target |
| 8 | Control Limits (3σ) | $\text{UCL/LCL} = \overline{\overline{X}} \pm 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ | Out-of-control boundary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** All limits share the **same engineering unit** as the characteristic being measured (e.g., mm, kg, MPa, °C). The 2σ and 3σ multipliers are **dimensionless multipliers** on the standard deviation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Manufacturing:** Monitoring piston diameter — warning limits catch tool wear before a defect batch is produced.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication:** Wafer thickness control — warning limits signal deposition chamber drift.
- **Pharmaceutical Production:** Tablet weight monitoring — strict regulatory compliance requires early detection.
- **Food Processing:** Bottle fill volume — 2σ limits prevent both under-fill (regulatory) and over-fill (costly waste).
- **Six Sigma / Lean Manufacturing:** Warning limits are the foundation of **DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control)** in the Control phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Warning Limit Distance (Mathematical Justification)

We begin with the **Standard Normal Distribution**, where the probability density function is:

$$f(Z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{Z^2}{2}\right), \quad Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

The probability that a sample point falls *within* a distance of $k\sigma$ from the mean is given by:

$$P(\vert Z \vert \leq k) = \int_{-k}^{+k} f(Z)\,dZ = 2\int_{0}^{k} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-Z^2/2}\,dZ = 2\Phi(k) - 1$$

For $k = 2$, we have from the standard normal table:
$$\Phi(2) = 0.9772$$

Substituting:
$$P(\vert Z \vert \leq 2) = 2(0.9772) - 1 = 0.9544 \approx 0.9545$$

Hence, the probability of a point falling *outside* the warning limits is:
$$P(\vert Z \vert > 2) = 1 - 0.9545 = 0.0455 \;\; \text{(i.e., 4.55%)}$$

Splitting this symmetrically (using $\Phi(2) - \Phi(3) = 0.9772 - 0.9987 = 0.0215$):
$$P(2 < Z < 3) = 0.0214 \;\; \text{(Zone A — upper side)}$$
$$P(-3 < Z < -2) = 0.0214 \;\; \text{(Zone A — lower side)}$$

This means that, on average, **2.14% of points** will land in each of the upper and lower Zone A regions. This is the statistical basis for the **"2 out of 3" Western Electric rule** for warning limits.

### 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example (Step-by-Step Numerical Evaluation)

**Problem Statement (KTU Standard Format):**
A bearing manufacturing company monitors the inner diameter of roller bearings using an $\bar{X}$-chart. The subgroup size is $n = 5$, and 25 preliminary subgroups (each of size 5) yielded:
- Grand average: $\overline{\overline{X}} = 50.020$ mm
- Average range: $\bar{R} = 0.045$ mm

From standard SQC tables for $n = 5$: $d_2 = 2.326$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$.

**Required:**
1. Compute the **Central Line**, **Warning Limits**, and **Control Limits** for the $\bar{X}$-chart.
2. State the probability that a sample point will fall between the warning and control limits.
3. Comment on the engineering significance.

---

**Step 1 — Estimate $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$:**

The standard deviation of subgroup means is given by:
$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$$

Substituting the values:
$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{0.045}{2.326 \times \sqrt{5}} = \frac{0.045}{2.326 \times 2.2361}$$

Evaluating the denominator:
$$2.326 \times 2.2361 = 5.2010$$

Therefore:
$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{0.045}{5.2010} = 0.008653 \;\;\text{mm}$$

---

**Step 2 — Central Line (CL):**

$$\text{CL} = \overline{\overline{X}} = 50.020 \;\;\text{mm}$$

---

**Step 3 — Warning Limits (2σ):**

$$\text{UWL} = \overline{\overline{X}} + 2\sigma_{\bar{X}} = 50.020 + 2(0.008653)$$
$$\text{UWL} = 50.020 + 0.017306 = 50.0373 \;\;\text{mm}$$

$$\text{LWL} = \overline{\overline{X}} - 2\sigma_{\bar{X}} = 50.020 - 0.017306 = 50.0027 \;\;\text{mm}$$

---

**Step 4 — Control Limits (3σ):**

$$\text{UCL} = \overline{\overline{X}} + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = 50.020 + 3(0.008653)$$
$$\text{UCL} = 50.020 + 0.025959 = 50.0460 \;\;\text{mm}$$

$$\text{LCL} = \overline{\overline{X}} - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = 50.020 - 0.025959 = 49.9940 \;\;\text{mm}$$

---

**Step 5 — Probability Between Warning and Control Limits:**

$$P(2\sigma \leq \vert Z \vert \leq 3\sigma) = 2\left[\Phi(3) - \Phi(2)\right]$$
$$= 2\left[0.99865 - 0.97725\right] = 2(0.02140) = 0.0428$$

So, **4.28%** of points will naturally fall in the warning zone (between 2σ and 3σ on either side).

---

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> The UWL (50.0373 mm) and UCL (50.0460 mm) differ by approximately **0.0087 mm**, which is the radius of the **early-warning buffer zone**. If a tool begins to drift upward (e.g., thermal expansion, cutter wear), sample means will first approach the UWL — giving the operator an opportunity to **adjust the process before actual defects are produced**. This 0.0087 mm buffer is the **value of warning limits** in numerical terms.

### 3.3 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Limit Type | Symbol | Numerical Value (mm) | Distance from CL (mm) | Prob. of Random Crossing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Upper Control Limit | UCL | **50.0460** | +0.0260 | 0.00135 |
| Upper Warning Limit | UWL | **50.0373** | +0.0173 | 0.02275 |
| Central Line | CL | **50.0200** | 0.0000 | — |
| Lower Warning Limit | LWL | **50.0027** | −0.0173 | 0.02275 |
| Lower Control Limit | LCL | **49.9940** | −0.0260 | 0.00135 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Control Chart Zone Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["UCL = CL + 3 sigma | Out of Control Boundary"]
    nodeB["UWL = CL + 2 sigma | Upper Warning Limit"]
    nodeC["CL = Grand Mean | Process Target"]
    nodeD["LWL = CL - 2 sigma | Lower Warning Limit"]
    nodeE["LCL = CL - 3 sigma | Out of Control Boundary"]
    nodeF["Zone A Upper | 2 sigma to 3 sigma | Caution"]
    nodeG["Zone B Upper | 1 sigma to 2 sigma | Watch"]
    nodeH["Zone C Upper | 0 to 1 sigma | Safe"]
    nodeI["Zone C Lower | 0 to 1 sigma | Safe"]
    nodeJ["Zone B Lower | 1 sigma to 2 sigma | Watch"]
    nodeK["Zone A Lower | 2 sigma to 3 sigma | Caution"]
    nodeL["Western Electric Rule 1: 1 point beyond Zone A"]
    nodeM["Western Electric Rule 2: 2 of 3 in Zone A or beyond"]
    nodeN["Western Electric Rule 3: 4 of 5 in Zone B or beyond"]
    nodeO["Western Electric Rule 5: 7 consecutive on one side"]

    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeJ
    nodeE --> nodeK
    nodeF --> nodeL
    nodeF --> nodeM
    nodeG --> nodeN
    nodeH --> nodeO
    nodeI --> nodeO
```

### 4.2 Process Monitoring Decision Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1["Subgroup Data Collection | n = 5 samples"]
    input2["Compute Subgroup Mean X-bar"]
    input3["Plot X-bar on Control Chart"]
    decision1{"X-bar between LWL and UWL?"}
    decision2{"X-bar between WL and CL? 2 of 3 rule?"}
    decision3{"X-bar beyond 3 sigma?"}
    out1["Continue Production | No Action"]
    out2["Heightened Monitoring | Watch for Trends"]
    out3["STOP PRODUCTION | Investigate Assignable Cause"]
    out4["STOP PRODUCTION | Out of Control Signal"]

    input1 --> input2
    input2 --> input3
    input3 --> decision1
    decision1 -- Yes --> out1
    decision1 -- No -- within warning zone --> decision2
    decision2 -- Yes --> out2
    decision2 -- No --> decision3
    decision3 -- Yes --> out3
    decision3 -- No beyond 3 sigma --> out4
```

### 4.3 Comparison Schematic: Warning vs. Control Limits

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["Control Chart Hierarchy"]
        direction TB
        A1["ACT = 0.27 percent | Beyond 3 sigma | UCL and LCL"]
        A2["WARN = 4.55 percent | Beyond 2 sigma | UWL and LWL"]
        A3["NORMAL = 95.45 percent | Within 2 sigma | Safe Zone"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Action Levels"]
        direction TB
        B1["Out of Control Action | Stop Investigate Reset"]
        B2["Preventive Action | Monitor Adjust Verify"]
        B3["No Action | Process in Statistical Control"]
    end
    A1 --- B1
    A2 --- B2
    A3 --- B3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for KTU Exam:** When asked to draw a control chart, students are expected to mark **all five lines** (UCL, UWL, CL, LWL, LCL) and label the **three zones** (A, B, C) on each side. Marks are frequently lost when students omit the warning limits or fail to label the zone names.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Warning Limits. Why are they set at 2σ from the process mean and not at 1σ or 3σ?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Warning limits are statistical boundaries placed on a control chart at a distance of **±2 standard deviations (2σ)** from the central line. They lie *inside* the conventional 3σ control limits and serve as an **early-warning zone** for detecting process drift before an actual out-of-control condition occurs.
>
> **Why 2σ, not 1σ (1 mark):** At 1σ, approximately **32%** of all points would cross the boundary, producing excessive false alarms. This makes the chart practically useless for operational decision-making.
>
> **Why 2σ, not 3σ (1 mark):** At 3σ, the warning limit coincides with the control limit, providing **no early-warning buffer**. The unique value of the 2σ boundary lies in giving operators a **predictive window** to intervene *before* a defect-producing condition is reached.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State any three Western Electric Run Rules that are specifically applicable to the Warning Limit zone.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Rule 1 (1 mark):** **One point beyond Zone A** (i.e., beyond 3σ / beyond control limits) → Stop and investigate the assignable cause immediately.
>
> **Rule 2 (1 mark):** **Two out of three consecutive points** in Zone A or beyond (i.e., beyond the warning limits) → Indicates a process shift; tighten monitoring and prepare for corrective action.
>
> **Rule 3 (1 mark):** **Four out of five consecutive points** in Zone B or beyond (i.e., between 1σ and 3σ) → Suggests a sustained process drift; perform root-cause analysis.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, OEPET611]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of **Warning Limits** in Statistical Quality Control. Discuss the zone-based interpretation of control charts and how warning limits enable **proactive process control**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The diameter of a shaft is monitored using an $\bar{X}$-chart with subgroup size $n = 4$. From 20 preliminary subgroups, the following statistics were computed:
- Grand mean: $\overline{\overline{X}} = 25.040$ mm
- Mean range: $\bar{R} = 0.080$ mm

Given $d_2 = 2.059$ for $n = 4$:
- (i) Calculate the **Central Line**, **Upper Warning Limit**, **Lower Warning Limit**, **Upper Control Limit**, and **Lower Control Limit**. **(5 marks)**
- (ii) If a new sample mean of $\bar{X}_{\text{new}} = 25.075$ mm is observed, interpret the result in light of the warning and control limits. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Full Working):**

**(a) Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

> **Introduction (1.5 marks):** Warning limits are auxiliary statistical control boundaries established at **±2σ** from the process mean, lying inside the conventional 3σ control limits. They transform a Shewhart control chart from a *reactive* tool (which only signals after a defect is produced) into a *proactive* quality management instrument.
>
> **Zone Architecture (2 marks):** The control chart is divided into three zones on each side of the central line:
> - **Zone C** (0 to 1σ): 68.27% of in-control points lie here. Routine operation.
> - **Zone B** (1σ to 2σ): 13.59% of points lie here. The "Watch Zone."
> - **Zone A** (2σ to 3σ): 4.28% of points lie here. The "Caution Zone," bounded by the **warning limit (2σ)** and the **control limit (3σ)**.
>
> **Proactive Control Mechanism (2 marks):** When 2 out of 3 consecutive points fall in Zone A, or 4 out of 5 in Zone B, the operator receives an early signal of assignable-cause influence (e.g., tool wear, raw material variation, ambient temperature drift). Corrective action can be initiated *before* a non-conforming product is manufactured — saving cost, reducing scrap, and ensuring on-time delivery.
>
> **Industrial Relevance (1.5 marks):** In modern Industry 4.0 environments, warning limits are programmed into SPC software and connected to **automated feedback loops** in CNC machines, injection molding, and packaging lines — enabling real-time process adjustment.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Limit Calculations (5 marks):**

Step 1: Compute $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$:
$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.080}{2.059 \times \sqrt{4}} = \frac{0.080}{2.059 \times 2} = \frac{0.080}{4.118} = 0.01943 \;\text{mm}$$

Step 2: Central Line:
$$\text{CL} = \overline{\overline{X}} = 25.040 \;\text{mm}$$

Step 3: Warning Limits (2σ):
$$\text{UWL} = 25.040 + 2(0.01943) = 25.040 + 0.03886 = 25.0789 \;\text{mm}$$
$$\text{LWL} = 25.040 - 0.03886 = 25.0011 \;\text{mm}$$

Step 4: Control Limits (3σ):
$$\text{UCL} = 25.040 + 3(0.01943) = 25.040 + 0.05829 = 25.0983 \;\text{mm}$$
$$\text{LCL} = 25.040 - 0.05829 = 24.9817 \;\text{mm}$$

| Parameter | Value (mm) |
|---|---|
| UCL | 25.0983 |
| UWL | 25.0789 |
| CL | 25.0400 |
| LWL | 25.0011 |
| LCL | 24.9817 |

**Part (ii) — Interpretation (2 marks):**

> Given $\bar{X}_{\text{new}} = 25.075$ mm:
> - Compare with UWL = 25.0789 mm: $25.075 < 25.0789$, so the point lies **inside the warning limit** but very close to it.
> - Compare with UCL = 25.0983 mm: $25.075 < 25.0983$, so the point has **not exceeded the control limit**.
> - **Engineering Conclusion:** The process is **not yet out of control**, but the new mean lies in **Zone A** (between 2σ and 3σ). The operator should **heighten monitoring** of subsequent subgroups, verify machine settings, and check for tool wear. If 2 out of 3 consecutive points fall in this zone, corrective action is mandatory under Western Electric Rule 2.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, OEPET611]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and interpretation** of an $\bar{X}$-chart with **both warning and control limits** clearly marked. Highlight the role of the **2σ warning zone** in modern Six-Sigma quality systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical company monitors the fill volume of 500 mL bottles. Subgroup size is $n = 5$, and from 30 subgroups:
- $\overline{\overline{X}} = 500.15$ mL
- $\bar{R} = 4.20$ mL

For $n = 5$, $d_2 = 2.326$.
- (i) Calculate the **Upper and Lower Warning Limits** and **Upper and Lower Control Limits** for the $\bar{X}$-chart. **(5 marks)**
- (ii) Compute the **probability** that a sample mean will fall in the warning zone (between 2σ and 3σ on either side) when the process is in statistical control. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sketch and Theory (7 marks):**

> **Sketch Description (2 marks):** The $\bar{X}$-chart is drawn with the sample number on the x-axis and the subgroup mean $\bar{X}$ on the y-axis. Five horizontal lines are drawn:
> - UCL = $\overline{\overline{X}} + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ (top)
> - UWL = $\overline{\overline{X}} + 2\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ (second)
> - CL = $\overline{\overline{X}}$ (center)
> - LWL = $\overline{\overline{X}} - 2\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ (fourth)
> - LCL = $\overline{\overline{X}} - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ (bottom)
>
> **Zone Labeling (2 marks):** Each side of the CL is divided into three zones: C (0 to 1σ), B (1σ to 2σ), and A (2σ to 3σ).
>
> **Six-Sigma Context (3 marks):** In Six-Sigma methodology, processes are designed to operate with **±6σ tolerance** relative to specification limits, but the control chart still uses **3σ for control** and **2σ for warning**. The warning zone (2σ to 3σ) acts as a **Predictive Indicator Zone (PIZ)**, enabling the DMAIC "Control" phase to be executed proactively. When 2 of 3 points fall in Zone A, the **Cp and Cpk indices** are automatically re-evaluated by the SPC software, ensuring that the process capability remains ≥ 1.33 (the industry benchmark for capable processes).

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Limit Calculations (5 marks):**

Step 1: Compute $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$:
$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2\sqrt{n}} = \frac{4.20}{2.326 \times \sqrt{5}} = \frac{4.20}{2.326 \times 2.2361} = \frac{4.20}{5.2010} = 0.8075 \;\text{mL}$$

Step 2: Central Line:
$$\text{CL} = \overline{\overline{X}} = 500.15 \;\text{mL}$$

Step 3: Warning Limits (2σ):
$$\text{UWL} = 500.15 + 2(0.8075) = 500.15 + 1.615 = 501.765 \;\text{mL}$$
$$\text{LWL} = 500.15 - 1.615 = 498.535 \;\text{mL}$$

Step 4: Control Limits (3σ):
$$\text{UCL} = 500.15 + 3(0.8075) = 500.15 + 2.4225 = 502.5725 \;\text{mL}$$
$$\text{LCL} = 500.15 - 2.4225 = 497.7275 \;\text{mL}$$

| Parameter | Value (mL) |
|---|---|
| UCL | 502.5725 |
| UWL | 501.7650 |
| CL | 500.1500 |
| LWL | 498.5350 |
| LCL | 497.7275 |

**Part (ii) — Probability Calculation (2 marks):**

$$P(2\sigma \leq \vert Z \vert \leq 3\sigma) = 2[\Phi(3) - \Phi(2)]$$
$$= 2[0.99865 - 0.97725] = 2(0.02140) = 0.0428$$

> **Probability = 4.28%** — This is the expected proportion of subgroup means that will randomly fall in the warning zone (Zone A on either side) when the process is truly in statistical control. Any significantly higher frequency indicates a process shift requiring intervention.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks (KTU Board Examiner Notes):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $d_2$ separately for each $n$:** Students often copy the wrong $d_2$ value from tables. For $n=4$, $d_2 = 2.059$; for $n=5$, $d_2 = 2.326$. This error alone can cascade and cost **2 to 3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Omitting Warning Limits in the final answer:** When asked to "construct an $\bar{X}$-chart," students frequently plot only UCL, CL, and LCL. The **UWL and LWL are mandatory** and worth at least **1 mark each** in the exam.
>
> 3. **Confusing $\sigma$ with $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$:** The 2σ and 3σ are applied to $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ (standard error of the mean), not to $\sigma$ (process standard deviation). This is the **most common conceptual error** flagged by KTU examiners.
>
> 4. **Not stating the unit (mm, mL, kg, etc.) in the final limit values:** Numerical values without units are considered incomplete in KTU answer scripts and may be penalized.
>
> 5. **Misinterpreting the Western Electric rules:** Students often write "if any point crosses the warning limit, stop the process." This is **incorrect** — a single point in the warning zone is *not* a stop signal. Only the *patterns* described in the run rules trigger action.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read Before Exam Day**
>
> - **Definition:** Warning limits = **±2σ** from the process mean; lie inside the 3σ control limits.
> - **Purpose:** Provide an **early-warning buffer zone** to enable proactive process intervention *before* out-of-control conditions occur.
> - **Five Lines to Draw on Any Control Chart:** UCL (3σ) → UWL (2σ) → CL (mean) → LWL (−2σ) → LCL (−3σ).
> - **Three Zones per Side:** Zone C (0 to 1σ) | Zone B (1σ to 2σ) | Zone A (2σ to 3σ).
> - **Probability Facts:**
>   - 95.45% of points lie within ±2σ (inside warning limits).
>   - 4.55% of points lie beyond ±2σ (in warning zone or further).
>   - 4.28% (2 × 2.14%) of points lie *between* 2σ and 3σ.
> - **Key Western Electric Run Rules (Zone-Based):**
>   - **Rule 1:** 1 point beyond Zone A (i.e., beyond 3σ) → Stop.
>   - **Rule 2:** 2 of 3 consecutive points in Zone A or beyond → Investigate.
>   - **Rule 3:** 4 of 5 consecutive points in Zone B or beyond → Investigate.
>   - **Rule 5:** 7 or more consecutive points on one side of CL → Investigate.
> - **Standard Formula Set:**
>   - $\text{UWL} = \overline{\overline{X}} + 2\sigma_{\bar{X}}$
>   - $\text{LWL} = \overline{\overline{X}} - 2\sigma_{\bar{X}}$
>   - $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$
> - **Why 2σ and not 1σ or 3σ?**
>   - 1σ: Too many false alarms (32% outside).
>   - 3σ: No early warning benefit (coincides with control limit).
>   - 2σ: Optimal balance (4.55% outside, manageable).
> - **Common Exam Traps to Avoid:**
>   - Don't confuse $\sigma$ with $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$.
>   - Don't forget to include units in your numerical answers.
>   - Don't state that a single point in the warning zone means "out of control" — only the *patterns* do.
>   - Don't use the wrong $d_2$ value for the given $n$.
> - **Industrial Application Snapshot:** Warning limits are the cornerstone of Six-Sigma DMAIC's "Control" phase, IoT-enabled smart factories, and FDA/ISO 9001-compliant SPC systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Revised Control Limits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET611 - INDUSTRIAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Revised Control Limits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Revised Control Limits** are the *final, adjusted control boundaries* established for a Shewhart control chart after the process has been demonstrated to be in a state of **statistical control** using *trial (preliminary) control limits*. In this stage, the **sample-based estimators** of the process parameters (e.g., $\overline{\overline{X}}$, $\overline{R}$, $\overline{p}$, $\overline{c}$) are replaced by the **true process parameters** (or refined estimates derived from the rational subgroups), and the limits are recomputed using the standard $3\sigma$ (three-sigma) spread.

**Trial Control Limits** = Limits calculated using sample statistics from a small number (usually **20 to 25**) of preliminary subgroups.

**Revised Control Limits** = Limits recalculated after:
1. Process stability is confirmed (no out-of-control points on trial charts).
2. The grand averages (like $\overline{\overline{X}}$, $\overline{R}$) are adopted as the official **Centre Line (CL)**.
3. Limits are tightened/refined to reflect process capability more accurately.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **driving instructor** teaching a student:

- **Trial Limits** are like drawing *temporary lane markings* with chalk while observing how the student drives for a few rounds. You use the *average wobble* to mark the lines.
- After confirming the student drives steadily (no erratic swerves), you now **repaint the lane markings using solid paint** based on the *true central tendency* and the *standard deviation of the actual driving pattern*.

The *chalk lines* are **trial limits** — they help you verify stability.
The *permanent paint lines* are **revised control limits** — they are the official reference for all future monitoring.

---

## Key Standard Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Constant | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| **$3\sigma$** | Three-sigma spread defining the control band (covers **99.73%** of common-cause variation under normality). |
| **$A_2$** | Shewhart factor for $\overline{X}$ chart using $\overline{R}$. |
| **$D_3$, $D_4$** | Shewhart factors for the R-chart limits. |
