# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**Differentiate between accuracy and precision in a measurement system, with one industrial example for each concept.** *(Module 3, Static Characteristics)*

**Model Answer:**

- **Accuracy** is the closeness of a *single* (or mean of) measurement to the **true value** of the measurand. It is governed by **systematic errors / bias**.
- **Precision** is the closeness of agreement among **repeated measurements** of the same quantity under identical conditions. It is governed by **random errors / standard deviation**.

**Example (Industrial):**
- A calibrated torque wrench set to 100 N·m indicates 100.2 N·m on a certified test rig — this is **accurate** (closeness to true value).
- The same wrench is used by the same operator 10 times on the same bolt and gives readings 99.8, 100.1, 99.9, 100.0, 100.2, 99.7, 100.0, 100.1, 99.9, 100.0 — this is **precise** (low scatter).

A measurement can be precise without being accurate (e.g., instrument zero has shifted) and accurate without being precise (e.g., noisy instrument with mean equal to true value).

---

### Question 2
**Define hysteresis and dead band in a measurement system. State one method to reduce hysteresis in a Bourdon-tube pressure gauge.** *(Module 3, Static Characteristics)*

**Model Answer:**

- **Hysteresis** is the maximum difference in the output reading of an instrument when the input is approached first from an increasing direction and then from a decreasing direction, expressed as a percentage of full-scale output.
- **Dead band (or dead zone)** is the range of input values over which the output shows *no change at all*. It is the input range below the threshold of detectability.

**Reduction method for Bourdon-tube pressure gauge:**
Use **elastic materials with low internal friction** (e.g., beryllium-copper instead of mild steel) and **over-coil the tube during manufacture** to ensure uniform stress distribution. Additionally, take readings in *both* increasing and decreasing modes and average them, or apply a correction curve.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat labelled block diagram, describe the functional elements of a generalised measurement system. Identify the element in which loading error is most likely to originate.** *(Module 3, Measurement System)*

**Model Solution:**

**Functional elements (in order):**

1. **Primary sensing element** – directly senses the measurand (e.g., Bourdon tube for pressure, thermocouple junction for temperature). The element must not appreciably disturb the measurand.
2. **Variable conversion element** – converts the sensed signal into a more useful form (e.g., strain gauge converts strain into resistance change; LVDT converts displacement into voltage).
3. **Variable manipulation element** – amplifies, filters, integrates, or differentiates the signal (e.g., instrumentation amplifier, low-pass filter, ADC).
4. **Data transmission element** – carries the signal from the field to the display/recorder (e.g., 4-20 mA current loop, fibre-optic link, telemetry).
5. **Data presentation element** – presents the signal in human-readable form (e.g., digital display, strip-chart recorder, computer monitor with SCADA/HMI).

**Block diagram:**

```
Measurand → [Sensing] → [Conversion] → [Manipulation] → [Transmission] → [Presentation] → Indicated Value
```

**Loading error origin:** Loading error arises when the **primary sensing element** extracts energy from the measurand and thereby alters it. Example: A heavy Bourdon tube in a low-pressure gas system physically displaces gas as it uncoils, slightly changing the pressure — this is a loading error. Mitigation: use a **high-impedance, low-mass** sensor.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A first-order temperature-measuring instrument has a time constant of 8 s. It is suddenly placed in a bath whose temperature is 120 °C. The initial reading is 30 °C. Find (i) the reading at $t = 10$ s, (ii) the time to reach 119 °C, and (iii) the time to reach 1% of the final value error.** *(Module 3, Dynamic Characteristics)*

**Model Solution:**

Given: $\tau = 8$ s, $T_i = 30$ °C, $T_f = 120$ °C, $K = 1$ (assumed unity for a direct-reading instrument).

**Step 1 — Step response of a first-order instrument:**

$$
T(t) = T_f + (T_i - T_f) e^{-t/\tau}
$$

$$
T(t) = 120 + (30 - 120) e^{-t/8} = 120 - 90 e^{-t/8}
$$

**(i) Reading at $t = 10$ s:**

$$
T(10) = 120 - 90 \cdot e^{-10/8} = 120 - 90 \cdot e^{-1.25}
$$

$$
e^{-1.25} = 0.2865
$$

$$
T(10) = 120 - 90(0.2865) = 120 - 25.78 = 94.22 \text{ °C}
$$

**(ii) Time to reach 119 °C:**

Set $T(t) = 119$:

$$
119 = 120 - 90 e^{-t/8}
\implies 90 e^{-t/8} = 1
\implies e^{-t/8} = \frac{1}{90}
$$

$$
t = -8 \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{90}\right) = 8 \ln(90) = 8 \times 4.4998 = 36.0 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Time to reach 1% of the final-value error:**

The error is $|T_f - T(t)| = 90 e^{-t/8}$. For 1% of *full-scale step* (i.e., 1% of 90 = 0.9):

$$
90 e^{-t/8} = 0.009 \times 90 \text{ (i.e., 1% of step magnitude)}
$$

Wait — the 1% criterion is interpreted as 1% of the *step magnitude*:

$$
90 e^{-t/8} = 0.01 \times 90 = 0.9
\implies e^{-t/8} = 0.01
\implies t = -8 \ln(0.01) = 8 \times 4.605 = 36.84 \text{ s}
$$

(Alternative reading: 1% of final value, i.e., $T_f - T(t) = 0.01 \times 120 = 1.2$ → $t = 34.4$ s. Examiners accept either, but state your interpretation.)

**Final answers:** (i) **94.22 °C**, (ii) **36.0 s**, (iii) **36.8 s** (1% of step criterion).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Define the following static characteristics of a measurement system, giving the relevant mathematical expressions: (i) Sensitivity, (ii) Linearity error, (iii) Hysteresis, (iv) Threshold, (v) Drift, (vi) Resolution, (vii) Static error.** *(Module 3, Static Characteristics)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Sensitivity $K$:** Ratio of change in output to the corresponding change in input. For a linear system, $K$ is constant; for non-linear systems, $K = dq_o / dq_i$ at a point.

$$
K = \frac{\Delta q_o}{\Delta q_i}
$$

**(ii) Linearity error (non-linearity) $e_L$:** The maximum deviation of the calibration curve from the best-fit straight line, expressed as a percentage of full-scale output.

$$
\%e_L = \frac{(q_o)_{actual} - (q_o)_{ideal}}{FS} \times 100
$$

**(iii) Hysteresis $e_H$:** The maximum difference between the upscale and downscale readings for the same input value, expressed as % of FS.

$$
\%e_H = \frac{\Delta H_{max}}{FS}\times 100
$$

**(iv) Threshold:** The smallest incremental change in the input (measurand) that produces a detectable change in the output.

$$
\text{Threshold} = \Delta q_i \bigg\vert_{q_o \text{ just-detectable change}}
$$

**(v) Drift:** An undesired change in the output over a period of time, with no corresponding change in input. Two types:
- *Zero drift* — change in output at zero input.
- *Sensitivity drift* — change in the slope $K$ over time.

**(vi) Resolution:** The smallest discernible change in the *output* indication. For a digital system of $n$ bits and full-scale $FS$:

$$
\Delta = \frac{FS}{2^n}
$$

**(vii) Static error $e_s$:** The difference between the indicated value and the true value, measured under static (steady) input conditions.

$$
e_s = A_m - A_t
$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A pharmaceutical tablet-pressing process produces tablets with target weight 500 mg. Twenty-five samples give a mean of 502 mg and a standard deviation of 4 mg. The specification limits are $LSL = 488$ mg and $USL = 512$ mg. Compute (i) $C_p$, (ii) $C_{pk}$, (iii) the expected defective rate in ppm, and (iv) comment on process capability.** *(Module 3, Process Capability)*

**Model Solution:**

Given: $\mu = 502$ mg, $\sigma = 4$ mg, $LSL = 488$ mg, $USL = 512$ mg, target = 500 mg.

**(i) Compute $C_p$ (process potential, assuming centred):**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{512 - 488}{6 \times 4} = \frac{24}{24} = 1.000
$$

The potential is exactly 1.0 — the *spread* of the process is just sufficient, but the process must be **perfectly centred** at the midpoint $(488+512)/2 = 500$ mg to realise it.

**(ii) Compute $C_{pk}$ (actual capability, accounting for off-centring):**

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{512 - 502}{3 \times 4} = \frac{10}{12} = 0.833
$$

$$
C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{502 - 488}{12} = \frac{14}{12} = 1.167
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = \min(0.833, 1.167) = 0.833
$$

**(iii) Expected defective rate:**

The upper-tail $Z$ for $USL$ is $Z_U = 3 C_{pu} = 3 \times 0.833 = 2.5$.
The lower-tail $Z$ for $LSL$ is $Z_L = 3 C_{pl} = 3 \times 1.167 = 3.5$.

From the standard normal table:
- $P(Z > 2.5) = 0.00621$ → **6 210 ppm** above USL.
- $P(Z < -3.5) = 0.000233$ → **233 ppm** below LSL.

**Total defective rate ≈ 6 210 + 233 = 6 443 ppm.**

**(iv) Comment:**

The process is **not capable** by KTU 2024 industry standard ($C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ is the typical benchmark; $C_{pk} \ge 1.00$ is sometimes tolerated for less-critical applications). The mean is shifted **upward** (502 mg vs target 500 mg), so the upper tail is the limiting side. Recommendations:
1. Re-centre the press to bring the mean closer to 500 mg — this will raise $C_{pu}$.
2. Reduce $\sigma$ by tightening the powder-flow control — this will raise both $C_{pu}$ and $C_{pl}$.
3. Re-verify that the **measurement system's %R&R is < 30%** before trusting these indices; otherwise a $C_{pk}$ of 0.833 might be partially artefactual.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in this topic (KTU 2024 trend, Module 3):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$** — $C_p$ assumes centring; $C_{pk}$ does not. Examiners specifically ask for *both* and deduct a full mark if only one is computed.
> 2. **Unit mismatches in first-order step response** — using the wrong initial condition (e.g., assuming $q_o(0) = KQ_i$ instead of 0). Always start from $q_o(0) = 0$ unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 3. **Forgetting to label units** in sensitivity calculations — a "bar/bar" sensitivity is fine, but in mixed units (e.g., mV/°C) the units **must** be quoted.
> 4. **Writing %R&R > 30% as "bad sensor"** — the examiner wants the classification **plus** a recommended action (re-calibrate, use a higher-resolution gauge, train operator).
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** in the 7-mark part on functional elements — a block diagram is *worth 2 marks* on its own; skipping it can cost you the entire sub-part.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations — "Assuming linearity" or "Assuming normal distribution" should always precede the formula.
> 7. **Mixing up accuracy and precision** in Part A — examiners routinely award 1 mark out of 3 for partially correct definitions. Use the exact words "closeness to true value" vs "closeness among repeated measurements."

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Process = 6M** (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother-Nature).
- [x] **Measurement system = 5 functional blocks** (Sensing → Conversion → Manipulation → Transmission → Presentation).
- [x] **Static characteristics:** accuracy, precision, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, reproducibility, threshold, resolution, drift, dead band.
- [x] **Dynamic characteristics:** zero-order, first-order ($\tau$), second-order ($\omega_n$, $\zeta$).
- [x] **First-order step response:** $q_o(t) = KQ_i(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$; reaches 63.2% in $\tau$, 95% in $3\tau$, 98% in $4\tau$.
- [x] **Process capability:** $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$; $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$.
- [x] **Capability benchmark (KTU 2024):** $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ = capable; $1.00 \le C_{pk} < 1.33$ = marginal; $C_{pk} < 1.00$ = not capable.
- [x] **%R&R benchmark:** < 10% excellent; 10–30% conditional; > 30% unacceptable.
- [x] **Errors:** gross (human), systematic (bias, calibration), random (noise), environmental, loading, quantisation.
- [x] **Loading error** originates in the **primary sensing element**.
- [x] **Calibration** corrects systematic error; **replication (multiple readings)** reduces random error.
- [x] **Process potential vs actual performance:** $C_p$ (centred) vs $C_{pk}$ (off-centred).
- [x] **Digital resolution:** $\Delta = FS/2^n$ — quote bit-count to score full marks.
- [x] **Drift** has two flavours: **zero drift** and **sensitivity drift** — examiners frequently ask for both.
- [x] **In a second-order system, $\zeta = 1$ is critical damping** — fastest non-oscillatory response. $\zeta < 1$ is under-damped (oscillates). $\zeta > 1$ is over-damped (sluggish).
- [x] **Always pair a formula with a unit and an interpretation sentence.** Examiners reward clarity.
- [x] **Gauge R&R must be done BEFORE process control charts** — otherwise you cannot trust the data.
- [x] **Final formula to memorise (damped natural frequency):** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capability Analysis: Using Probability Plot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET611 - INDUSTRIAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capability Analysis: Using Probability Plot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Capability Analysis** is a statistical procedure used in Statistical Quality Control (SQC) to evaluate the inherent ability of a manufacturing or service process to produce output that conforms to the engineering specification limits (USL — Upper Specification Limit and LSL — Lower Specification Limit). It quantifies the relationship between the actual process spread (governed by the process mean **μ** and standard deviation **σ**) and the design tolerance band.

A **Probability Plot** (specifically, the **Normal Probability Plot** in capability analysis) is a graphical diagnostic tool that plots the ordered sample observations against their corresponding *cumulative normal probabilities* (or their inverses, the *Normal Equivalent Deviates / Z-scores*). It is used to **verify the underlying distributional assumption (normality)** of the process data *before* computing capability indices. If the data points lie approximately along a straight line, the assumption of a **Normal Distribution** is validated, and indices such as **C_p**, **C_pk**, **C_pl**, and **C_pu** become meaningful and reportable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Capability analysis is *invalid* if the process output is not (approximately) Normal. The probability plot is the simplest, most visually intuitive KTU-approved method to *verify normality* and to *estimate the process standard deviation* from the slope of the fitted line — both prerequisites for a valid Cp/Cpk calculation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a baker producing biscuits that must weigh between 48 g and 52 g (tolerance = 4 g). Most biscuits come out near 50 g, but some are heavier, some lighter. Now imagine weighing 50 biscuits and writing their weights on a strip of paper from lightest to heaviest.

If you then stretch a rubber band over these 50 dots in a way that best fits them, two things can happen:

1. **The dots lie almost on a straight line** → the biscuit weights are *normally distributed* (the classic bell-shaped spread). You can confidently say: "Out of every million biscuits, only a handful will fall outside 48–52 g." Now your **process capability number (Cpk)** is trustworthy.
2. **The dots bend, curve, or wander** → the biscuit weights do *not* follow a normal pattern (perhaps they are skewed, or have a sudden clustering). Any capability number you calculate using the simple ±3σ rule will be a *lie* about your process. The probability plot has caught the lie.

Think of the probability plot as a **"lie-detector test"** for the normality assumption. It does not calculate capability directly — it **certifies whether the process is eligible** for capability calculation, and simultaneously provides a visual estimate of the process mean (intercept) and standard deviation (slope).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric Used in this Module:** The two standard normal-distribution reference points are **z = −3** and **z = +3**, which together cover **99.73%** of the area under the bell curve. All capability indices are built around this 6-σ spread concept.

## 1.3 Where Probability Plot Capability Analysis is Used in Industry

- **Automotive component manufacturing** (SPC/PPAP submissions)
- **Pharmaceutical tablet weight and dissolution testing**
- **Bearing race diameter inspection**
- **Six Sigma Green Belt / Black Belt certification projects**
- **Pre-launch validation** of any new process before Cpk is reported to the customer
- **Audit scenarios** (IATF 16949, ISO 9001) where the auditor may ask: *"Show me how you verified normality before quoting Cpk."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Flow of Capability Analysis using a Probability Plot

The complete analytical workflow consists of **six sequential reasoning steps**:

1. **Collect the data:** Draw a *rational subgroup* of size *n* (typically *n* = 5, with *k* = 25 subgroups, total 125 observations) from a *stable, in-control* process (verified first using a control chart such as the **X̄–R chart** or **X̄–s chart**).

2. **Verify process stability:** Before capability, the process must be in statistical control. Otherwise, common-cause variation estimates become contaminated with special-cause noise.

3. **Construct the Normal Probability Plot:**
   - **Step 3a:** Sort the *n* observations in ascending order → *x_{(1)} ≤ x_{(2)} ≤ … ≤ x_{(n)}*.
   - **Step 3b:** Calculate the *plotting position* for the *i*-th ordered observation using a formula such as the **Hazen plotting position**:
     $$f_i = \frac{i - 0.5}{n}$$
     or alternatively the **Weibull plotting position** *f_i = i/(n+1)* or **Blom's** *(i − 0.375)/(n + 0.25)*.
   - **Step 3c:** Convert each *f_i* to its **Normal Equivalent Deviate (NED)** *z_i = Φ⁻¹(f_i)*, where *Φ⁻¹* is the inverse of the standard normal cumulative distribution function.
   - **Step 3d:** Plot *x_{(i)}* (Y-axis) versus *z_i* (X-axis). Draw a best-fit straight line.

4. **Interpret the plot (Normality Test by Eye / Quantile–Quantile reasoning):**
   - **Linear pattern through the centre:** Normality is supported.
   - **S-shape (concave then convex):** Heavy tails / outliers.
   - **Convex curve above the line:** Right (positive) skew.
   - **Concave curve below the line:** Left (negative) skew.
   - **A single point far from the line:** Outlier or measurement error.

5. **Estimate μ and σ from the fitted line:**
   - The **intercept of the line** at *z = 0* is the estimated process mean **μ̂**.
   - The **slope of the line** is the estimated process standard deviation **σ̂**.
   - This is a key advantage: *both μ and σ can be read off a single probability plot*, avoiding the bias of *x̄ ± 3s* when data is contaminated.

6. **Compute capability indices** (C_p, C_pk, C_pl, C_pu) and the **process performance ratio (PPR)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why use the probability plot's slope for σ, and not just the sample standard deviation *s*?**
> The slope is the standard deviation of the *central* portion of the data (the line is fitted to all the points, but outliers pull *s* disproportionately). In practice the two estimates are close, but the probability plot gives a *visual confirmation* that the assumed Gaussian model is appropriate.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are listed in the order they would be required during a KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) numerical problem.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Plotting position (Hazen) | $f_i = (i - 0.5)/n$ | Dimensionless probability between 0 and 1 |
| 2 | Normal score (NED) | $z_i = \Phi^{-1}(f_i)$ | Read from standard normal tables |
| 3 | Process mean estimate | $\hat{\mu}$ = intercept of fitted line at $z=0$ | Same unit as *x* |
| 4 | Process std-dev estimate | $\hat{\sigma}$ = slope of fitted line | Same unit as *x* |
| 5 | Specification spread | $T = \text{USL} - \text{LSL}$ | Engineering tolerance |
| 6 | Process spread | $6\hat{\sigma}$ | Natural tolerance of the process |
| 7 | Process Capability Index | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}}$ | Centered-process measure |
| 8 | Lower one-sided index | $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\hat{\mu} - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}}$ | Measures distance of mean above LSL |
| 9 | Upper one-sided index | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \hat{\mu}}{3\hat{\sigma}}$ | Measures distance of mean below USL |
| 10 | Process Capability Index (centring included) | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pl},\ C_{pu})$ | Most reported index |
| 11 | Off-centring / process shift | $k = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert \hat{\mu} - M \vert}{T}$ where $M = (\text{USL}+\text{LSL})/2$ | $0 \le k \le 1$ |
| 12 | Centred Cpk | $C_{pk,\text{ideal}} = C_p (1 - k)$ | Theoretical Cpk after re-centring |
| 13 | Process Performance Index | $P_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6s}$ | Uses *overall* standard deviation *s* |
| 14 | Process Performance Index (centring) | $P_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\bar{x}}{3s},\ \dfrac{\bar{x}-\text{LSL}}{3s}\right)$ | Long-term index |
| 15 | Defects per Million Opportunities (DPMO) from Cpk | $\text{DPMO} \approx 2 \, \Phi(-3 C_{pk}) \times 10^6$ | Six-Sigma metric |
| 16 | Sigma level | $\Sigma = 3 C_{pk}$ | Industry sigma scale |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Error:** Mixing up the short-term *C_p / C_pk* (uses $\hat{\sigma}$ from probability plot or R̄/d₂) with the long-term *P_p / P_pk* (uses overall *s*). The probability plot method described in Module 3 is the *short-term* estimation technique.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- The probability plot is the de-facto tool used in **Six Sigma DMAIC-Measure phase** to baseline a process.
- **Automotive IATF 16949** PPAP submissions require a *normal probability plot* attached to the Cpk study.
- **Pharma / FDA validation** requires documented proof of normality of assay / content-uniformity data.
- **Bearing industry** (SKF, FAG) uses probability plots to identify *bi-modal* distributions (e.g., two spindles producing slightly different diameters) — the plot shows a clear *kink* or *step* in the line.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Cp Formula from First Principles

Let the *i*-th observation be *x_i* drawn from *N(μ, σ²)*. For a normal distribution, the probability of falling inside *μ ± 3σ* is:

$$P(\mu - 3\sigma \le X \le \mu + 3\sigma) = \Phi(3) - \Phi(-3)$$

Using the symmetry property $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ and the table value $\Phi(3) = 0.99865$:

$$P(\mu - 3\sigma \le X \le \mu + 3\sigma) = 0.99865 - (1 - 0.99865) = 0.9973$$

Therefore, **99.73% of a normal process's output lies within the band $\mu \pm 3\sigma$**, i.e. within a width of $6\sigma$.

If the **specification tolerance band** is *USL − LSL*, and the **process natural tolerance band** is $6\sigma$, then the *capability ratio* (the simpler predecessor to Cp, denoted *CR*) is:

$$CR = \frac{6\sigma}{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}$$

The *Process Capability Index* is the *reciprocal* of *CR* (so that *higher* value ⇒ *better* process):

$$C_p = \frac{1}{CR} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

This is the fundamental Cp equation. **The probability plot is what allows us to legitimately replace σ with σ̂** (the slope of the fitted normal-probability line) because the fit proves the data is Gaussian.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cpk from Cp and Off-Centring Factor *k*

The midpoint of the specification band is:

$$M = \frac{\text{USL} + \text{LSL}}{2}$$

The *signed shift* of the process mean from this midpoint is:

$$d = \hat{\mu} - M$$

Normalising by half the tolerance:

$$k = \frac{2d}{T} = \frac{2(\hat{\mu} - M)}{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}, \quad 0 \le k \le 1$$

The closer *k* is to 1, the more the process is shifted toward a specification limit. The factor *(1 − k)* represents the *loss in capability* due to off-centring. Hence:

$$C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k) = C_p \left( 1 - \frac{2 \vert \hat{\mu} - M \vert}{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}} \right)$$

Algebraically expanding:

$$C_{pk} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} - \frac{\vert \hat{\mu} - M \vert}{3\sigma}$$

It can be shown (by taking *min* of the two one-sided indices) that this is identical to:

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \hat{\mu}}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\hat{\mu} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** You may use either the *min-formula* or the *Cp(1−k)* formula to compute Cpk. If the two give slightly different answers (due to rounding), the *min-formula* is considered the primary KTU-marked answer.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reading μ and σ from the Probability Plot

The probability plot is fundamentally a *Quantile–Quantile (Q–Q) plot* of the sample quantiles against the *theoretical* normal quantiles. A straight-line fit:

$$x_{(i)} = a + b \, z_i$$

is fitted by **least-squares** through the points $(z_i, x_{(i)})$.

The least-squares estimators are:

$$b = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(z_i - \bar{z})(x_{(i)} - \bar{x})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(z_i - \bar{z})^2}, \quad a = \bar{x} - b\bar{z}$$

By the definition of the Q–Q plot:

- At $z_i = 0$ (i.e. the *50th percentile*), the fitted line gives $x = a$. Hence:
$$\hat{\mu} = a = \bar{x} - b\bar{z}$$
- The slope *b* converts one unit of standard-normal quantile into the original-data unit, hence:
$$\hat{\sigma} = b$$

Since the standard normal is *symmetric*, $\bar{z} \approx 0$ for a balanced set of plotting positions, simplifying the mean estimate to:

$$\hat{\mu} \approx \bar{x} \quad \text{and} \quad \hat{\sigma} \approx b$$

This is a *derivation of the key result*: **the probability plot's slope is the process standard deviation**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Full Solution)

**Problem statement:**
A shaft-diameter process is monitored. The engineering specifications are *LSL = 24.90 mm* and *USL = 25.10 mm*. A rational subgroup of *n* = 10 measurements (in mm) is taken from a stable process:

```
25.04, 25.06, 24.98, 25.01, 25.05, 25.03, 24.99, 25.02, 25.07, 25.00
```

Using a *Hazen normal probability plot*:

**(a)** Verify normality of the data,
**(b)** Estimate μ and σ from the fitted line,
**(c)** Compute *C_p* and *C_pk*,
**(d)** Comment on process capability.

---

### Step 1: Sort the data ascending

| i | $x_{(i)}$ (mm) |
|---|----------------|
| 1 | 24.98 |
| 2 | 24.99 |
| 3 | 25.00 |
| 4 | 25.01 |
| 5 | 25.02 |
| 6 | 25.03 |
| 7 | 25.04 |
| 8 | 25.05 |
| 9 | 25.06 |
| 10 | 25.07 |

### Step 2: Plotting positions (Hazen)

$$f_i = \frac{i - 0.5}{10}$$

| i | $f_i$ | $z_i = \Phi^{-1}(f_i)$ |
|---|-------|------------------------|
| 1 | 0.050 | −1.645 |
| 2 | 0.150 | −1.036 |
| 3 | 0.250 | −0.674 |
| 4 | 0.350 | −0.385 |
| 5 | 0.450 | −0.126 |
| 6 | 0.550 | 0.126 |
| 7 | 0.650 | 0.385 |
| 8 | 0.750 | 0.674 |
| 9 | 0.850 | 1.036 |
| 10 | 0.950 | 1.645 |

### Step 3: Compute means

$$\bar{x} = \frac{25.04+25.06+24.98+25.01+25.05+25.03+24.99+25.02+25.07+25.00}{10} = 25.025 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{z} = \frac{0}{10} = 0.000 \text{ (by symmetry of } f_i \text{ around 0.5)}$$

### Step 4: Compute slope b (σ̂)

The denominator:

$$\sum (z_i - \bar{z})^2 = 2(1.645^2 + 1.036^2 + 0.674^2 + 0.385^2 + 0.126^2)$$

Computing each term:

$$1.645^2 = 2.706, \quad 1.036^2 = 1.073, \quad 0.674^2 = 0.454, \quad 0.385^2 = 0.148, \quad 0.126^2 = 0.016$$

Sum of one half = $2.706 + 1.073 + 0.454 + 0.148 + 0.016 = 4.397$

Total = $2 \times 4.397 = 8.794$

The numerator (using $\bar{z} = 0$, this simplifies to $\sum z_i x_{(i)}$):

$$\sum z_i x_{(i)} = (-1.645)(24.98) + (-1.036)(24.99) + (-0.674)(25.00) + (-0.385)(25.01)$$
$$+ (-0.126)(25.02) + (0.126)(25.03) + (0.385)(25.04) + (0.674)(25.05)$$
$$+ (1.036)(25.06) + (1.645)(25.07)$$

Computing term by term (each in mm units):

$$= -41.092 - 26.130 - 16.850 - 9.629 - 3.153 + 3.158 + 9.640 + 16.884 + 25.962 + 41.240$$

Grouping negatives and positives:

$$\text{Negatives} = -41.092 - 26.130 - 16.850 - 9.629 - 3.153 = -96.854$$
$$\text{Positives} = 3.158 + 9.640 + 16.884 + 25.962 + 41.240 = 96.884$$

Total $= -96.854 + 96.884 = 0.030$

Therefore:

$$b = \hat{\sigma} = \frac{0.030}{8.794} = 0.00341 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5: Compute intercept (μ̂)

$$\hat{\mu} = \bar{x} - b\bar{z} = 25.025 - 0.00341 \times 0 = 25.025 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 6: Compute Cp

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.00341} = \frac{0.20}{0.02046} = 9.78$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A $C_p$ of **9.78** is *extraordinarily* capable — far above the industry benchmark of 1.33. This suggests the process variability is *very small* compared to the tolerance, OR the data sample is too small / clean (real production data usually shows $C_p$ between 1.0 and 2.0).

### Step 7: Compute Cpk

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\hat{\mu} - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.025 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.00341} = \frac{0.125}{0.01023} = 12.22$$

$$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \hat{\mu}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.10 - 25.025}{3 \times 0.00341} = \frac{0.075}{0.01023} = 7.33$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(12.22,\ 7.33) = 7.33$$

### Step 8: Normality Assessment

Plotting the points $(z_i, x_{(i)})$: the spread of $z_i$ ranges from −1.645 to +1.645, and the corresponding $x_{(i)}$ range is 24.98 to 25.07. The points fall along a tight straight line — normality is *visually confirmed*.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Method Hint:** If you only have 10 points, you can also perform a **Shapiro–Wilk test** (W statistic) for formal normality assessment, but for KTU Module 3, the *visual* probability-plot interpretation carries full marks.

### Step 9: Final Comment

The process is *highly capable* ($C_p = 9.78$, $C_{pk} = 7.33$) and well within specification. The mean (25.025 mm) is slightly above the midpoint (25.00 mm) by 0.025 mm, but the small process variability makes this irrelevant. A 14-mark KTU answer would also state the **off-centring factor**:

$$k = \frac{2 \vert 25.025 - 25.00 \vert}{0.20} = \frac{0.050}{0.20} = 0.25$$

This is well below the threshold of 0.25 typically used to flag centring issues — in this case, it just touches 0.25, so centring is *acceptable*.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table

| Probability-Plot Pattern | Diagnosis | Recommended Action |
|--------------------------|-----------|--------------------|
| Straight line | Normal | Proceed with C_p, C_pk |
| Convex upward (above line on right) | Right-skewed | Transform (e.g. log) or use non-normal capability |
| Concave (below line on right) | Left-skewed | Transform data |
| S-shape | Heavy tails | Check for outliers / mixture |
| Step or kink | Mixture of two populations | Stratify; compute capability within each subgroup |
| Single far point | Outlier | Investigate cause; consider removal with documented reason |
| Curved entirely | Strongly non-normal | Use *non-normal* capability (e.g. percentile-based) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Probability-Plot-Based Capability Analysis

The following Mermaid diagram captures the full procedure: from raw measurement data, through probability-plot construction and normality check, to capability index calculation and final decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect n observations from a stable in-control process] --> B[Sort data in ascending order x_1 to x_n]
    B --> C[Compute plotting position f_i = i - 0.5 divided by n]
    C --> D[Look up Normal Equivalent Deviate z_i from standard normal table]
    D --> E[Plot x_i on Y-axis versus z_i on X-axis]
    E --> F[Fit a least-squares straight line through the points]
    F --> G{Does the line pattern look straight with no major curvature?}
    G -- No --> H[Flag NON-NORMAL data: stop classical capability]
    H --> H1[Apply data transformation or use non-normal capability method]
    H1 --> Z[End: report non-normal capability result]
    G -- Yes --> I[Estimate process mean mu-hat = intercept of line at z equals 0]
    I --> J[Estimate process standard deviation sigma-hat = slope of fitted line]
    J --> K[Compute Cp using USL minus LSL divided by 6 sigma-hat]
    K --> L[Compute Cpl and Cpu one-sided indices]
    L --> M[Compute Cpk as the minimum of Cpl and Cpu]
    M --> N[Compute off-centring factor k]
    N --> O{Is Cpk greater than or equal to 1.33 AND k less than 0.25?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Process is HIGHLY CAPABLE: report as is]
    O -- No --> Q[Process needs improvement: re-centre or reduce sigma]
    P --> R[End: final capability report to customer]
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Anatomy of a Normal Probability Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Data x_1 to x_n] --> B[Step 1: Order ascending]
    B --> C[Step 2: Assign plotting position f_i]
    C --> D[Step 3: Convert to z_i by inverse normal CDF]
    D --> E[Plot points on graph paper]
    E --> F[Step 4: Draw best fit straight line]
    F --> G[Step 5: Read intercept equals mu-hat]
    F --> H[Step 6: Read slope equals sigma-hat]
    G --> I[Use mu-hat and sigma-hat in capability formulas]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Cp and Cpk value]
```

## 4.3 Capability Decision Tree (Sub-Process View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cpk computed] --> B{Is Cpk greater than 1.67?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Process is Six-Sigma capable: 0.6 DPMO or less]
    B -- No --> D{Is Cpk between 1.33 and 1.67?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Process is highly capable: industry standard for critical dimensions]
    D -- No --> F{Is Cpk between 1.00 and 1.33?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Process is marginally capable: monitor closely]
    F -- No --> H{Is Cpk between 0.67 and 1.00?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Process is poor: improvement project required]
    H -- No --> J[Cpk less than 0.67: process is not capable: immediate corrective action]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** In the diagrams above, every node label uses only plain uppercase alphanumeric text. The arrowed connectors represent the *direction of analytical flow*, not physical movement of parts. This is the *block-level functional architecture* style recommended for KTU Module 3 descriptive answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`

**Q:** *Define Process Capability Index C_pk. Why is a normal probability plot drawn before calculating C_pk in industrial practice?*

**Model Answer:**

C_pk (Process Capability Index, centring-included) is a numerical measure of a process's ability to produce output within specification limits, accounting for both the process spread and the location of the process mean. Its formula is:

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \hat{\mu}}{3\hat{\sigma}},\ \frac{\hat{\mu} - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}} \right)$$

The normal probability plot is drawn *before* calculating C_pk to **verify the underlying assumption of normality** of the process data. The ±3σ spread used in the C_pk formula is meaningful only if the data is approximately Gaussian; if the distribution is skewed or heavy-tailed, the ±3σ band does *not* contain 99.73% of the data, and the calculated C_pk would be misleading. The probability plot also yields visual estimates of μ̂ and σ̂ (intercept and slope of the fitted line), which are then used in the formula.

**(3 marks = 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark reason for probability plot)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** *Explain how the slope of the fitted line on a normal probability plot is used to estimate the process standard deviation. State the Hazen plotting position formula.*

**Model Answer:**

On a normal probability plot, the ordered sample values $x_{(i)}$ are plotted on the Y-axis against the Normal Equivalent Deviates $z_i = \Phi^{-1}(f_i)$ on the X-axis, where $f_i = (i-0.5)/n$ (the **Hazen plotting position**). A least-squares straight line $x = a + b z$ is fitted through the points. Since the X-axis is in units of standard-normal standard deviations, the **slope *b* of this line converts one unit of standard-normal deviation into the original-data unit, and is therefore an estimate of the process standard deviation**:

$$\hat{\sigma} = b = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(z_i - \bar{z})(x_{(i)} - \bar{x})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(z_i - \bar{z})^2}$$

The **Hazen plotting position** is:

$$f_i = \frac{i - 0.5}{n}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

This avoids 0 and 1 in the probability axis, which would otherwise correspond to ±∞ in the z-axis.

**(3 marks = 1 mark slope interpretation + 1 mark formula + 1 mark Hazen formula)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** *(a)* Explain, with the help of a sketch, the construction and interpretation of a *Normal Probability Plot* for verifying the normality of process data. Discuss the patterns observed for **(i) normal, (ii) right-skewed, and (iii) mixture** data. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A CNC turning process produces shafts with specification limits LSL = 19.95 mm and USL = 20.05 mm. Twenty-five sample observations (in mm) are collected from a stable process and arranged in ascending order as:

```
20.001, 20.004, 20.005, 20.006, 20.007, 20.008, 20.009, 20.010, 20.011,
20.012, 20.013, 20.014, 20.015, 20.016, 20.017, 20.018, 20.019, 20.020,
20.021, 20.022, 20.023, 20.025, 20.028, 20.031, 20.035
```

Using the *Hazen plotting position* and the straight-line fit, the estimated process mean is $\hat{\mu} = 20.0150$ mm and the estimated process standard deviation is $\hat{\sigma} = 0.0080$ mm. Calculate:
- (i) C_p, (ii) C_pk, (iii) the off-centring factor *k*, and
- (iv) the expected defects per million opportunities (DPMO) using the C_pk value.

Comment on the process capability. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Construction and Interpretation (7 marks)

**Construction Steps (3 marks):**

1. Arrange the *n* observations in ascending order: $x_{(1)} \le x_{(2)} \le \ldots \le x_{(n)}$.
2. Compute the Hazen plotting position for each: $f_i = (i - 0.5)/n$.
3. Convert each $f_i$ to its Normal Equivalent Deviate (NED) $z_i = \Phi^{-1}(f_i)$ from standard normal tables.
4. Plot the points $(z_i,\ x_{(i)})$ on graph paper with *z* on the X-axis and *x* on the Y-axis.
5. Fit a straight line by eye (or by least-squares) through the points. A *non-linear scale* on the X-axis (where the standard normal CDF is linear) is often used; this is called a *normal probability paper*.

**Sketch (2 marks):** *(Students must draw the coordinate axes with z on the X-axis, x on the Y-axis, a sample linear fit, and indicate the intercept and slope)*.

The intercept of the line at $z = 0$ gives the process mean $\hat{\mu}$.
The slope of the line gives the process standard deviation $\hat{\sigma}$.

**Interpretation Patterns (2 marks):**

- **(i) Normal data:** Points lie on a straight line throughout its length.
- **(ii) Right-skewed data:** Points bend *upward* in the upper tail — convex shape — the right tail is longer than the left.
- **(iii) Mixture data:** A *kink* or *step* appears in the line, indicating two overlapping populations (e.g. two machines producing different sub-populations).

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

Given: LSL = 19.95 mm, USL = 20.05 mm, $\hat{\mu} = 20.0150$ mm, $\hat{\sigma} = 0.0080$ mm.

**(i) Process Capability Index C_p (2 marks):**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{20.05 - 19.95}{6 \times 0.0080} = \frac{0.10}{0.048} = 2.083$$

**(ii) C_pk (2 marks):**

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\hat{\mu} - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{20.0150 - 19.95}{3 \times 0.0080} = \frac{0.0650}{0.024} = 2.708$$

$$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \hat{\mu}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{20.05 - 20.0150}{0.024} = \frac{0.0350}{0.024} = 1.458$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(2.708,\ 1.458) = 1.458$$

**(iii) Off-centring factor k (1.5 marks):**

Midpoint $M = (\text{USL} + \text{LSL})/2 = (20.05 + 19.95)/2 = 20.000$ mm.

$$k = \frac{2 \vert \hat{\mu} - M \vert}{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}} = \frac{2 \vert 20.0150 - 20.000 \vert}{0.10} = \frac{0.030}{0.10} = 0.30$$

**(iv) Expected DPMO (1.5 marks):**

The one-sided tail probability at $z = 3 C_{pk} = 3 \times 1.458 = 4.375$:

$$\Phi(-4.375) \approx 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ (from standard normal tables)}$$

Total defective fraction $\approx 2 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-6} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$

$$\text{DPMO} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^6 = 12 \text{ defects per million}$$

**Comment on Capability (1 mark):**

With $C_p = 2.083 > 1.33$ and $C_{pk} = 1.458 > 1.33$, the process is **highly capable** and *exceeds* industry benchmarks. However, the off-centring factor $k = 0.30$ is *above* the 0.25 threshold — the process mean is shifted 0.015 mm above the midpoint, towards the USL. While still capable, **re-centring the mean to 20.000 mm** would raise $C_{pk}$ to the theoretical maximum $C_p(1-k) = 2.083 \times 0.70 = 1.458$ (here it equals C_pk because USL is the binding limit), reducing variation and providing safety margin.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** *(a)* Differentiate between *C_p* and *C_pk*. Under what condition does $C_p = C_{pk}$? What is the role of a *normal probability plot* in identifying this condition? **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Twenty observations of a process output (in mm) are:

```
49.95, 50.02, 50.08, 49.99, 50.10, 50.05, 49.97, 50.01, 50.06, 50.03,
49.98, 50.04, 50.09, 49.96, 50.07, 50.00, 50.11, 50.02, 50.05, 50.06
```

The specification limits are LSL = 49.90 mm and USL = 50.10 mm. Construct the *Hazen normal probability plot* data (table of $x_{(i)}$, $f_i$, $z_i$), determine the least-squares estimates $\hat{\mu}$ and $\hat{\sigma}$ from the fitted line, and compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and the *sigma level*. State whether the process is acceptable for an automotive IATF 16949 critical dimension. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Differentiation (7 marks)

**C_p vs C_pk (3 marks):**

| Aspect | C_p | C_pk |
|--------|-----|------|
| Considers centring? | No (assumes μ = M) | Yes |
| Formula | $\frac{\text{USL}-\text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}}$ | $\min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL}-\hat{\mu}}{3\hat{\sigma}},\ \frac{\hat{\mu}-\text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}}\right)$ |
| Always ≥ C_pk? | Yes, C_p ≥ C_pk | Smaller of the two one-sided indices |
| Use case | Potential capability | Actual capability (includes shift) |

**Condition for $C_p = C_{pk}$ (2 marks):**

$C_p = C_{pk}$ when the process mean equals the midpoint of the specification band, i.e. $\hat{\mu} = M = (\text{USL}+\text{LSL})/2$, which implies the off-centring factor $k = 0$. In this state the process is said to be *perfectly centred* and the *full tolerance* is being used symmetrically.

**Role of Normal Probability Plot (2 marks):**

The normal probability plot is used *before* and *during* capability analysis to:
1. **Verify normality** — necessary for C_p / C_pk to be meaningful.
2. **Estimate μ̂ and σ̂** (intercept and slope of the fitted line) — required inputs to the C_pk formula.
3. **Detect off-centring visually** — if the fitted line is *shifted horizontally* on the plot (intercept deviates from the midpoint of the data), this signals a mean-shift problem, which would imply $C_p > C_{pk}$.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Sort data ascending (1 mark):**

```
49.95, 49.96, 49.97, 49.98, 49.99, 50.00, 50.01, 50.02, 50.02, 50.03,
50.04, 50.05, 50.05, 50.06, 50.06, 50.07, 50.08, 50.09, 50.10, 50.11
```

**Step 2: Compute plotting position $f_i = (i-0.5)/20$ and look up $z_i$ (1.5 marks):**

| i | $x_{(i)}$ | $f_i$ | $z_i$ |
|---|-----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | 49.95 | 0.025 | −1.960 |
| 2 | 49.96 | 0.075 | −1.440 |
| 3 | 49.97 | 0.125 | −1.150 |
| 4 | 49.98 | 0.175 | −0.935 |
| 5 | 49.99 | 0.225 | −0.755 |
| 6 | 50.00 | 0.275 | −0.598 |
| 7 | 50.01 | 0.325 | −0.454 |
| 8 | 50.02 | 0.375 | −0.319 |
| 9 | 50.02 | 0.425 | −0.189 |
| 10 | 50.03 | 0.475 | −0.062 |
| 11 | 50.04 | 0.525 | 0.062 |
| 12 | 50.05 | 0.575 | 0.189 |
| 13 | 50.05 | 0.625 | 0.319 |
| 14 | 50.06 | 0.675 | 0.454 |
| 15 | 50.06 | 0.725 | 0.598 |
| 16 | 50.07 | 0.775 | 0.755 |
| 17 | 50.08 | 0.825 | 0.935 |
| 18 | 50.09 | 0.875 | 1.150 |
| 19 | 50.10 | 0.925 | 1.440 |
| 20 | 50.11 | 0.975 | 1.960 |

**Step 3: Compute mean and slope (1.5 marks):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{20}\sum x_{(i)} = \frac{1000.31}{20} = 50.0155 \text{ mm}$$

By symmetry of plotting positions, $\bar{z} \approx 0$. Using the calculation method (or a calculator):

$$\sum z_i x_{(i)} = 0.1844 \text{ mm (after careful summation)}$$

$$\sum z_i^2 = 19.997 \approx 20.00$$

$$\hat{\sigma} = b = \frac{0.1844}{19.997} \approx 0.00922 \text{ mm}$$

$$\hat{\mu} = a = \bar{x} - b \bar{z} \approx 50.016 \text{ mm}$$

(Students may obtain slightly different numerical values depending on the rounding of $z_i$. Award full marks if the methodology is correct and the final values are within ±5%.)

**Step 4: Compute C_p and C_pk (2 marks):**

$$C_p = \frac{50.10 - 49.90}{6 \times 0.00922} = \frac{0.20}{0.0553} = 3.615$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{50.016 - 49.90}{3 \times 0.00922} = \frac{0.116}{0.02766} = 4.193$$

$$C_{pu} = \frac{50.10 - 50.016}{0.02766} = \frac{0.084}{0.02766} = 3.037$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(4.193,\ 3.037) = 3.037$$

**Step 5: Compute Sigma Level (1 mark):**

$$\Sigma = 3 C_{pk} = 3 \times 3.037 = 9.11 \, \sigma$$

> [!NOTE]
> A sigma level of 9+ corresponds to *World-Class* performance, indicating virtually zero defects.

**Final Comment on Capability (1 mark):**

With $C_p = 3.615$, $C_{pk} = 3.037$, and a sigma level of 9.11σ, the process is **excellent** — far exceeding the IATF 16949 critical-dimension requirement of $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$. The off-centring is minor (the process mean is 0.016 mm above the midpoint 50.00 mm), and the slight skewness (one observation at 50.10, one at 50.11) does not cause normality to fail. The process is **acceptable without modification**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse the Hazen plotting position $f_i = (i-0.5)/n$ with the Weibull $i/(n+1)$ or Blom $(i-0.375)/(n+0.25)$ — each gives *different* z_i, and the resulting $\hat{\sigma}$ will change by 5–10%. Use the same formula consistently. **Marks lost: 1–2 per error.**
> 2. **Do not** use $s$ (sample standard deviation) in the C_p formula when the question asks for the *probability-plot method*. The probability-plot method specifically uses the *slope* of the fitted line as σ̂. **Marks lost: 1–2.**
> 3. **Always** include units in the final C_p / C_pk answer (it is a dimensionless ratio, but stating "C_p is dimensionless" or "C_p = 1.33 (no units)" earns credit).
> 4. **Always** show the *min* step in the C_pk calculation explicitly — students often write only one of $C_{pl}$ or $C_{pu}$ and skip the *min*. **Marks lost: 0.5–1.**
> 5. **For 14-mark questions:** students frequently forget to *comment* on the process capability at the end. This is a compulsory conclusion step and is worth 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — print this out before your KTU exam.**

- **Probability plot = graphical normality test** (NOT a direct capability calculator; it is the *enabler* of capability calculation).
- **Hazen plotting position:** $f_i = (i - 0.5)/n$.
- **Normal score (NED):** $z_i = \Phi^{-1}(f_i)$, read from standard normal tables.
- **Slope of fitted line = σ̂**, **Intercept at $z = 0$ = μ̂**.
- **C_p = (USL − LSL) / (6σ̂)** — measures *spread only*.
- **C_pk = min((USL−μ̂)/(3σ̂), (μ̂−LSL)/(3σ̂))** — measures *spread + centring*.
- **Off-centring factor:** $k = 2|\hat{\mu} - M|/T$, where $M$ = midpoint, $T$ = tolerance.
- **C_pk = C_p(1 − k)** (algebraically identical to the min-formula).
- **Interpretation guide:** $C_{pk} < 1$ (not capable), $1 \le C_{pk} < 1.33$ (marginal), $1.33 \le C_{pk} < 1.67$ (capable), $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$ (excellent / Six-Sigma capable).
- **Sigma level = 3 × C_pk** (used in Six-Sigma literature).
- **DPMO ≈ 2 × Φ(−3 C_pk) × 10⁶**.
- **Always verify stability first** (control chart), then normality (probability plot), *then* compute capability.
- **Probability plot patterns to recognise:** linear (normal), S-shape (heavy tails), convex (right skew), concave (left skew), kink/step (mixture), single outlier.
- **Probability plot ≠ Q–Q plot in axis labelling** (probability plot has *f* on one axis; Q–Q has *z* on both axes; mathematically equivalent).
- **Use the probability plot slope as σ̂** for short-term capability; use overall *s* for long-term P_p / P_pk.
- **P_p ≥ C_p** in general (long-term variation ≥ short-term variation by 1.5× in Six-Sigma shifting rule of thumb).
- **For KTU 14-mark answers:** always include (a) data table, (b) μ̂ and σ̂, (c) C_p, (d) C_pk via *min*-formula, (e) *k* value, (f) comment on capability.
- **Never use the term "Bell curve" formally** in KTU answers; use *"normal distribution"* or *"Gaussian distribution"*.
- **Probability plot paper** is *not* linear-z on X-axis; the X-axis is non-linear (CDF-scaled) so that a normal distribution plots as a straight line *without* converting to $z_i$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability Ratios
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET611 - INDUSTRIAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Capability Ratios

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Capability Ratios (PCR)** are dimensionless numerical indices that quantify the ability of a **stable** (in statistical control) manufacturing or service process to produce output within the pre-defined engineering specification limits prescribed by the customer (USL & LSL) or design target (T).

The fundamental role of a Process Capability Ratio is to bridge the gap between two distinct domains of variation:

- **Process Variation** — represented by the standard deviation **σ** (or long-term **s**) of the process output.
- **Specification Tolerance** — represented by the engineering **USL (Upper Specification Limit)** and **LSL (Lower Specification Limit)**.

The American Society for Quality (ASQ) and AIAG (Automotive Industry Action Group) classify capability indices into two major families:

1. **Process Potential Indices** — compare process spread to specification spread.
2. **Process Performance Indices** — measure actual performance relative to specifications, including location.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A high capability ratio means the process "voice" aligns with the customer "voice." A low ratio means the process is producing defective output even when in statistical control.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a car** on a two-lane highway. The **road width** represents the engineering tolerance (USL − LSL), and the **wobble of your car** (how much it sways left and right while you drive straight) represents the process standard deviation **σ**.

- If your car's wobble is **small** compared to the road width, you are a **safe, capable driver** → High **Cp**.
- If your car wobbles a lot, you will frequently hit the road edges (defects) → Low **Cp**.

Now, even if the wobble is small, if you are **driving on the right edge of the road** instead of the center, you are at risk of going off the road on one side. This is the **centering problem**, which is captured by **Cpk**. A driver who wobbles equally on both sides is "centered" — meaning **Cp = Cpk**.

## 1.3 The Four Standard Ratios — Overview

| Symbol | Name | What it Captures |
|:------:|:-----|:-----------------|
| **Cp** | Process Capability Index | Spread only (potential) |
| **Cpk** | Process Capability Index (centered) | Spread + Centering |
| **Cpm** | Process Capability Index (Taguchi) | Spread + Centering + Target |
| **Pp / Ppk** | Process Performance Indices | Long-term performance (using **s** instead of σ̄) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - Cp ≥ **1.33** → Capable
> - Cp ≥ **1.67** → Highly Capable (Six-Sigma territory)
> - Cp < 1.00 → Process is NOT capable (defects inevitable even if centered)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions Underlying Capability Ratios

Before applying any ratio, the process **must** satisfy two prerequisites:

- **Stability:** The process must be in **statistical control** (no special causes on the control chart).
- **Normality:** The quality characteristic must be **normally distributed** (or transformable to normal via Box-Cox).
- **Independence:** Observations must be statistically independent.

> [!WARNING]
> If the process is NOT in control, capability ratios are **mathematically valid but practically misleading**. Always confirm with a control chart (X̄-R, I-MR) first.

## 2.2 The Core Family of Indices

### 2.2.1 Cp — Process Capability Index (Potential)

**Logic Steps:**
- Process natural tolerance = **6σ** (covers 99.73% of variation under normality).
- Engineering tolerance = **USL − LSL**.
- Cp = ratio of specification width to process width.

**Interpretation:**
- Cp = 1.00 → Process exactly fits the specification (zero margin).
- Cp = 1.33 → Process uses 75% of the spec width; 25% margin.
- Cp = 1.67 → Process uses 60% of the spec width; 40% margin.
- Cp = 2.00 → Process uses 50% of the spec width; 50% margin.

### 2.2.2 Cpk — Process Capability Index (Adjusted for Centering)

**Logic Steps:**
- One-sided capability: **Cpu = (USL − μ) / 3σ**
- One-sided capability: **Cpl = (μ − LSL) / 3σ**
- Cpk = **minimum** of the two one-sided values.
- This penalizes the process for being off-center.

**Interpretation:**
- Cp = Cpk → Process is **perfectly centered** on the midpoint of specifications.
- Cp > Cpk → Process is **off-center** (shifted toward one specification limit).
- Cpk < 0 → Process mean is **outside** specification limits (catastrophic).

### 2.2.3 Cpm — Taguchi Capability Index (Target-Oriented)

**Logic Steps:**
- Includes the **deviation from target** as an additional source of variation.
- Adds the term **(μ − T)²** (squared deviation of mean from target T) under the square root.
- Reflects **Taguchi's Quality Loss Philosophy** — deviation from target always incurs loss.

### 2.2.4 Cr — Capability Ratio

- Cr = 1 / Cp
- A Cr ≤ **0.75** is desirable (equivalent to Cp ≥ 1.33).

### 2.2.5 Pp and Ppk — Process Performance Indices

- Use the **overall standard deviation (s_overall)** computed from all individuals, not within-subgroup variation (σ_within = R̄/d₂).
- Pp reflects **long-term** performance; Cp reflects **short-term** potential.
- Pp and Ppk are typically lower than Cp and Cpk because they include between-subgroup variation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{\overline{R}}{d_2}$ | Within-subgroup standard deviation (from X̄-R chart) | same as variable |
| 2 | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Overall standard deviation (for Pp, Ppk) | same as variable |
| 3 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability Index (potential) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided upper capability | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided lower capability | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu},\; C_{pl})$ | Process Capability Index (centered) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_{pm} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\bar{x} - T)^2}}$ | Taguchi Capability Index | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_r = \dfrac{1}{C_p}$ | Capability Ratio (≤ 0.75 desired) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | Process Performance (long-term) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3s},\; \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3s}\right)$ | Process Performance (centered, long-term) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{Defect Rate (ppm)} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times \left[1 - \Phi(3 C_{pk})\right] \times 2$ | Approx. PPM defects (assuming normality) | ppm |

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of **2** in Equation 11 assumes both tails (above USL and below LSL) contribute defects. For one-sided specs, drop the factor of 2.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Application

| Industry | Application of PCR |
|:---------|:-------------------|
| Automotive (AIAG) | Mandates **Cpk ≥ 1.33** for critical dimensions per PPAP |
| Semiconductor | Drives Six-Sigma programs targeting **Cpk ≥ 2.00** |
| Pharmaceutical | Validates manufacturing consistency for FDA submissions |
| Aerospace | Cpk-based process qualification (AS9100) |
| Service Sector | Used in call-centre AHT, banking transaction times |

## 2.5 Sigma Level Relationship

The "Sigma Level" of a process is defined as the **process Sigma (Z)** at its worst side:

$$Z_{\text{short-term}} = 3 \cdot C_p \quad \text{and} \quad Z_{\text{shifted}} = 3 \cdot C_{pk}$$

A "3-Sigma" process corresponds to Cp = 1.00; a "6-Sigma" process corresponds to Cp = 2.00 (with 1.5σ shift, the operating Cpk is 1.50).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Cp from First Principles

Consider a normally distributed quality characteristic **X ~ N(μ, σ²)**. By the **Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7)**:

$$P(\mu - 3\sigma \le X \le \mu + 3\sigma) = 0.9973$$

The process "natural tolerance width" is therefore:

$$\text{Process Width} = (\mu + 3\sigma) - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma$$

The "specification width" provided by the customer is:

$$\text{Specification Width} = USL - LSL$$

A **dimensionless measure of fit** is formed by taking the ratio:

$$C_p = \frac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Width}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

**Logical Interpretation:** If Cp = 1, the process spread EXACTLY equals the specification spread — no room for shifts. If Cp > 1, the process has a safety margin.

## 3.2 Derivation of Cpk — Penalty for Off-Center

Suppose the mean μ is **not** at the midpoint M = (USL + LSL)/2. Define the **upper distance** from mean to upper spec:

$$d_u = USL - \mu$$

And the **lower distance** from mean to lower spec:

$$d_l = \mu - LSL$$

The "effective" one-sided capability indices are:

$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{d_u}{3\sigma}, \qquad C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{d_l}{3\sigma}$$

Each of these represents the number of **3σ** half-windows that fit between the mean and the corresponding specification limit. The **worse (smaller)** of the two governs the risk of defects:

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu},\; C_{pl}) = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

**Algebraic Compact Form (Pearn–Lin–Chen Identity):**

$$C_{pk} = C_p(1 - k), \quad \text{where} \quad k = \frac{2\mu - (USL + LSL)}{(USL - LSL)} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 = \frac{\vert \mu - M \vert}{(USL - LSL)/2}$$

The factor **(1 − k)** is the **centering correction**, where k ∈ [0, 1] is the relative shift of the mean from the midpoint M.

## 3.3 Derivation of Cpm — Taguchi's Loss-Inclusive Index

Taguchi argued that any deviation from the **target T** incurs a quadratic quality loss. Total variation should therefore include both **process variance** and **mean-deviation-from-target variance**:

$$\text{Total Variation} = \sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2$$

The total "spread" measure becomes:

$$\text{Effective Width} = 6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}$$

Therefore:

$$C_{pm} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$$

**Special Case Check:** If μ = T (process mean ON target) AND spec midpoint M = T (target at center), then (μ − T)² = 0, and Cpm = Cp = Cpk.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A shaft diameter has specifications **LSL = 10.00 mm** and **USL = 10.50 mm**. The process mean is **μ = 10.20 mm** and the within-subgroup standard deviation is **σ = 0.05 mm**. Target value is **T = 10.25 mm**.
>
> Compute Cp, Cpk, Cpm, Cr, and the corresponding PPM defect rate.

**Step 1 — Compute Cp:**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{10.50 - 10.00}{6 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.50}{0.30} = 1.667$$

The process is **highly capable** in terms of spread.

**Step 2 — Compute Cpk:**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{10.50 - 10.20}{3 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.30}{0.15} = 2.000$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{10.20 - 10.00}{3 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.20}{0.15} = 1.333$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(2.000,\; 1.333) = 1.333$$

Process is still capable, but **penalized for being closer to the LSL**.

**Step 3 — Compute Cpm (Target = 10.25 mm):**

$$\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2 = (0.05)^2 + (10.20 - 10.25)^2 = 0.0025 + 0.0025 = 0.0050$$

$$\sqrt{0.0050} = 0.07071$$

$$C_{pm} = \frac{0.50}{6 \times 0.07071} = \frac{0.50}{0.4243} = 1.179$$

Cpm is **lower** than Cpk because the process is **off-target** by 0.05 mm.

**Step 4 — Compute Cr:**

$$C_r = \frac{1}{C_p} = \frac{1}{1.667} = 0.600$$

Since Cr = 0.60 < 0.75, the process is **acceptable** by the Cr rule.

**Step 5 — Estimate PPM Defects:**

Using the normal distribution, the distance from the process mean to the nearest spec limit is **3 Cpk σ** units. The area beyond this is:

$$Z_{\min} = 3 \times C_{pk} = 3 \times 1.333 = 4.00 \text{ sigma}$$

Total defect rate (both tails) ≈ 2 × Φ(−4.00) × 10⁶ ≈ 2 × 31.67 ≈ **63 ppm**.

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix of the Four Ratios

| Scenario | Cp | Cpk | Cpm | Interpretation |
|:---------|:--:|:---:|:---:|:---------------|
| Centered & on-target, wide process | 1.67 | 1.67 | 1.67 | Ideal Six-Sigma process |
| Centered but off-target | 1.67 | 1.67 | 1.10 | Penalized by Taguchi loss |
| Off-center, on-target | 1.67 | 1.20 | 1.10 | Both penalty factors apply |
| Off-center AND off-target | 1.67 | 1.20 | 0.95 | Worst case — full penalty |

## 3.6 Quick-Reference Decision Tree

```
            ┌─ Compute Cp  → Spread only OK?
            │              └─ If Cp < 1.33 → REDUCE VARIATION
            │
Process ────┼─ Compute Cpk → Centering OK?
            │              └─ If Cpk < Cp → RECENTER
            │
            └─ Compute Cpm → Target OK?
                           └─ If Cpm < Cpk → MOVE TO TARGET
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```
       LSL          M                USL
        |           |                 |
        |     [ PROCESS SPREAD ]     |
        |           |                 |
        v           v                 v
   -----|-----------|-----------------|----->  X
        |           |                 |
        |   .   .   |    .   .        |
        | .   .   . |.   .   . .      |

        |           ||                |

        |___________||________________|
                  
        |<--d_l-->||<-----d_u----->|

   Cp = (USL - LSL) / (6σ)
   Cpl = d_l / (3σ)   ← Lower one-sided
```
